        *   ONC's definition and adoption of criteria must enable HIT developers to constitute a module as they see fit or certify to part of overall ePA criteria, allowing coupling with HIT from other developers.
    *   Should ONC adopt a certification criterion for prior authorization that accounts for the full, HIPAA compliant workflow for prior authorization transactions including translation from FHIR to the X12 standard? Or should ONC adopt certification criteria that include only the workflows up to the point of translation? What ongoing challenges will stakeholders face if there is a need to translate between HIPAA-adopted standards and other standards that have only been adopted under the Certification Program used to support prior authorization transactions? How should HHS address alignment between standards adopted for HIPAA transactions and standards adopted under the Certification Program?
        *   ONC should not adopt a singular ePA criterion encompassing all capabilities and interoperability requirements.
        *   ONC should develop criteria allowing clinical HIT to be certified as a module that could be coupled with financial/administrative HIT for end-to-end ePA workflow.
        *   Market reality is that many provider organizations obtain clinical and revenue cycle/administrative HIT from different sources.
        *   ONC should allow HIT developers discretion to certify to some or all adopted ePA criteria.
        *   In defining criteria, ONC should separate:
            *   Capabilities necessary to capture data from interoperability requirements as distinct criteria.
            *   Processes accruing to clinical workflow (source data collection, ordering, referring, triggering ePA) from administrative activities (addressing denials, responding to additional documentation requests, ingesting approved authorizations).
        *   Interoperability requirements for Da Vinci Documentation Requirements Lookup Service (DRLS) should be distinct from any use of Prior Authorization Support (PAS) IG, including X12 translation.
    *   If ONC were to propose to include these functional capabilities as part of the Certification Program, how should a new certification criterion (or multiple certification criteria) be structured, including technical requirements, attributed standards, and implementation specifications? ONC’s experience adopting certification criteria suggests that, at times, combining related functions into a single Health IT Module is most appropriate, while in other cases, health IT functionalities are best represented by separate certification criteria, despite being functionally related. For instance, under a single criterion, different products and services in the market may be “tightly coupled” for the purposes of certification, even when they can be purchased and implemented separately. We seek the public’s input on which functional capabilities for prior authorization should be tested and certified together as part of one certification criterion, and which capabilities should be separated into different certification criteria.
        *   ONC should not create a predefined criteria set that must be prescriptively certified together.
        *   A more modular approach is needed, building on existing FHIR US Core and SMART App frameworks.
        *   Certification should be done to individual interactions, given that ePA process points may be distributed across HIT types beyond traditional EHRs.
        *   HIT developers should be able to make use of relied-on software or combinations of their own products for modular certification.
        *   It would be inappropriate to limit configurations presented for certification due to the need to contend with multiple payers, intermediaries, and SMART App developers.
        *   Da Vinci guides have not fully matured in operational use, particularly regarding capability distribution across HIT.
        *   A staged certification program is suggested.

*   Implementation Specifications for Prior Authorization
    *   What is the current readiness of the three FHIR-based Da Vinci IGs described above for adoption as part of certification criteria for health IT? Given limited testing of these specifications to date, what would be a feasible timeline for use of these IGs in production for prior authorization transactions? What, if any, additional changes are needed for these IGs prior to adoption as part of certification criteria for health IT?
        *   A progression path for certification should start with emphasizing provider HIT's ability to support workflow automation by minimally being able to:
            *   Initiate other HIT (e.g., SMART App) to begin workflow and receive progress notifications.
            *   Support FHIR US Core for systems maintaining supporting information, enabling other HIT (using SMART Launch) to query FHIR US Core based APIs for prior authorization information requirements.
            *   Receive a final disposition of the authorization request.
        *   On the payer side, certification should embrace making available standards-based APIs for ePA interoperability, allowing providers, SMART Apps, and intermediaries to connect.
        *   Do not recommend a hard timeline for provider-focused HIT certification; initial criteria should be optional or focus on functional capabilities and data sourcing to initiate PA.
        *   Having payer-side, certified APIs can guide standards adoption, similar to EHRs adopting FHIR-based APIs for clinical data access.
    *   If the existing IGs are not yet ready for adoption, should ONC still propose certification criteria? Should ONC consider proposing certification criteria incorporating the FHIR Release 4 base standard but delay adopting implementation specifications until a later date? What are the potential risks of this approach?
        *   Do not recommend starting with only FHIR Release 4 base standard.
        *   At a minimum, source systems for supporting prior authorization information should support FHIR US Core in accordance with existing criteria, with ability for higher specification levels via SVAP.
        *   Certification criteria should enable a variety of modular approaches, with minimum support for any HIT serving as a source system to initiate ePA requests.
        *   Criteria for Da Vinci IGs should support SMART Apps certifiable independently or coupled with source systems as an HIT module.
        *   Additional guidance for other interactions (e.g., consumer queries) can follow.
        *   Da Vinci IGs provide sufficient guidance to begin the process and enhance as learned, but emphasize more granular adoption of interactions within each IG to enable HIT to collectively support ePA workflow.
    *   If we were to adopt certification criteria referencing the base standard and then update those criteria to integrate implementation specifications in the future, how should these integrations be handled? When and how should the existing systems be replaced? All at once, or as a series of transitional steps?
        *   Recommend a phased approach for development, definition, and adoption of certification criteria that supports various HIT module configurations.
        *   ONC should phase in adoption of criteria requiring Da Vinci IGs.
        *   ONC could define distinct criteria from RFI functional capabilities to bundle those directly supporting clinical workflow (triggering PA) separately from downstream processes (ingesting responses, status queries, documentation fulfillment).
        *   ONC could define an order of adoption for Da Vinci IGs, starting with CRD and PAS IGs for clinical workflow integration and information provision, and later DTR IG.
        *   Criteria should recognize that HIT developers can present modules in different configurations, from specific capabilities paired with third-party apps to comprehensive functions.
        *   ONC should not prescribe HIT replacement but support market innovation to more capable levels.
    *   Do the Da Vinci IGs effectively support Federal and state legal requirements and/or health plan compliance requirements for clinical documentation, for example, signatures (or other indications of provider review and assent), record retention over long periods of time, and document security to ensure data integrity once stored?
        *   Cerner defers to the payer community for this question.
    *   What alternative approaches to designing certification criteria should ONC explore that are not based on the three Da Vinci IGs described herein?
        *   Separate functional capabilities from interoperability capabilities for ePA as distinct criteria at a more granular level than Da Vinci IG boundaries to allow flexibility.
        *   Start with the ability for:
            *   Initiating applications (EHR, scheduling, registration, practice management) to initiate other HIT (e.g., SMART App) to manage workflow.
            *   Source systems of supporting information (EHR, HIM, longitudinal health record systems) to use FHIR US Core profiles (already part of 2015 Edition Cures Act Updates) to enable other HIT (e.g., SMART apps) to gather relevant information and submit PA requests.
        *   This can be built upon via Da Vinci IGs over time.
    *   Are there simplified approaches to the workflows described in the Da Vinci IGs that ONC should consider as alternative approaches to support electronic prior authorization?
        *   Simplification is more about payer policies on what requires PA and what data is needed, rather than Da Vinci IG interactions.
        *   Examples: authorization for recurring services or a bundle of services for a condition/episode (one upfront authorization), persisting open authorizations if patient changes health plans or plan year, standardization of information required across payers for common services.
    *   Are there new IGs which need to be developed in order to integrate with other workflows relevant to prior authorization? In particular, what IGs may still need to be developed in order to integrate with HIPAA administrative transaction standards?
        *   No immediate need for new IGs per se. Instead, existing IGs should be enhanced to define clearer building blocks for HIT to support certification criteria.
        *   Guidance for each actor (source/server and consumer/client side) is needed for suggested criteria.
        *   Example iterations of interoperability-related criteria:
            *   CRD related criteria:
                *   Criterion A: Initiate process to determine PA necessity; Notify initiating system of necessity. (Focus on initiating app and intermediary HIT managing workflow, expand to consumer apps over time).
                *   Criterion B: Request necessity of authorization from appropriate payer. (Focus on HIT interacting with payers, i.e., full CRD IG).
            *   DTR related criteria:
                *   Criterion C: Request documentation requirements; Request additional supporting information. (Focus on provider HIT and payer interaction to obtain documentation requirements).
                *   Criterion D: Gather supporting information for PA request. (Focus on ability to obtain information from another source system).
                    *   Note: Da Vinci’s CDex IG is a potential plug-in, but Cerner has concerns with using CDex if signed documents are required for attachments. PAS guidance on including data sets is sufficient, eliminating need for CDex.
            *   PAS related criteria:
                *   Criterion E: Submit prior authorization request. (Focus on submission, addressing information sharing if gathered by different HIT).
                *   Criterion F: Monitor request status.
                *   Criterion G: Request additional supporting information. (Payer requesting more info, managed via DTR, resulting in updated PAS submission; guidance for this step is early).
                *   Criterion H: Communicate authorization request response. (Payer to provider, then to initiating and other interested systems like revenue cycle).

*   Healthcare Attachment Standards
    *   Would the specifications within the CDA Attachments IG, if adopted as part of a certification criterion, support more effective exchange of healthcare attachments for prior authorization? Would any changes to the IG be needed, or would additional functionalities or standards be required for effective implementation of the CDA Attachments IG in certified health IT?
        *   Recommend moving directly to a FHIR-based approach given current C-CDA attachments implementations and the opportunity to support submission of data sets (discrete data and/or existing documents).
        *   This reduces unnecessary transformation and administrative burden.
        *   Opportunity to fully align on FHIR-based syntax as in PAS IG, which allows for FHIR Bundle=collection or (if needed) FHIR Bundle=document.
        *   Such a construct might be necessary for FHIR to X12 translation but should be an artifact of that transformation, not the full FHIR-based flow.
    *   Would the use of FHIR Documents, if adopted as part of a certification criterion, support more effective exchange of healthcare attachments? Are there any gaps or constraints that would need to be further specified, such as through an IG, in order for FHIR Documents to be effective for this use case when implemented in certified health IT? Would the adoption of a certification criterion for FHIR Documents support other administrative use cases beyond prior authorization?
        *   Recommend moving away from a document-centric attachment approach to a data set approach, considering available data form and automation level, thus not needing human intervention for review and signature.
        *   This does not preclude documents in submission but does not require them.
        *   A fully FHIR-based approach (as in PAS IG) is appropriate as it accommodates any data type, including C-CDA, CCD, CCR, PDF documents where needed.
        *   Given C-CDA Attachment standard's adoption status, adopting it is less beneficial when FHIR Bundles are within reach and better position the industry for the future.
    *   Given limited testing of these approaches to date, what would be a feasible timeline for use of the CDA Attachments IG or FHIR Documents in production for prior authorization transactions?
        *   Supporting either CDA or FHIR Documents resource would require significant development and testing between providers and payers.
    *   Which of these approaches would better accommodate improvements over time to meet payer and provider needs? Should ONC consider adopting certification criteria referencing one approach over the other, or should ONC consider supporting both approaches within certified health IT?
        *   Given C-CDA Attachment standard's adoption state, recommend going directly to a FHIR-based approach for healthcare attachments certification.
    *   If the IGs developed by the Da Vinci Project, or an alternate set of IGs addressing the full scope of prior authorization workflows, are not yet ready for adoption in certified health IT, should ONC propose certification criteria to support healthcare attachments transactions for prior authorization alone?
        *   No, do not recommend this. There is little value in addressing only a healthcare attachment standard without addressing the real points of burden in prior authorization (difficulties in determining coverage and documentation requirements).
        *   Fulfilling documentation requirements once known does not significantly ease burden as existing non-standard electronic documentation methods exist.
    *   Healthcare attachments are used for a wide range of operations and administrative workflows beyond prior authorization. Are either of the standards discussed above commonly used in other administrative or operations transactions? Would there be a burden or benefit to using either, or both, standards in light of other administrative or operations workflows? Are there additional standards or implementation specifications ONC should consider that are in common use for healthcare attachments used in other administrative or operations workflows?
        *   Recommend considering a common healthcare attachment standards approach for other use cases, including claims attachments and providing clinical documentation for payer requests (payment audit, utilization review).
        *   These should not be exclusively document-based but can consist of a relevant data set (mix of discrete data and existing documents) that need not be transformed in automated submissions where systems have established trust, provenance, and auditability.
        *   A certified workflow involving multiple HITs for ePA, with fundamental security, provenance, and auditing, would enable original data use versus transmission-only documents.

*   Impact on Patients
    *   How could potential changes to the Certification Program to better support prior authorization positively impact healthcare consumers?
        *   Reduced turnaround times and inefficiencies in prior authorization mean reduced delays in accessing services, better transparency of authorization requirements, and increased provider capacity for care delivery.
        *   Patients may directly query payers/providers for authorization status.
        *   Patients may research service authorization requirements themselves, reducing provider burden and increasing consumer engagement.
    *   How could potential changes reduce the time for patients to receive needed healthcare services, reduce patient non-adherence, and/or lower out-of-pocket costs?
        *   Patient access to care should improve and become timelier due to reduced prior authorization burden.
        *   Increased transparency of coverage requirements will reduce cycle time to determine PA need.
        *   Increased availability of documentation requirements will improve accuracy and completeness of initial submissions, reducing follow-up rounds.
        *   This should increase patient adherence to seeking care due to reduced burdens and turnaround time.
        *   Lower out-of-pocket costs are speculative, depending on whether payers pass administrative cost reductions to patients.
        *   Streamlining provider workflow alone is insufficient; commensurate improvement in payer transparency and actionable responses are crucial.
    *   Besides the provider to payer interactions discussed in this RFI, is there additional functionality that could be added to the Certification Program that would better support patients’ participation in the prior authorization process?
        *   Consider enabling consumers to directly query payers for in-process prior authorization status.
        *   Consider consumer-facing transparency tools for understanding PA requirements.
        *   Spurring support for portability of approved authorizations if a patient changes health plans, or across plan years for chronic conditions, and requiring single authorization for recurring service series/episodes would greatly improve consumer experience.

*   Impact on Providers
    *   To what degree is availability of electronic prior authorization capabilities within certified health IT likely to reduce burden for healthcare providers who currently engage in prior authorization activities?
        *   Promises to reduce administrative steps, enhance transparency of requirements, improve payer responsiveness/clarity, and integrate better with provider workflow.
        *   This should reduce provider time and resources spent on manual/semi-automated administrative PA processes.
        *   ONC's report noted physicians spend inordinate amounts of time on these activities, contributing to burnout.
        *   ePA holds promise for accelerating turnaround time and improving transparency, but payer engagement is essential (transparent, usable, consistent requirements, improved automation).
    *   To what degree are healthcare providers likely to use these new capabilities across their patient panels? Will additional incentives or requirements be needed to ensure healthcare providers effectively use these capabilities? What accompanying documentation or support would be needed to ensure that technology capabilities are implemented in ways that effectively improve clinical workflows?
        *   Potential benefits (reduced operating costs, reduced burden, enhanced transparency) are significant incentives for adoption.
        *   Adoption will be enhanced by broader payer support for reducing individual service authorization necessity when recurring service/episode-level authorization is possible.
        *   "Simple" authorization use cases could emerge where approval is easily adjudicated or automated based on service context.
        *   Ease of use, especially automated collection of transparently defined data from payers, will enhance adoption. Much depends on payer support.

*   Impact on Developers
    *   What estimates can health IT developers share about the cost and time (in hours) of developing electronic prior authorization functionality within certified health IT products?
        *   Effort will be substantial, involving interactions with SMART Apps/intermediaries and integration of workflows for data capture (not automatically retrieved).
        *   This will be a progression over time, not a one-time development for all data.
    *   What factors would inform the burden for health IT developers to develop certified Health IT Modules for electronic prior authorization based on the three Da Vinci IGs described above?
        *   The extent to which ONC requires CHIT to directly support Da Vinci IGs versus enabling flexible configurations and modularity.
        *   Strongly urge ONC not to require CHIT to directly support the three Da Vinci IGs.
        *   Focus on what enables source CHIT to connect to SMART Apps using FHIR US Core for certification.
        *   Current approach with USCDI and FHIR US Core requirements may unnecessarily encumber participating HIT with supporting more data than needed for their role if the entirety of USCDI/FHIR US Core support is imposed on any HIT certified for ePA.
    *   What would be the burden on health IT developers for prior authorization certification criteria referencing the base FHIR standard if there were not yet specific IGs adopted as well? How would potentially moving to criteria with use case specific IGs over time impact development burden? Would such a staged approach be detrimental or beneficial to the long-term development timeline and burden for health IT developers seeking to support electronic prior authorization?
        *   If FHIR R4 is chosen without specific IGs, there is too much potential variation across HIT components, leading to wasted effort once specific guidance is adopted.
        *   A staged approach and flexibility for HIT solutions to address specific ePA workflow steps in Da Vinci guides provide a good starting point and reduce risks of widely different interpretations.
        *   DTR stage needs maturation, so it should not be the initial focus for certification.

*   Payer Implementation
    *   How could the Certification Program support the technology needs of healthcare payers in implementing electronic prior authorization? Should ONC consider payer workflows in the development of certification criteria to support the potential use of certified Health IT Modules by healthcare payers? Would the availability of certified Health IT Modules supporting these workflows reduce the burden for healthcare payers of engaging with healthcare providers in prior authorization processes?
        *   It is imperative that payer HIT is held to the same certification criteria as applicable to their role in interactions as all other HIT.
        *   If all parties adhere to the same standards, confidence levels of all stakeholders will increase.
    *   To what extent would healthcare payers be likely to use these certified Health IT Modules if they were available? To what extent are health IT developers likely to seek certification for Health IT Modules supporting payer workflows if these certification criteria were available?
        *   Without certification criteria for all parties, provider HIT vendors are less likely to seek certification.
        *   Lack of requirements around HIPAA resulted in lack of adoption, and similar can be assumed if parallel efforts for provider and payer HIT are not made.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Cerner Corporation, a leading EHR vendor, advocates for a modular, phased, and flexible approach to electronic prior authorization (ePA) certification, urging ONC to prioritize payer API development and granular criteria to reduce burden across the healthcare ecosystem.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Cerner Corporation
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Leading supplier of electronic health record, clinical, and revenue cycle information systems; John Travis is Vice President and Regulatory Strategy Executive.
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly affected as a major developer of Health Information Technology (HIT), including EHRs, that would need to implement and certify capabilities for electronic prior authorization. They seek practical, implementable standards that account for diverse HIT environments and workflows.
structuredSections.corePosition: We urge ONC to adopt a flexible, modular, and staged approach to electronic prior authorization (ePA) certification criteria, recognizing the complexity of existing HIT configurations across various provider settings. Our core position is that certification should initially focus on enabling provider-side HIT to interoperate with third-party applications and prioritize payer-side API development, rather than imposing a monolithic set of requirements on traditional EHRs.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Develop ePA certification criteria in a progressive, stepwise fashion, starting with functional capabilities for triggering requests and providing necessary health record information.
- Avoid including ePA certification criteria in the definition of "Certified EHR Technology" or "Base EHR" until widespread market adoption occurs.
- Define certification criteria at a finer-grained level to allow HIT developers flexibility in assembling modules, rather than requiring sole-sourcing or overly broad criteria.
- Consider a progression where clinical/administrative HIT is initially paired with third-party products (e.g., SMART apps) to handle payer API connections, shifting the burden of supporting Da Vinci IGs to those apps.
- Prioritize development of criteria for HIT functions supporting clinical workflow and initial information provision, then for downstream processes like ingesting payer decisions and status queries.
- Develop separate certification criteria for interoperability requirements (e.g., Da Vinci IGs) that can be paired with functional criteria.
- Define a distinct healthcare attachments criterion to allow standalone pairing with HIPAA standard transactions or health record requests.
- Ensure ONC criteria anticipate parallel capabilities from payers, especially regarding payer API connections based on Da Vinci IGs, allowing for progressive HIT interoperability.
- Adopt a staged approach for certification, focusing Stage 1 on encouraging payer adoption and certification of CRD, DTR, and PAS IGs, and making test harnesses available for interactions.
- In Stage 2, establish HIT certification criteria based on matured interaction distributions, clearly documenting functional needs within IGs.
- Focus initially on CRD and PAS interactions due to complexity of DTR capabilities.
- For data collection, recognize automated collection via FHIR US Core as a floor, but permit manual/proprietary methods where standards don't cover specific data.
- Standardize information collection for items and services across payers to reduce burden.
- Move towards a FHIR-based approach for healthcare attachments that supports submission of data sets (discrete data and/or existing documents) rather than solely document-centric methods, reducing unnecessary transformation and administrative burden.
- Consider a common healthcare attachment standards approach for other use cases like claims attachments and post-payment audit responses.
- Certify payer HIT to the same standards as other HIT.
- Enhance existing Da Vinci IGs to clearly define building blocks for various HIT to support unambiguous certification criteria.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- The complexity of developing ePA certification criteria due to varied combinations of HIT that may be part of a certified module.
- The risk of "disenfranchising" smaller providers (physician practices, clinics, post-acute care, home health) with less capable HIT portfolios if a flexible, modular approach is not adopted.
- Overly broad certification criteria that could preclude HIT developers from choosing to sole source all capabilities or rely on third-party products.
- The lack of wide deployment and limited testing of Da Vinci IGs, which could impact feasible timelines for their use in production.
- The substantial development effort and cost for HIT developers to implement ePA functionality, especially if criteria are not flexible or require direct support of all Da Vinci IGs.
- The potential for too much variation across HIT components if only the base FHIR Release 4 standard is chosen without specific IGs, leading to wasted efforts.
- The current burden of varied data requirements across payers for prior authorization.
- The potential for patient burden and frustration if only provider workflow is streamlined without commensurate improvement in payer transparency and actionable responses.
- The need for human review and signature for attachments, which is seen as an extraneous step in an automated workflow.
- Lack of clear definitions for digital signatures in ePA processes.
- The risk that provider HIT vendors will decline to seek certification if payer HIT is not held to the same standards.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Cerner draws a parallel between the current ePA certification challenge and the early stages of "Certified Electronic Health Record Technology" (CEHRT) development, where the concept of a "complete EHR" from one HIT developer was limiting, and the market demanded portfolio building from multiple sources/modules. This informs their strong advocacy for modularity.
- They highlight the practical reality that data required for prior authorization may reside across *multiple* source provider systems, and not all of it may be directly available from the HIT that initiates the request, necessitating flexible data collection approaches.
- Cerner points out the "highly wasteful and painful activities for patients" who must seek new authorizations for chronic conditions simply due to changing health plans or turning a new plan year, despite the condition not changing.
- They offer a counterintuitive insight that "little purpose will be served just to streamline and automate the provider workflow if there is not a commensurate improvement in transparency with payers," emphasizing that automation alone without actionable payer responses could lead to quicker frustration.
- They note the current practice where SMART Apps translate requirements into queries to FHIR-based APIs, aligning on FHIR US Core, demonstrating a working model in connectathons that doesn't require source systems to generate QuestionnaireResponses.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "ePA marks potentially the most significant expansion to the certification criteria set since the original 2015 Edition that honed the focus of certification on interoperability, security, consumer access and quality. A boundary of sorts is crossed into the administrative side of healthcare, and the traditional core focus of Certified HIT (CHIT) on clinical EHR is expanded."
- "ONC should take care not to “disenfranchise” smaller providers including physician practices, freestanding clinics and even post-acute care settings or home health agencies that may have less capable HIT portfolios but still may benefit from a level of certified capability to support ePA."
- "These are highly wasteful and painful activities for patients to have to go through just for the sake of satisfying a payer’s administrative processes for approving anew what has been approved for a clinical condition that does not change."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   CAQH CORE appreciates the opportunity to provide feedback on ONC's Request for Information (RFI) regarding Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Criteria.
    *   We specifically appreciate the consideration of how the ONC Health IT Certification Program can address electronic prior authorization for items and services beyond medications.
    *   This will advance adoption and enable consistent exchange of clinical and administrative information.
*   The Committee on Operating Rules for Information Exchange (CORE), an initiative of CAQH, is a non-profit, national multi-stakeholder collaborative.
    *   CORE drives the creation and adoption of healthcare operating rules that support standards, accelerate interoperability, and align administrative and clinical activities among providers, payers, and consumers.
    *   CAQH CORE Participating Organizations represent over 75% of insured Americans, including health plans, providers, EHR vendors, clearinghouses, government entities, associations, and standards development organizations.
    *   CAQH CORE is designated by HHS as the author of federal operating rules for HIPAA administrative healthcare transactions.
        *   Operating rules are defined as "the necessary business rules and guidelines for the electronic exchange of information that are not defined by a standard or its implementation specifications."
        *   Rules are developed via a multi-stakeholder, consensus-based process.
    *   The CORE Certification program demonstrates conformance with CAQH CORE Operating Rules, with over 400 certifications awarded.
*   Although electronic adoption of the HIPAA-mandated X12 278 prior authorization transaction lags other administrative transactions, adoption has increased significantly from 8% to 26% of all prior authorizations over the past five years.
    *   CAQH CORE has observed that electronic transaction adoption is often incremental, growing with industry education, real-world operational experience, gap identification, and ongoing refinement of technical implementation specifications and operating rules.
    *   Simply requiring a technical standard may not result in timely adoption that satisfies industry business needs.
*   Primary barriers to improving prior authorization workflow and increasing electronic adoption include:
    *   Absence of a standard for electronic attachments.
    *   Lack of detail and consistency in the use of data content in the prior authorization transaction.
        *   CAQH CORE operating rules released in 2020 address this barrier and specify requirements for response times.
    *   Other barriers: vendor readiness, industry awareness of a HIPAA standard, ubiquity of proprietary solutions, lack of integration between clinical and administrative systems, and a multitude of state laws.
*   CAQH CORE recently finalized Attachments Prior Authorization Operating Rules.
    *   These rules build on existing CAQH CORE Prior Authorization & Referrals Operating Rules.
    *   They include operating rules to support the X12 275 transaction, HL7-CDA, and emerging standards like HL7 FHIR.
*   Our comments delineate the benefits of a phased approach and the role of standard agnostic operating rules.
    *   We also address the opportunity for HHS to leverage its existing authority under HIPAA to facilitate industry-wide adoption.
*   CAQH CORE comments are based on our history of working with stakeholders to promote interoperability and reduce administrative burden from operational and technical perspectives.
    *   Development of the CAQH CORE Prior Authorization and Attachments Operating Rules, and operational testing via the CAQH CORE Pilot and Measurement Initiative and CORE Certification Program, are particularly relevant.
    *   Our remarks address the following areas ONC is seeking comment:
        *   How to address alignment between standards adopted for HIPAA and standards adopted under the ONC Certification Program.
        *   Methods to facilitate ONC adoption of the proposed certification criterion for the prior authorization workflow, while accounting for a fully HIPAA compliant exchange.
        *   Potential intersection with other administrative operating processes that should be considered when exploring options for attachment exchange and how to harmonize efforts.
        *   Feasible timeline for use of proposed implementation guides given limited testing.
        *   Risks to advancing implementation if proposed implementation guides are not yet ready for adoption, considering potential delays.

*   HHS Authority to Adopt New and Modified Standards for All HIPAA-covered Entities
    *   CAQH CORE encourages HHS to leverage its existing authority under the Administrative Simplification provisions in HIPAA and expanded under the Affordable Care Act (45 CFR § 162.910-930).
    *   This authority should drive industry-wide adoption of standards and operating rules for prior authorization and associated clinical/administrative information exchange.
    *   HHS authority can modify existing standards (45 CFR § 162.910) and/or advance new/emerging standards for additional administrative functions per Section 1173 of the Social Security Act.
        *   Section 1173 states the Secretary shall adopt standards for transactions and data elements to enable electronic health information exchange that are appropriate for financial/administrative transactions and other determined appropriate transactions, consistent with improving healthcare system operation and reducing administrative costs.
    *   Using HIPAA authority ensures the entire industry moves forward together without different requirements for commercial vs. government sectors.
        *   Different rules would complicate adoption and create more administrative burden for payers and providers.
        *   Different requirements for prior authorization and attachments under ONC authorities and HIPAA are likely to confuse the industry and stall adoption.
    *   HIPAA authority ensures administrative transactions with clinical components can be used alongside other associated administrative transactions for a seamless end-to-end workflow.
        *   This includes common applicability of other HIPAA components like the Privacy Rule, which facilitates data exchange between covered entities for treatment, payment, and operations.

*   Facilitating Interoperability Across the Technology Spectrum with Industry-Approved Guardrails
    *   A Standard Agnostic Approach to Prior Authorization Attachments
        *   Establishing a standard approach for exchanging clinical information/attachments for prior authorization with consistent applicability of infrastructure and data content, regardless of the standard used, would:
            *   Ease transition to emerging technology.
            *   Decrease industry burden.
            *   Address the proliferation of proprietary solutions.
        *   Attachments are the bridge between clinical and administrative data, but the workflow remains primarily manual.
            *   The 2021 CAQH Index found only 21% of attachments are sent electronically using defined standards, the lowest of all measured transactions.
            *   A primary pain point cited by stakeholders is the lack of an established attachments standard, leading to uncertainty about organizational investments and deterring uniform vendor support for the X12 278 transaction.
            *   This has resulted in numerous manual workarounds and proprietary approaches that providers must support.
        *   To address these challenges, CAQH CORE launched an Attachments Initiative to establish common specifications for attachment/documentation exchange using existing standards (X12 275, HL7-CDA) and emerging standards (HL7 FHIR).
        *   During rule development, consideration was given to the industry's transformation from traditional EDI to APIs.
        *   The recently published Attachments Operating Rules are scoped to apply when:
            *   Attachments are sent using the X12 275 transaction to support an X12 278 Prior Authorization Request.
            *   Additional documentation is sent without using the X12 275 transaction (e.g., using CORE Connectivity vC4.0.0 to exchange non-X12 payloads like HL7 FHIR Resources, HL7 C-CDA, .PDF) to support an X12 278 Prior Authorization Request.
        *   This concept can serve as a foundation for harmonizing HIPAA-adopted standards (e.g., X12 278, X12 837) and other proposed standards like Da Vinci specifications.
            *   This approach moves the industry forward while capitalizing on existing backend system value and supporting smaller entities.
        *   We encourage ONC and HHS to consider this approach for aligning HIPAA and ONC Certification Program standards until further operational testing of new standards is conducted.
    *   Application to Additional Attachments Use Cases
        *   CAQH CORE recognizes that the attachments workflow could apply beyond prior authorization, including attachments for healthcare claim submissions.
        *   Research during CAQH CORE attachments rule development found considerable alignment in best practices for data variability and workflow issues across different attachment uses (prior authorization vs. claims).
        *   For example, CAQH CORE Attachments Prior Authorization Operating Rules establish data elements for providers to include with attachments to link them to the original prior authorization request.
            *   The pain point for health plans linking attachments to original requests is mirrored in the healthcare claims workflow.
            *   Many data elements for reassociation in prior authorization overlap with those for claims transactions.
        *   CAQH CORE urges ONC to conduct further research into standard alignment and implementation approaches across administrative use cases when developing certification criteria, as it will support efficiency across administrative and clinical systems.
        *   Additionally, we encourage HL7 and the Da Vinci Project to conduct operational workflow testing to understand the generalizability of proposed Da Vinci specifications to healthcare claims and other administrative transactions, to avoid disparate workflows.

*   The Role for Standard Agnostic Operating Rules
    *   Regardless of whether an attachment for prior authorization uses X12 275 or HL7 FHIR, the data and infrastructure of the transaction must remain consistent.
    *   The industry cannot shift from current to emerging standards overnight; enabling common expectations regardless of the standard will keep backend data consistent and facilitate a more successful transition.
    *   Consistent expectations for data content, infrastructure, and connectivity are needed to prevent entities from implementing the same standard in different ways.
        *   Historically, CAQH CORE operating rules have addressed these gaps, aligning exchange expectations across standards for the same business process.
        *   Standard agnostic CAQH CORE operating rules, like the new Attachments Operating Rules, include flexibility to update requirements as the industry evolves, providing structure for providers to interoperate regardless of their technology adoption spectrum.
    *   The most recent version of the CAQH CORE Connectivity Rule vC4.0.0 includes requirements for information exchange using both SOAP and REST technologies, applicable to all CAQH CORE operating rules (including attachments, prior authorization, and healthcare claim transactions).
        *   This creates a standard agnostic approach for uniform transaction exchange.
        *   For example, entities may:
            *   Use CORE Connectivity vC4.0.0 to exchange an X12 275 attachment transaction supporting an X12 278 prior authorization request or an X12 837 claim submission.
            *   Use CORE Connectivity vC4.0.0 to exchange the HL7 FHIR standard for a prior authorization request or claim submission via a REST API.
        *   CORE Connectivity vC4.0.0 thus facilitates the use of X12 standards like X12 278 with new exchange methods including HL7 FHIR.
        *   With this version, data content and infrastructure requirements in the CAQH CORE Prior Authorization Operating Rules and CAQH CORE Attachments Operating Rules can be applied in a standard agnostic manner to align industry use of other prior authorization and attachments exchange mechanisms like HL7 FHIR.
    *   For example, the CAQH CORE Prior Authorization Infrastructure Rule specifies response times for health plans to request clinical information and make final decisions.
        *   While scoped to X12 278, these response time specifications serve as an example of a standard agnostic requirement applicable to other standards to ensure consistent and timely information exchange for prior authorization adjudication.
    *   We urge ONC and HHS to explore a standard agnostic approach to infrastructure and data content requirements that establishes consistent expectations for data exchange throughout the industry, despite the standard used.

*   Value of a Phased Approach and ROI Measurement to Incrementally Move Industry Forward
    *   Real World Implementation to Inform Feasibility
        *   We encourage ONC and HHS to consider a phased approach to electronic prior authorization standards, including operational testing and piloting to understand feasibility and limitations before adoption under the ONC Certification Program.
        *   A phase-based approach eases technology transition and supports smaller health plans and providers with fewer resources, while still moving the industry towards a more automated prior authorization workflow.
        *   Given the need for operational workflow testing, we recommend ONC initially delay adoption of proposed implementation specifications until the Da Vinci implementation guides are tested in an operational workflow.
        *   HHS has multiple ways to communicate and implement policy changes at regular intervals, and we urge ONC and HHS to apply these existing mechanisms to support a phased approach.
        *   Initial results from the CAQH CORE Prior Authorization Pilot and Measurement Initiative (partnership with Cleveland Clinic and PriorAuthNow) demonstrate the value of incremental prior authorization workflow automation in an operational setting.
            *   Leveraging CAQH CORE prior authorization requirements, the HIPAA-mandated X12 278 standard, and APIs, overall time for an end-to-end prior authorization decreased from an average of 45 minutes without automation to 4 minutes with automation.
            *   These results, combined with 2021 CAQH Index data (estimating $3.47 savings for health plans and $7.52 for providers per transaction if fully electronic), indicate that automation using standards and operating rules, even without consistent expectations for attachment exchange, significantly improves provider/health plan time, cost, and patient care.
        *   Implementation of real-world pilot and testing initiatives is imperative for successful inclusion of proposed implementation specifications, especially given limited operational testing of Da Vinci implementation guides.
        *   We recommend ONC and HHS conduct similar operational real-world testing using proposed standards to measure implementation feasibility and impact on workflow efficiency metrics prior to adoption under the ONC Certification Program.
    *   CAQH CORE Certification for the Prior Authorization Workflow
        *   Through the CORE Certification Program, CAQH CORE certifies entities that create, transmit, or use healthcare administrative and financial transactions addressed by CAQH CORE operating rules.
        *   We understand that the time needed to build, implement, alpha/beta test, and successfully certify organizations is often 18 months or more from initial build to certification.
        *   Given limited testing of proposed specifications and the recommended piloting prior to ONC certification, a minimum of 24 months is needed for proposed specifications to be used in production for prior authorization transactions.
        *   Results from the CAQH CORE pilot study and early CORE Certifiers of the CAQH CORE Prior Authorization Operating Rules clearly demonstrate that incremental automation of the prior authorization workflow is beneficial, improving cost savings, administrative burden, and patient care, even without addressing additional information exchange.
        *   Adopting certification criteria that only accounts for a portion of the prior authorization workflow can promote increased adoption of electronic prior authorizations.
        *   However, ONC and HHS should also establish a clear roadmap to help the industry transition to a fully automated prior authorization workflow that includes attachments.

*   Next Steps
    *   There is a need for an overarching strategy to consider how standards for electronic prior authorization workflow, including attachments, can be tested operationally, implemented, and harmonized across administrative transactions to reduce unnecessary burden.
    *   Phased implementation coupling a standard agnostic approach with extensive testing and piloting could ensure adoption of a standardized electronic prior authorization workflow that moves the industry forward without creating additional burden.
    *   CAQH CORE looks forward to supporting this work and encourages agencies to continue considering industry feedback to develop standardized approaches and appropriate timelines that reduce process burden and ensure lasting value for patients.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: CAQH CORE recommends a phased, standards-agnostic approach, leveraging HIPAA authority and real-world testing, to advance electronic prior authorization and attachment exchange, ensuring industry-wide consistency and reducing administrative burden.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** CAQH CORE
- **Type:** Other (Non-profit, national multi-stakeholder collaborative designated by HHS)
- **Role/Expertise:** Author of federal operating rules for HIPAA administrative healthcare transactions; drives creation and adoption of healthcare operating rules; expertise in interoperability, administrative burden reduction, and certification programs.
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** As the HHS-designated author of HIPAA operating rules and an organization focused on healthcare administrative simplification and interoperability, CAQH CORE has a direct stake in ensuring effective, consistent, and widely adopted electronic prior authorization standards and implementation. They represent organizations covering over 75% of insured Americans.
structuredSections.corePosition: We urge HHS to use its existing HIPAA authority to drive industry-wide adoption of standards and operating rules for electronic prior authorization and attachments. We advocate for a phased, standard-agnostic approach that includes robust operational testing and piloting of new specifications, ensuring consistent expectations for data exchange across all industry segments and technological maturities.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- **Leverage existing HHS authority under HIPAA Administrative Simplification provisions** to drive industry-wide adoption of standards and operating rules for prior authorization and associated information exchange.
    - Use this authority to modify existing standards and/or advance new and emerging standards for additional administrative functions.
    - This ensures uniform requirements across commercial and government sectors, avoiding increased administrative burden and confusion.
    - It also facilitates seamless end-to-end workflows by applying HIPAA components like the Privacy Rule consistently.
- **Adopt a standard-agnostic approach for prior authorization attachments** to facilitate interoperability across the technology spectrum.
    - Establish consistent specifications for exchanging clinical information/attachments regardless of the underlying standard (e.g., X12 275, HL7-CDA, HL7 FHIR).
    - This eases the transition to emerging technology, decreases burden, and addresses proprietary solution proliferation.
- **Conduct further research into alignment of standards and implementation approaches across administrative use cases** (e.g., prior authorization and claims attachments) when developing certification criteria.
    - This will support overall efficiency across administrative and clinical systems.
    - Encourage HL7 and the Da Vinci Project to conduct operational workflow testing to assess the generalizability of proposed Da Vinci specifications to other administrative transactions.
- **Implement a phased approach for addressing electronic prior authorization standards** that includes operational testing and piloting prior to adoption under the ONC Certification Program.
    - Initially delay adoption of proposed implementation specifications until Da Vinci implementation guides are tested in an operational workflow.
    - Apply existing HHS mechanisms to support a phased approach, easing technology transition and supporting smaller entities.
- **Allow a minimum of 24 months for the use of proposed specifications in production** for prior authorization transactions, given limited testing and the time required for organizations to build, implement, and certify.
- **Establish a clear roadmap to help the industry transition to a fully automated prior authorization workflow that includes attachments**, even if initial certification criteria only account for a portion of the workflow.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- **Lagging Adoption of Electronic Prior Authorization:** Despite increases, electronic adoption of the HIPAA-mandated X12 278 prior authorization transaction still significantly lags other administrative transactions.
- **Absence of Standard for Electronic Attachments:** This is a primary barrier to improving workflow and increasing electronic adoption, fueling uncertainty about investments and deterring vendor support.
- **Lack of Detail and Consistency in Data Content:** Historical issue leading to lagged adoption, alongside other factors like vendor readiness, industry awareness, proprietary solutions, and lack of system integration.
- **Risk of Disparate Requirements:** Different requirements for commercial vs. government sectors, or between ONC and HIPAA authorities, would complicate adoption, create more administrative burden, confuse the industry, and stall progress.
- **Limited Testing of Proposed Standards:** The Da Vinci implementation guides have largely not been tested in an operational setting, raising concerns about feasibility and potential limitations if adopted prematurely.
- **Insufficient Time for Implementation and Certification:** The typical 18 months or more needed for organizations to build, test, and certify for new standards is insufficient given the limited testing of proposed specifications, potentially delaying adoption.
- **Proliferation of Manual Workarounds and Proprietary Solutions:** The lack of established standards for attachments forces providers to support numerous manual processes and varied proprietary systems.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- CAQH CORE has firsthand experience that electronic transaction adoption is incremental, requiring education, real-world experience, gap identification, and ongoing refinement, rather than just technical standard mandates.
- The 2021 CAQH Index data reveals that only 21% of attachments are sent electronically, marking the lowest adoption rate among all measured transactions, directly linking this to stakeholder pain points around the lack of an established standard.
- Initial results from the CAQH CORE Prior Authorization Pilot and Measurement Initiative showed a significant reduction in end-to-end prior authorization time: from an average of 45 minutes manually to 4 minutes with automation using existing HIPAA standards and APIs.
- This pilot data, combined with CAQH Index cost estimates ($3.47 savings for health plans, $7.52 for providers per fully electronic transaction), demonstrates that even incremental automation yields substantial improvements in time, cost, and patient care, even without fully addressing attachments.
- Research conducted during CAQH CORE's attachment rule development found considerable alignment in best practices for data variability and workflow issues between prior authorization attachments and healthcare claim attachments, suggesting a strong case for harmonized standards.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Simply requiring a technical standard may not result in timely adoption that satisfies industry business needs."
- "Different rules for different segments of the industry would complicate adoption and create more administrative burden for payers and providers. Furthermore, having different requirements for prior authorization and attachments under ONC authorities and HIPAA is likely to confuse the industry and thus stall adoption."
- "The 2021 CAQH Index found that only 21% of attachments are sent electronically using defined standards, the lowest of all measured transactions."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The Academy of Managed Care Pharmacy (AMCP) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments in response to the Request for Information (RFI) regarding electronic prior authorization (ePA) standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria.
    *   AMCP appreciates the Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT (ONC)'s efforts to remove barriers to interoperability and health information exchange in the context of ePA.
    *   AMCP looks forward to continued dialogue with ONC on how managed care pharmacy can be integrated into the electronic prior authorization process.
*   AMCP is the professional association leading the way to help patients get the medications they need at a cost they can afford.
    *   AMCP’s diverse membership includes pharmacists, physicians, nurses, biopharmaceutical professionals, and other stakeholders.
    *   Members leverage specialized expertise in clinical evidence and pharmacoeconomics to:
        *   Optimize medication benefit design.
        *   Optimize population health management.
        *   Help patients access cost-effective and safe medications and other drug therapies.
    *   AMCP members improve the lives of nearly 300 million Americans served by private and public health plans, pharmacy benefit management firms, and emerging care models.
*   AMCP supports standardized ePA processes using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) SCRIPT standard.
    *   The NCPDP SCRIPT standard led to the transformation in the pharmacies services sector by creating and promoting standards for electronic health care transactions and has been widely adopted by healthcare stakeholders.
    *   Recent advancements with standards-based Real-Time Benefit Check transactions have further enabled the increase in prospective ePA and submission of prior authorization before or during electronic prescribing (ePrescribing) workflows in the context of medications.
    *   While the RFI seeks comments on supporting ePA processes for items and services other than medications, AMCP offers its comments as instructive and reflective of its experience on the medication side.
*   Background of Prior Authorization
    *   Prior authorization (PA) is a key step in the managed care prescribing workflow.
    *   PA serves as an important tool pertaining to several categories of managed care utilization management activities, including:
        *   Step therapy.
        *   Practice guideline or pathway use.
        *   Specialist prescribing restrictions.
        *   Authorization requirements related to benefit design (e.g., Medicare Part B benefit eligibility).
    *   PA is not designed to save money at the expense of patient care, nor is it intended to restrict the practice of medicine or frustrate providers.
    *   One of the most critical functions of PA is ensuring patients do not receive concurrent prescriptions that have harmful interactions, sometimes that may even be life-threatening reactions.
    *   PA programs are overwhelmingly evidence-based, employing peer-reviewed literature, federal studies or guidelines, and other evidence-based criteria to ensure appropriate medication use.
    *   PA, however, has generated intense frustration among providers, payers, and patients due to its reliance on outdated modes of communication.
        *   This includes the pervasive use of fax machines, which have contributed to increased wait times and a general lack of transparency.
        *   Moreover, to the extent stakeholders implement electronic systems for prior authorization (e.g., prior authorization web portals), such systems often overlap and are otherwise redundant with systems established by other stakeholders.
            *   This increases administrative burden and otherwise undermines the purpose of switching PA to an electronic process.
            *   For instance, a PA web portal for one payer may require information that is not used by another payer, which prevents providers and patients from standardizing and effectively predicting the information necessary to access covered medications.
    *   Some studies have found that the annual cost from PA-related burdens amounted to $11,440 per pharmacist.
    *   The COVID-19 pandemic further increased these burdens, despite the need for providers to focus on patient care rather than administrative processes such as PA.
    *   The American Medical Association (AMA) surveyed 1,000 practicing physicians between November 23, 2020, and December 14, 2020, and found that 88 percent of physicians described the burden associated with PA as “high” or “extremely high.”
    *   Adoption of electronic PA systems among providers has lagged significantly behind that of payers.
        *   A 2020 survey from AMA found that:
            *   58 percent of providers reported always or often submitting prescription PA requests over the phone.
            *   48 percent reported submitting prescription PA via fax machine.
            *   For medical PA, 59 percent of providers reported always or often using phones.
            *   46 percent reported always or often using fax machines.
        *   These findings indicate substantial opportunity to improve patient care by focusing on expanding the adoption of standardized modern electronic prescribing and prior authorization platforms among health care providers.
*   Electronic Prior Authorization (ePA)
    *   Electronic prescribing (e-prescribing) covers a wide range of prescribing practices that improve patient outcomes and prescribing efficiency.
        *   This is achieved by providing real-time information to prescribers about past medications and patient cost information.
    *   Electronic transmission of prescription information offers benefits over written and oral prescriptions in terms of:
        *   Accuracy
        *   Storage capacity
        *   Accessibility
        *   Security
        *   Productivity
    *   Benefits of electronic prescriptions include the reduction of errors due to:
        *   Misinterpretation of handwritten prescriptions.
        *   Confusion between similarly sounding drug names during oral transmission of prescription orders.
        *   Order‐entry errors.
    *   ePrescribing systems alert prescribers to potentially harmful:
        *   Drug interactions.
        *   Patient drug allergies.
        *   Duplicate or overlapping drug therapy.
        *   This enables the prescriber to adjust the prescription before the pharmacy dispenses the drug.
    *   ePrescribing systems can also allow prescribers to access the formulary for a patient’s prescription drug benefit.
        *   This ensures that they select a therapy for which the patient has coverage, in addition to any clinical edits that may be present.
    *   ePA is an essential component of e-prescribing that facilitates timely access to necessary medications.
        *   It integrates seamlessly with a prescriber’s workflow and reduces many of the administrative burdens noted previously.
    *   Electronic transmission of prior authorization requests from pharmacies and prescribers improves the quality of the request, reduces the time for review, and improves the patient and prescriber experience.
        *   Prescribers often are not aware that a PA is needed until the prescription claim is rejected at the pharmacy.
        *   Pharmacists are often in the middle between the payer and the patient, contacting the prescriber for additional information.
    *   Recent advancements with a standards-based Real-Time Benefit Check have further enabled progress to increase prospective ePA.
    *   ePA can proactively identify the need for a prior authorization, minimizes the information required to make a determination, and presents questions in a logical fashion, reducing the processing time.
    *   This can lead to improved access to needed medications.
*   AMCP Supports the NCPDP SCRIPT ePA and ePA in the Context of Other Items and Services
    *   AMCP has been a strong supporter of ePA and the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs’ (NCPDP) efforts to develop the NCPDP SCRIPT ePA.
    *   Several AMCP members participated in its development.
    *   That standard allows more efficient and timely communication between the prescriber and the patient’s health plan, in addition to optimizing health.
    *   The commercial marketplace has already seen the benefits of ePA and using the NCPDP SCRIPT ePA standard.
    *   AMCP supports the ONC incorporation of the NCPDP SCRIPT ePA into the Health IT Certification Program.
        *   This is viewed as a mechanism to improve the adoption and proliferation of ePA for medications, both among commercial stakeholders and the Medicare/Medicaid programs.
    *   Based on AMCP's experience, they believe that ePA in the context of other items and services can also improve patient care and reduce providers’ administrative burden.
*   AMCP appreciates the opportunity to offer its experience with ePA in the context of medications as ONC considers incorporating ePA for other items and services as part of the Certification Program.
    *   AMCP is committed to lending its managed care expertise in the ONC’s efforts and welcomes the opportunity for further dialogue.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The Academy of Managed Care Pharmacy advocates for the adoption of the NCPDP SCRIPT standard for electronic prior authorization (ePA) within the ONC Certification Program, emphasizing its proven benefits in medication management and potential for other health services to reduce administrative burden and improve patient access.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** ACADEMY OF MANAGED CARE PHARMACY (AMCP)
- **Type:** Organization
- **Role/Expertise:** Professional association representing pharmacists, physicians, nurses, biopharmaceutical professionals, and other stakeholders specializing in clinical evidence and pharmacoeconomics to optimize medication benefit design and population health management. Their members serve nearly 300 million Americans through health plans, pharmacy benefit management firms, and emerging care models.
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** As a professional association focused on optimizing medication access and affordability, they are directly affected by the efficiency and standardization of prior authorization processes, particularly for medications. Their members implement and manage medication benefits for millions of Americans, making ePA a critical tool for improving patient care and reducing administrative burden.
structuredSections.corePosition: We, the Academy of Managed Care Pharmacy (AMCP), strongly support the standardization of electronic prior authorization (ePA) processes through the adoption of the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) SCRIPT standard. We advocate for its incorporation into the Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT (ONC) Certification Program, believing this will reduce significant administrative burdens, improve patient access to necessary medications, and can serve as a model for ePA in other healthcare services.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Incorporate the NCPDP SCRIPT ePA standard into the Health IT Certification Program.
  - This will improve the adoption and proliferation of ePA for medications among commercial stakeholders and Medicare/Medicaid programs.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Reliance on outdated communication methods, such as pervasive use of fax machines, for prior authorization, leading to increased wait times and a general lack of transparency.
- Overlapping and redundant electronic prior authorization systems (e.g., payer-specific web portals) that increase administrative burden and prevent standardization of information needed by providers and patients.
- Significant financial burden associated with PA-related processes on pharmacists, estimated at an annual cost of $11,440 per pharmacist.
- High administrative burden for physicians, with a survey finding 88% described it as "high" or "extremely high."
- Lagging adoption of standardized electronic PA systems among health care providers, with many still relying on phone (58-59%) and fax (46-48%) for submitting requests.
- Prescribers often being unaware of PA needs until a prescription claim is rejected at the pharmacy, frequently placing pharmacists in the middle to contact prescribers for additional information.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- The NCPDP SCRIPT standard has already transformed the pharmacy services sector by creating and promoting standards for electronic health care transactions, demonstrating its widespread adoption and effectiveness.
- Recent advancements with standards-based Real-Time Benefit Check transactions have enabled an increase in prospective ePA and the submission of prior authorization before or during ePrescribing workflows for medications.
- The commercial marketplace has already experienced the benefits of ePA and the use of the NCPDP SCRIPT ePA standard.
- The COVID-19 pandemic further exacerbated prior authorization-related burdens, highlighting the critical need for providers to focus on patient care rather than administrative processes.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "PA is not designed to save money at the expense of patient care, nor is it intended to restrict the practice of medicine or frustrate providers. One of the most critical functions of PA is ensuring patients do not receive concurrent prescriptions that have harmful interactions, sometimes that may even be life-threatening reactions."
- "Some studies have found that the annual cost from PA-related burdens amounted to $11,440 per pharmacist."
- "For instance, a PA web portal for one payer may require information that is not used by another payer, which prevents providers and patients from standardizing and effectively predicting the information necessary to access covered medications."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The American Hospital Association (AHA) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) Request for Information (RFI) regarding electronic prior authorization standards.
*   The AHA commends ONC for taking action to remove inappropriate barriers to patient care by streamlining prior authorization processes.
*   While prior authorization can be a helpful tool for ensuring appropriate care, it is too often used in a manner that leads to:
    *   Dangerous delays in treatment
    *   Clinician burnout
    *   Waste in the health care system
*   The RFI is a welcome step towards helping providers focus time on patient care rather than paperwork.
*   The AHA strongly supports the creation of a usable, scalable, and efficient solution to reduce prior authorization impacts on patients and providers.
*   The AHA urges ONC, in collaboration with the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), to pilot the technologies and workflows described in the rule prior to taking any regulatory steps, including certification or codification of standards, to minimize unintended negative consequences such as increased costs or burden.

*   Background
    *   Philosophically, the AHA agrees with the goals of prior authorization, which America’s Health Insurance Plans states are "to help ensure patients receive optimal care based on well-established evidence of efficacy and safety, while providing benefit to the individual patient."
    *   However, many health plans apply prior authorization requirements in ways that create:
        *   Dangerous delays in care
        *   Clinician burnout
        *   Increased costs for the health care system
    *   Inefficient prior authorization can negatively impact the quality of care.
        *   According to a 2021 American Medical Association (AMA) survey of over 1,000 physicians:
            *   91% of respondents indicated that prior authorization "had a significant or somewhat negative clinical impact."
            *   34% reported that prior authorization had led to a serious adverse event such as a death, hospitalization, disability, permanent bodily damage, or other life-threatening event for a patient in their care.
        *   The federal government has acknowledged the risk of delays in care caused by prior authorization, urging health plans to ease such requirements during the COVID-19 public health emergency to allow staff to focus on care delivery and prevent delays.
    *   Prior authorization puts a heavy burden on clinicians and contributes to workforce burnout.
        *   National Academies of Medicine states that clinician burnout is associated with job demands related to workload, time pressure, and work inefficiencies, such as burdensome administrative processes that divert clinicians’ attention from patients.
    *   The use of standardized electronic prior authorization transactions has the potential to save patients, providers, and utilization review entities significant time and resources and can speed up the care delivery process.
    *   To effectively update and create standard transactions without unduly burdening health care payment processes, regulators should approach potential changes judiciously.
    *   Any substantial change in the technology and/or standards used in health care information exchange should be:
        *   Sufficiently tested to ensure functionality.
        *   Analyzed to establish projected return on investment.
        *   Incorporated according to an appropriate glide path to minimize systematic disruption.

*   Certified Health IT Functionality
    *   One of the most frustrating issues with prior authorization for providers and patients is the variation in plan submission processes.
    *   Plans vary widely on accepted methods of prior authorization requests and supporting documentation submission.
        *   The most common methods remain using fax machines and contacting call centers, with regular hold times of 20 to 30 minutes.
        *   Plans offering electronic methods most commonly use proprietary plan portals, which require:
            *   Significant time spent logging into a system.
            *   Extracting data from the provider’s clinical system.
            *   Inserting information into the payer’s portal.
            *   Adhering to idiosyncratic plan requirements, thereby reducing administrative efficiencies.
    *   Transposing electronic health record (EHR) information into payer portals often leads to entry errors, resulting in denials for medically appropriate procedures that must be reprocessed or appealed, delaying patient access to care.
    *   The AHA strongly supports an end-to-end automated prior authorization process that integrates with clinicians’ EHR workflow.
        *   Using EHR technology for submission and processing of prior authorizations empowers clinicians to utilize this information during treatment planning and creates potential for meaningful, real-time access to this data.
    *   Congress recognized the importance of standardizing the prior authorization workflow when it included the transaction in its administrative simplification provisions under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA).
    *   Unfortunately, adoption of the HIPAA standard transaction for processing prior authorizations (X12 278 Healthcare Service Review and Response) has been extremely poor, partly due to the failure to establish an attachment standard for submitting supporting clinical documentation.
    *   The AHA has concerns with the RFI's reference to the continued use of the 278 transaction in tandem with the Da Vinci Prior Authorization Support (PAS) application programming interface (API).
        *   To be HIPAA compliant, the PAS Implementation Guide (IG) requires FHIR-API prior authorization information to be translated into the 278 transaction for transmission to the health plan, then translated back to FHIR.
        *   This translation would likely require providers to procure additional clearinghouse services, which runs contrary to HIPAA’s administrative simplification goals and undermines provider and industry savings.
        *   Absent additional benefits or efficiencies, a standard process should not require translation from FHIR.
        *   The AHA recommends continued granting of HIPAA exceptions to pilot direct FHIR-to-FHIR exchange for Da Vinci IGs to test this new technology and inform future decisions regarding prior authorization electronic standards under HIPAA.

*   Implementation Specifications for Prior Authorization
    *   The AHA fully supports ONC’s interest in streamlining prior authorization by utilizing new technology to increase consistency and timeliness.
    *   However, the AHA cautions against premature implementation of a solution that has yet to be completed and tested to ensure functionality in a real-world environment.
    *   The proposed IGs are at the Standard for Trial Use (STU) ballot level and are not yet normative (finalized).
        *   According to HL7, an STU ballot classification "is used to vet content that is deemed ‘ready to implement’ by a sponsoring work group, but where there has not yet been significant implementation experience."
        *   The recommended IGs are currently out for ballots and will inevitably undergo revision.
