    *   The most 'advanced' electronic transactions implemented, like the 835 ERA (Electronic Remittance Advice), still face numerous challenges in provider enrollment and receipt, even 8 years after the compliance deadline.
    *   Hundreds of clearinghouses service over 50,000 health plans and over 1 million healthcare providers.
    *   A health plan typically uses one clearinghouse, which may impose unreasonable terms on the provider clearinghouse. More than two clearinghouses are often involved in one transaction, and health plans use separate vendors/clearinghouses for certain claims.
    *   Similarly, for prior authorization, some health plans use separate vendors for oncology or high-cost biological medications vs. other medications, or for radiology/physical therapy.
    *   There is no easy way for a provider to know who to contact electronically, especially without an HPID as a main point of contact.
    *   Previously adopted NCPDP Script standard 2017071 is dysfunctional; preauthorization for the same generic medication requires different forms with different data elements for each health plan.
    *   Currently adopted X12 278 standards lack real-time responses, the ability to attach required documentation, and fail routinely due to lack of standardization across health plans for the same service.
    *   This essentially requires a health IT provider (EHR provider) to custom-code for each transaction to 'prepopulate' some data.
    *   Despite EHR integration, staff often go to the provider portal to initiate requests due to frequent integration failures.
    *   A multitude of vendors are involved, requiring custom-built and tested two-way connections between clearinghouses as there is no single "plug-to-play" standard.
    *   The Cooperative Exchange (clearinghouse industry association) states its members maintain 8,000 payer and 1,000 HIT vendor connections, which is not feasible for every clearinghouse to maintain.

*   **National Data-Warehouse Exchange Hub / Clearinghouse**
    *   To address the problem, we recommend creating a national 'data exchange hub/clearinghouse' using standard real-time API connections, maintained by the Federal Government, possibly with a competing member-owned private option.
    *   This would allow providers and health plans/clearinghouses to maintain only one standard direct connection for seamless data flow across all electronic transactions.
    *   A national hub would vastly simplify and streamline the system, solving the inter-connectivity problem that persists for 835 ERA transactions despite deadlines.
    *   This would significantly lower transactional costs, a major HIPAA goal.
    *   An example of a functional 'national' data-warehouse exchange hub is the NACHA-governed ACH system, which uses two central clearinghouses (Federal Reserve Bank and "The Clearing House") with interconnections for over 10,000 financial institutions.
    *   This would enable each healthcare participant to maintain only ONE connection, reducing costs, increasing clearinghouse competition, and promoting innovation.
    *   The proposed national hub is the only practical way to address problems identified by ONC:
        *   (i) **difficulty in determining whether an item or service requires prior authorization;**
            *   Requires ability to directly, in real-time, query the health plan, which necessitates a centrally accessible National Health Plan Identifier (HPID).
            *   Healthcare providers must obtain basic information in real-time (within seconds) during the visit to resolve issues, consider alternative therapy, or inform the patient of delays. Asynchronous, delayed responses lead to patient care injury.
        *   (ii) **difficulty in determining payer-specific prior authorization requirements for those items and services;**
            *   A real-time transaction with a response on PA necessity must be returned within seconds.
            *   Complicated by lack of standardization for many treatments (e.g., lab codes for genetic testing).
            *   Standardized 'treatment-specific' prior authorization requirements should be adopted in consultation with industry (payers/providers).
            *   There should be minimal variability in information needed for each specific treatment from a clinical or payer perspective.
            *   Having to program/respond to 50,000 different health plan formats for the same treatment is a major cause of dysfunction.
            *   Non-standard PA requirements should only be for rare disease treatment or exceptions (not more than 5% of the time) and managed via "Exception" functionality.
        *   (iii) **inefficient use of provider and staff time to navigate communications channels such as fax, telephone, and various web portals; and (iv) unpredictable and lengthy amounts of time to receive payer decisions.**
            *   Relying on the existing dysfunctional system without a clear national standard for connectivity is costly and harmful to patients, increasing costs and denying care.
            *   Provider staff often give up trying to surmount insurmountable PA barriers.
            *   As much as 50% of medically-necessary care is not received due to prior authorization barriers (citing multiple studies).

*   **Patient access to ePA**
    *   Patients should be given access to an electronic prior authorization system by their health plans and be able to initiate and monitor the ePA process.
    *   Patient access to prior authorization empowers patients, puts them in control, and allows them to keep health plans and providers accountable.
    *   Patients have real-time access to their full medical records (21st Century Cures Act, HIPAA Access Right) and possess all requisite information for PA forms.
    *   Many patients are informed and may prefer doing their own prior authorizations, furthering patient autonomy.
    *   Since health plan coverage is 'owned' by the patient, they should have unfettered access to electronic authorization and a choice to perform PA or delegate it.
    *   Legally, physicians require patient consent to submit PA requests.

*   **Timeliness of Prior Authorization Responses**
    *   ONC should impose a reasonable requirement that a prior authorization decision be made within 24 hours, if not returned in real-time.
    *   Given the nature of healthcare, this is a reasonable requirement, especially since much PA administration is outsourced to different time zones.
    *   There is no rational reason why a decision cannot be made within 24 hours for >95% of requests.
    *   Just as providers must staff offices for timely care, health plans should respond to inquiries timely, regardless of weekends or holidays.
    *   Delays in diagnosis and treatment often result in increased morbidity, higher costs (to patient and GDP), and lost income/productivity.
    *   Fundamentally, prior authorization is a financial problem.
    *   There is a tremendous financial incentive for health plans to impose prior authorizations, even for low-cost, nearly harmless generics (e.g., tadalafil 5 mg daily for BPH costing <$5/month), as it decreases medically necessary utilization by as much as 50%, leading to lower Medical Loss Ratio (MLR).
    *   At the same time, prior authorization is an uncompensated financial burden on healthcare practices, as CPT codes do not account for its cost.
    *   If fairly compensated with a small profit margin, providers would hire staff to handle unlimited prior authorizations.
    *   Health plans 'internalize' financial benefits of prior authorization and 'externalize' costs by imposing them on providers, leading to limitless incentive for PAs.
    *   Limited automation on the provider end means this asymmetric financial incentive will worsen with automation.
    *   The only way to improve "prior authorization" processes is to eliminate as many as possible by internalizing the cost to the health plans that benefit.

*   **Barriers to Automation of ePA**
    *   ONC envisions ePA embedded into EHR workflow, forcing doctors to complete PA forms during ordering (e.g., for CT scans or medications).
    *   This vision assumes data is stored as 'discrete' categorized data (e.g., EHR knows difference between COVID PCR vs. antibody test), which is mostly not true.
    *   Most data is NOT discrete; there are no "standard" fields for lab results (each lab reports differently), or CT scan findings, or physical exam findings.
    *   None of this can be automated even by sophisticated EHRs (Cerner, Epic, AllScripts). AI will have little role as 95% statistical confidence is not the 'correct' answer.
    *   Because almost nothing in healthcare is standardized (lab tests, radiology tests), very few clinical data points can be "prepopulated" automatically beyond demographics.
    *   Example: a PSA test ordered by a primary care doctor and sent to a urologist is often received as a "Fax" or "PDF," not 'discrete data.'
    *   Deep embedding into the workflow will require physicians to spend additional uncompensated time, increasing administrative burden, lowering productivity, increasing wait times, and limiting care access.
    *   The only benefit of ePA to physician practices is electronic receipt/sending; 80% of the work will still be manual.
    *   Insurers save 90% of the cost with ePA as data is "typed-in" by doctors as "discrete" database, allowing their computers to 'automatically' approve or deny.
    *   Net result: 80%+ savings to health plans, minimal financial benefit to physician practices.
    *   There is a strong financial incentive for health plans to impose more prior authorization requirements due to their minimal costs with ePA.
    *   There are no National Standard forms for ePA for each medication, imaging study, or service. Each of 50,000+ plans can create its own form and criteria (e.g., for Eliquis), changing it multiple times a year.
    *   EHRs would have to 'support' each of these forms, imposing significant costs on EHRs, which would be paid for by physician practices.
    *   No compensation is envisioned for prior authorizations.

*   **Comments to Part B:**
    *   As part of standard operating rules adopted by CMS, fair compensation to the provider based on actual time required to respond to each prior authorization must be included.
    *   This aligns with HIPAA Administrative Simplification goals: improve adoption, lower costs, modernize healthcare.
    *   Prior authorization cannot be a money-losing activity for healthcare providers, nor a 'jackpot' to health plans.
    *   New CPT codes may be required to document this process, and CMS may request adoption and adequate statutory reimbursement adjusted for healthcare CPI yearly.

*   **Comments to Part C:**
    *   The Health Information Technology Advisory Committee (HITAC) recommendation to incorporate prior authorization into all EHR-ordering workflows is not desirable in all situations.
    *   Healthcare providers must be able to DISABLE this functionality in EHR.
    *   This recommendation presumes health-insurance-based treatment is the only viable option.
    *   Many Americans opt-out of health insurance 'barriers' and pay out-of-pocket for denied care or seek timely care through self-payment to avoid PA barriers.
    *   A sizable portion of healthcare providers opts out of participating with health plans due to administrative burdens unrelated to care delivery.
    *   Providers should have a choice whether to participate in electronic prior authorization deep-embedded in their "workflow."
    *   The requirement to integrate prior authorization in EHR-ordering workflow could essentially put health plans in charge of medical decisions and expose them to malpractice claims.
    *   **API calls and responses must be real-time.**
        *   A response requirement within 24 hours (if not real-time), regardless of weekends or holidays, must be imposed.
        *   There is no valid business argument that costs are lower if patients wait longer; delay beyond 24 hours only serves to delay care, knowing many patients will forgo medically necessary care due to time barriers.
        *   Pilots are not asked to wait until Monday to land; patients and practitioners should not wait until Monday to start treatment.
        *   Due to connection complexity and number of health plans, a central "HUB" data exchange managed by HHS/CMS would be the only viable solution.
        *   Implementation of the previously canceled standardized National Health Plan ID (HPID) must occur promptly.
        *   Just as health plans want provider NPI, providers need reciprocity and want to know HPID.

*   **Response to part D:**
    *   Practically, with arbitrary PA requirements across 50,000+ health plans and no legal limitations on services requiring PA, certified health IT providers cannot predict when PA is necessary.
    *   This is only possible if the provider, health IT, and health plan are integrated (e.g., Optum/UnitedHealthcare).
    *   The only way to address this is legislatively or through rule-making to standardize and limit what services are eligible for 'prior authorization.'
    *   Given the expected introduction of 'stable' coin blockchain-based cryptocurrency under Federal Reserve Bank supervision, a requirement for health plans to implement cryptocurrency blockchain capabilities to return payment along with prior authorization requests should be mandatory.
    *   The "Da Vinci Coverage Requirements Discovery (CRD) is not a panacea.
        *   Theoretically, CRD can result in 'full automation,' but it requires Health-IT providers to custom code the "flow" for each prior-authorization service for each of the 50,000+ health plans.
        *   Health-IT providers would also need to manage arbitrary changes any health plan can implement at any moment. Without limitations on health plans, this is not practicable.
    *   This proposal must consider its effect on smaller healthcare practices and smaller Health-IT providers, as those with less than 500-1000 providers or Health-IT providers with less than 50,000 providers will not find it financially viable to comply.

*   **Documentation Template and Coverage Rules (DTR) comments:**
    *   Health plans must be required to 'prepopulate' as many fields as they can before requesting data from the healthcare provider.
    *   There should be a requirement that only an absolute minimum of data elements is requested by the health plan.
    *   Health plans have extensive access to data (clinical labs, insurance claims, pharmacy claims, patient-provided information).
    *   The only fields they should request from providers are those unobtainable by other means.
    *   Many health insurers manage PMBs, knowing prescription/refill history and claim history when patients move plans; they have or can get Labcorp/Quest data.
    *   If providers can get SureScripts data for prescriptions and report narcotics to the state, insurers can do the same.
    *   Health plan IT systems must be certified for Prior Authorization.
    *   The costs of authorizations need to be 'internalized' to health plans, and physicians must be compensated properly. Ultimately, patients pay to get their authorizations done correctly.
    *   There should be a reasonable and justifiable high standard for requiring prior authorization; little justification for PAs on services approved 80-90% of the time or for $5-10 generic medications with minimal risks.
    *   Patients should be given access to an electronic prior authorization system by their health plans, as they have the greatest stake in its approval.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: New York Urology Specialists urges ONC to standardize electronic prior authorization (ePA) processes with a national data hub, patient access, and fair provider compensation, citing the current system's uncompensated burden on providers, patient care delays, and dysfunctional lack of interoperability.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** New York Urology Specialists / Alex Shteynshlyuger MD
- **Type:** Business (Medical Practice)
- **Role/Expertise:** Director of Urology; actively involved in direct patient care and managing administrative burdens of a medical practice.
- **Geographic Scope:** Local/State (New York, N.Y.), commenting on national federal regulation
- **Stake in Issue:** Direct experience as a healthcare provider with the significant administrative, financial, and patient care burdens imposed by complex, unstandardized, and uncompensated prior authorization processes.
structuredSections.corePosition: On behalf of New York Urology Specialists, we believe the current electronic prior authorization (ePA) landscape is dysfunctional, imposes uncompensated financial burdens on healthcare providers, and creates significant barriers to patient care. We advocate for mandated national standards, patient empowerment in the ePA process, and fair compensation for providers to ensure ePA adoption lowers costs and improves healthcare access, rather than increasing provider burden and denying care.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Mandate an ON/OFF switch for ePA service in EHRs, controlled by the healthcare provider.
- Grant patients access to and control over electronic prior authorization systems provided by their health plans, allowing them to initiate and monitor the process.
- Mandate national standards and standard forms for each service requiring prior authorization.
- Create a national 'data exchange hub/clearinghouse' (similar to the NACHA-governed ACH system) to simplify all healthcare electronic transactions, allowing one connection per participant.
  - This requires the prompt implementation of a standardized National Health Plan ID (HPID) to enable electronic identification of health plans.
- Establish a high standard for requiring prior authorization, eliminating it for services approved 80-90% of the time or for low-cost, low-risk generic medications.
- Require health plans to 'prepopulate' as many fields as possible on ePA forms before requesting data from providers, ensuring only absolute minimum, otherwise unobtainable data is requested.
- Mandate certification for health plan IT systems regarding prior authorization compliance (e.g., correct use of standard national forms, minimal data requests, prepopulation).
- Implement standard operating rules requiring fair compensation to providers based on the actual time required to respond to prior authorizations.
  - Consider new CPT codes for documenting this process with adequate statutory reimbursement adjusted yearly for healthcare CPI.
- Impose a requirement for prior authorization decisions to be made within 24 hours, if not returned in real-time, regardless of weekends or holidays.
- Legislate or rule-make to standardize and limit what specific services are eligible for prior authorization.
- Consider requiring health plans to implement cryptocurrency blockchain capabilities to return required payment along with prior authorization requests.
- Ensure any proposed changes financially viable for smaller healthcare practices and Health-IT providers.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- The healthcare ecosystem's complexity, with over 50,000 health plans and no standardized Health Plan ID (HPID), makes automation impossible and creates confusion about which entity to contact for prior authorization.
- Current prior authorization processes are highly inefficient, requiring providers and staff to navigate faxes, telephones, and multiple web portals, leading to extensive uncompensated time.
- Existing electronic standards (NCPDP Script 2017071, X12 278) are dysfunctional, lack real-time responses, cannot attach documentation, and require custom coding for each of the thousands of health plan formats, leading to frequent failures and staff reverting to manual processes.
- There is an asymmetric financial incentive where health plans 'internalize' benefits (lower Medical Loss Ratio due to reduced utilization) and 'externalize' costs onto healthcare providers, creating an endless incentive for health plans to impose more prior authorizations.
- Prior authorization is a money-losing activity for healthcare providers, as current CPT codes do not account for the administrative time and cost involved, making it a disincentive to provide care.
- The premise that EHR data is "discrete" and easily automatable for prior authorization is largely mistaken; most clinical data is not standardized or readily extractable, requiring manual physician input.
- Delays in prior authorization decisions (often days or weeks) lead to increased patient morbidity, higher costs, loss of patient income and productivity, and demonstrably result in as much as 50% of medically necessary care not being received.
- A requirement to deeply embed ePA into EHR workflows without an ON/OFF switch forces physicians to spend more uncompensated time during patient visits, lowering productivity and limiting patient access to care.
- The multitude of vendors and lack of "plug-to-play" standards means clearinghouses maintain thousands of non-standard connections, which is unsustainable and parallels persistent issues with electronic remittance advice (ERA) adoption.
- The Health Information Technology Advisory Committee (HITAC) recommendation for universal EHR integration removes provider choice and risks putting health plans in control of medical decisions, potentially exposing them to malpractice claims.
- Current proposals like "Da Vinci Coverage Requirements Discovery (CRD)" are not practical solutions without limitations on health plans, as they require continuous custom coding for arbitrary and frequent changes from over 50,000 health plans.
- The financial burden of complying with complex electronic prior authorization requirements may be unviable for smaller healthcare practices and Health-IT providers.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- The comment highlights that many physicians choose not to pursue ePA for certain low-cost generic medications, such as a $20 for 90-day supply generic medication available at Costco, suggesting the administrative burden outweighs the cost-saving purpose.
- It vividly describes the difficulty in determining the correct entity for prior authorization, where "the insurance card often does not actually list the health plan name but instead lists plan administrator and various contractors," leading to multiple phone calls.
- It draws a compelling analogy between the lack of standardized health plan identification and the need for a National Provider Identifier (NPI) database for providers, stating, "Without an ability to electronically identify each of over 50,000 plans...automation is not possible or feasible."
- The comment asserts that "As much as 50% of medically-necessary care is not received due to the prior authorization barriers," citing multiple research publications to support this significant impact on patient access.
- It provides a clear economic analysis, stating that "Health plans 'internalize' the financial benefits of prior authorization and 'externalize' the cost of prior authorization by imposing the costs on health care providers," explaining the perverse incentive for plans to impose more PAs, even for low-cost generics like tadalafil.
- It offers a surprising insight into the limitations of current EHR data for automation, noting that "Most data is NOT stored as discrete data elements," and "AI... will have little role in 'easing the pain points in this area as the 95% statistical confidence limit is not the same as the 'correct' answer."
- It uses a relatable example to illustrate data standardization problems: "If a primary care doctor orders a PSA test and sends it to the urologist, the urologist who is getting prior authorization has that PSA test as a 'Fax' or 'PDF', not as 'discrete data.'"
- A memorable analogy emphasizes the urgency of timely responses: "Pilots flying in over the weekend are not asked to wait until Monday to land; similarly, patients and healthcare practitioners should not wait until Monday to start treatment."
- The comment suggests a highly innovative, albeit unconventional, solution: requiring health plans to implement "cryptocurrency blockchain capabilities to return required payment along with prior authorization requests" in anticipation of "stable" coin blockchain integration.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "No one has a greater stake in getting prior authorization approved than the patient."
- "To be adopted, prior authorization cannot be a money-losing activity for healthcare providers. It cannot be a 'jackpot' to health plans."
- "As much as 50% of medically-necessary care is not received due to the prior authorization barriers."
- "Pilots flying in over the weekend are not asked to wait until Monday to land; similarly, patients and healthcare practitioners should not wait until Monday to start treatment."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The HL7® Da Vinci Project and its membership appreciate the opportunity to comment on RIN 0955–AA04, Request for Information: Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria.
    *   They specifically appreciate the recognition and consideration of the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction Implementation Guides (IGs):
        *   Coverage Requirements Discovery (CRD)
        *   Documentation Templates and Rules (DTR)
        *   Prior Authorization Support (PAS)
*   Prior Authorization (PA) is a significant burden for providers and payers, with noteworthy impact on patients.
    *   Research by RTI for AHIP indicates that electronic PA (ePA) can reduce PA decision time threefold, or by about 69%.
    *   This leads to more timely care for patients and less burden for all involved parties.
    *   The HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs can meaningfully support the move to ePA.
*   The HL7 Da Vinci Project and its members support leveraging these IGs for inclusion in the ONC Health IT Certification Program.
    *   To address the true burden of the current PA process on patients, providers, and payers, it is necessary to integrate PA into the workflow and automate the end-to-end process.
    *   FHIR, and specifically the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs, facilitate both workflow integration and automation.
*   One challenge in implementing these IGs and ePA will be payers declaring the data needed for PA decision-making.
    *   Therefore, they support phasing in the use of all or elements of the IGs with an incremental approach.
    *   ePA should first focus on policies that leverage fully structured data and/or provider attestation.
    *   An incremental approach supports the needed business transformation from a document-driven to a data-driven approach and supports automation.
    *   Specialty medications covered under the medical benefit should be included in ePA discussions and in any certified Health IT Module requirements, as the NCPDP SCRIPT Standard is already under ONC certification.
*   Requirements placed on providers, payers, or vendors involved in the PA process must be consistent and complimentary for all parties.
    *   ONC and CMS are encouraged to actively align requirements to ensure benefits of ePA are shared and primary burden drivers are efficiently addressed.
    *   This alignment will maximize benefits for patients, leading to PA processes embedded in provider workflows and fully automated, enabling meaningful conversations at the point of care, more timely care, and adherence to care plans.
*   Comments and suggestions regarding specific RFI questions:
    *   **Benefits to Patients, Providers, Payers, and Vendors**
        *   Improving the PA process through automation and workflow incorporation could profoundly impact patients.
        *   Knowing if PA is needed, its cost implications, and alternative options at the point of care enables informed choices during the visit.
        *   The HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs facilitate this conversation and can help approve PA before a patient leaves the office.
        *   This improves care by reducing treatment delays and supporting care plan adherence.
        *   It reduces cost by supporting timely care delivery.
        *   It supports transparency by giving patients and advocates access to PA information and visibility into the process.
        *   Any application via an API could access this information, keeping patients informed and engaged.
        *   Automating the PA process and integrating it into workflows using these IGs would significantly benefit patients.
        *   Providers would benefit by reducing burden through automation of manual processes.
        *   Workflow integration allows providers to make optimal care decisions with patients, improving the provider/patient relationship and care outcomes.
        *   Increased transparency for providers helps them understand PA needs, documentation requirements, and supports documentation compilation, reducing resubmissions.
        *   Payers would see reduced burden from automation and lower effort due to fewer unnecessary or insufficiently documented PA submissions.
        *   Initial effort for payers to declare data for PA decision-making can be minimized by starting incrementally with structured data/provider attestation.
        *   This provides time for payers to transform their business to support streamlined, automated PA.
        *   Building this infrastructure could also support efficiencies across other processes like claims attachments, denials, and chart pulls due to reusability.
        *   Vendors would support providers and payers by supplying technological tools for PA integration into clinical workflows, providing valuable services and patient benefits.
    *   **Functional Capabilities for Electronic Prior Authorization in Certified Health IT**
        *   Agreement that the first six functional capabilities outlined in the RFI are important for a complete end-to-end ePA process and should be included in certified Health IT Modules.
        *   The seventh functional capability is not believed to be needed.
        *   Each of the seven RFI functional capabilities is facilitated by one or more HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs (Table 1 provided).
            *   Table 1: Functional Capabilities and the Relevant HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IG(s)
                *   1. Identify when prior authorization is applicable for an item or service, using clinical decision support (CDS) and/or user input, and for receiving notifications of changes in such applicability: CRD
                *   2. Query a payer API for prior authorization requirements for each item and service and identify in real time specific rules and documentation requirements: CRD & DTR
                *   3. Collect clinical and administrative documentation needed to complete prior authorization documentation (electronic forms or templates) from a health IT system: DTR
                *   4. Electronically submit completed documentation for prior authorization to a payer's API, along with supporting information: PAS
                *   5. Receive a response from a payer regarding approval, denial (including a reason for denial), or need for additional information: PAS
                *   6. Query a payer's system for updates on a pending prior authorization request and have a reason returned as to why a request is still pending: PAS
                *   7. Effectively capture and persist digital signatures (or other indications of provider review and assent), enable data integrity of documentation over time, and support other features necessary to meet payer administrative requirements associated with prior authorization transactions: DTR & PAS
        *   The HL7 FHIR Da Vinci CRD IG defines a standard for Health IT to initiate PA workflow using CDS Hooks to query a payer CDS endpoint to determine:
            *   If an item/service is covered and/or requires PA.
            *   Alternative items/services with improved coverage and/or lower cost.
            *   Documentation requirements for medical necessity and appropriateness.
            *   Requirements for appropriate use.
        *   The CRD IG also supports determining changes in payer coverage.
        *   The HL7 FHIR Da Vinci DTR IG, as a companion to CRD, supports ensuring documentation requirements are met.
        *   Stakeholder concerns from December 2020 about these IGs using CDS Hooks, CQL, and SMART on FHIR are being actively addressed by the Da Vinci community.
            *   Additional refinement to the published IGs is part of the early May 2022 ballot cycle.
            *   Despite ongoing work, the IGs are considered valuable and actionable in their current state.
        *   A challenging aspect will be payers expressing data needed for PA decision-making in CQL for automated documentation collection and evaluation.
            *   This is primarily a business transformation issue, not a technology issue.
            *   Payers must declare needed data, and providers must adjust to new ePA capabilities.
            *   This is why an incremental, phased approach is advocated, starting with PA rules for items/services leveraging fully structured data and/or provider attestation.
            *   Additional items/services can be added over time, allowing payers to undergo necessary business transformation.
        *   The fourth, fifth, and sixth functional capabilities are facilitated by the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci PAS IG.
        *   The seventh functional capability (digital signatures) should not be included in certified Health IT Module(s).
            *   If IGs are implemented, the ePA process moves toward automation, DTR provides thorough understanding of requirements, and additional documentation should not be needed.
            *   Sufficient system controls (e.g., usernames, passwords, audit trails) make digital signatures unnecessary.
            *   Moving forward with ePA should transform, not just digitize, the paper process into an optimized digital solution.
            *   Including this capability could impede transformation and automation.
        *   If the seventh functional capability were included, support could be provided via the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Clinical Data Exchange (CDex) IG, which defines support for digital signatures in its draft Attachments section (May 2022 ballot cycle).
            *   This solution would be leveraged for DTR and PAS, allowing signatures to be exchanged as part of documentation within the digital workflow.
            *   Caution is advised, as this could encourage increased documentation burden and potential delays, and may be redundant given existing system controls.
        *   The first six functional capabilities are foundational for effective ePA and represent the appropriate minimum.
        *   The HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs facilitate these capabilities.
        *   Opportunities exist for continuous improvement, reflecting the value of IGs born from Agile methodology and iterative ballot processes.
        *   Da Vinci community members are seeing value; CRD functionality has reduced unnecessary PAs, reducing burden for providers/payers and improving patient experience.
    *   **Implementation Specifications to Support Electronic Prior Authorization Capabilities**
        *   Collectively, the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs support existing federal/state PA requirements and provide a path to more effective ePA.
        *   Phasing in these IGs is one way to improve the current PA process.
        *   HL7 and Da Vinci Project have a proven model for rapid standards improvement, allowing iterative improvements and incremental ePA adoption.
        *   ONC could leverage all or elements of these IGs as a "floor" for certified Health IT Module(s), providing minimum capabilities for those new to ePA and FHIR, while allowing leaders to pioneer advanced implementations.
        *   Existing regulations for ePA, though not widely adopted, mean some in the healthcare community have invested in current standards.
        *   The X12 278 Request and Response standard is required by HIPAA.
        *   The HL7 FHIR Da Vinci PAS IG includes ability to "translate" FHIR transactions to X12 278 and back to meet this requirement, leveraging FHIR efficiencies while honoring existing investments.
        *   The "translation" option is necessary given current HIPAA regulations and is supported by HL7 and X12.
        *   The PAS IG is structured so that if X12 278 requirement is removed, a FHIR-only transaction is possible without degrading IG integrity.
        *   This is demonstrated by the Da Vinci HIPAA Exception (until July 14, 2024), allowing payers/trading partners to use PAS IG without X12 278 translation.
        *   This further supports leveraging the IG now for an efficient current state, with a streamlined FHIR-only future state.
        *   This "translation" has been extensively tested in connectathons, indicating production readiness.
        *   The Agile process supports continuous improvement; May 2022 ballot includes enhancements for PAS IG, such as consistent error handling and leveraging CDex for immediate attachment needs.
        *   Concerns about the PAS IG:
            *   **Increased transaction numbers due to "translation":** The number of ePA transactions remains the same whether using PAS or X12 278; data simply transforms as needed, not increasing end-to-end transactions.
            *   **Role of intermediaries:** "Intermediary" in PAS IG can refer to a clearinghouse, but also a business associate, software service, or payer front-end. It is a step that accepts a FHIR bundle, translates it to/from X12 278, and returns a FHIR bundle.
        *   The HL7 FHIR Da Vinci PAS IG supports existing federal/state requirements, attachment sharing, and PA process transparency.
        *   It facilitates receiving payer responses (approval, denial reason, need for info) and querying for pending request updates (including reason for pending).
        *   This transparency assists providers by supplying information for successful PA submissions, reducing burden, and helps patients be informed partners in their care.
        *   Burden is also reduced on payers who don't manage unnecessary/incomplete PA requests or frequent inquiries.
    *   **Alternative Options and HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IG Readiness**
        *   Not a question of "either/or" regarding alternative IGs, base FHIR, or no FHIR standards, or only attachment standards.
        *   Leveraging Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs allows moving forward with FHIR standards to address primary PA burden drivers, while utilizing experience with existing standards like X12 278.
        *   It addresses the community's need for attachment standards.
        *   Given the IGs' ability to integrate these components, it's time to move forward and use policy levers to support all parties in adopting ePA with aligned requirements.
        *   Addressing one PA process component alone will not solve primary burden drivers; automation and workflow integration are necessary, recognizing multiple IT systems may be involved.
        *   FHIR and Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs support this, though more work is needed to detail IG leverage for certification.
        *   The Agile methodology and open ballot process allow efficient iteration, improvement, and learning from real-world experience.
        *   The IGs are vetted and tested, providing a good starting point, especially with incremental phasing and collaboration with ONC to articulate interaction sets for certification across multiple Health IT Modules.
*   The Da Vinci Project is committed to continued work with the healthcare community to incrementally advance these standards for the benefit of all patients, providers, payers, and vendors.
*   They look forward to discussing how to best phase in all or elements of these IGs as part of certified Health IT Module(s) in the ONC Health IT Certification Program.
*   Da Vinci Project leadership and IG development teams are eager to share expertise and support ONC in defining certification criteria.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The HL7 Da Vinci Project advocates for the adoption and certification of its FHIR-based Coverage Requirements Discovery (CRD), Documentation Templates and Rules (DTR), and Prior Authorization Support (PAS) Implementation Guides to automate and integrate electronic prior authorization, significantly reducing burden and improving patient care.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** HL7 Da Vinci Project
- **Type:** Organization
- **Role/Expertise:** Develops HL7 FHIR Implementation Guides focused on reducing administrative burden in healthcare, specifically for prior authorization. Jocelyn Keegan is the Program Manager for the HL7 Da Vinci Project.
- **Geographic Scope:** National/International (HL7 is a global standards organization; the Da Vinci Project focuses on U.S. healthcare burden).
- **Stake in Issue:** Their core mission is to create and implement FHIR-based solutions for healthcare interoperability and burden reduction; this RFI directly pertains to the adoption and certification of the specific IGs they have developed.
structuredSections.corePosition: We, the HL7 Da Vinci Project, strongly support leveraging our FHIR-based Burden Reduction Implementation Guides (CRD, DTR, PAS) to enable a certified Health IT Module or Modules within the ONC Health IT Certification Program. It is critical to integrate and automate the end-to-end prior authorization process using FHIR to address the significant burden on patients, providers, and payers, facilitating timely care and better outcomes.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- **Support leveraging HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs for ONC Health IT Certification Program.**
    - Facilitate workflow integration and automation of the end-to-end PA process.
- **Implement an incremental, phased approach for ePA adoption.**
    - Start with PA policies leveraging fully structured data and/or provider attestation.
    - This supports necessary business transformation from document-driven to data-driven.
- **Include specialty medications covered under the medical benefit in ePA requirements.**
- **Actively align requirements across providers, payers, and vendors.**
    - ONC and CMS should collaborate for consistent and complimentary requirements.
- **Include the first six functional capabilities outlined in the RFI for ePA certified Health IT Modules.**
    - These are foundational for effective ePA.
- **Exclude the seventh functional capability (digital signatures) from certified Health IT Module(s) requirements.**
    - Existing system controls (e.g., usernames, passwords, audit trails) make them unnecessary.
    - Including it could impede transformation to an optimized digital solution and promote documentation burden.
- **Retain the "translation" capability within the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci PAS IG.**
    - This is necessary to comply with current HIPAA regulations requiring the X12 278 standard.
    - Allows for leveraging FHIR efficiencies while respecting existing investments.
- **Recognize and utilize the HL7 Agile process for continuous improvement of IGs.**
    - Supports iterative improvement based on real-world experience and rapid advancement.
- **Collaborate with ONC to define how IGs can be phased into a certification program.**
    - Articulate interaction sets across multiple Health IT Modules to support the full ePA workflow for certification.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- **Payer effort in declaring data for PA decision-making in CQL:** This is a significant challenge, viewed more as a business transformation issue than a technology one.
- **Potential for increased documentation burden and delays if digital signatures are required:** Believe existing system controls are sufficient and digital signatures could hinder automation.
- **Compliance with existing X12 278 HIPAA regulations:** Requires a "translation" step in the PAS IG, which while supported, adds a layer compared to a pure FHIR transaction.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- **Quantitative evidence for ePA benefits:** Research by RTI for AHIP showed ePA can reduce PA decision time by approximately 69% (threefold).
- **Real-world impact of CRD functionality:** Members with CRD in place are experiencing reduced prior authorizations because providers can now ascertain when a PA is truly needed, preventing unnecessary submissions, thereby reducing burden for providers and payers and improving patient experience.
- **Transformative potential of ePA:** A key insight is the need to move beyond merely digitizing the current paper process to *transforming* it into an optimized digital solution for true automation and integration into clinical workflows.
- **Agile development for standards:** The Da Vinci Project emphasizes its Agile methodology for rapid standards improvement, allowing for iterative development, continuous refinement through ballot processes, and adaptation based on real-world experience.
- **HIPAA Exception for FHIR-only transactions:** The Da Vinci Project has secured a CMS-approved HIPAA Exception (through July 14, 2024) allowing use of the PAS IG without the X12 278 "translation," demonstrating the viability of a future FHIR-only approach.
- **Clarification on "translation" and "intermediaries":** The comment provides specific clarifications on common misunderstandings, asserting that the FHIR-to-X12-to-FHIR "translation" does not increase transaction numbers and that "intermediary" roles are flexible, not solely limited to clearinghouses.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Only in this way will patients also see the maximum benefit of a PA process embedded in a provider’s workflow, fully automated – the ability to have a meaningful conversation with their provider at the point of care about their options, facilitating more timely care and adherence to their care plan."
- "We believe it is important to move forward with ePA in a way that does not just digitize the current paper process but transforms it into an optimized digital solution."
- "Members that have CRD functionality in place are seeing reduced PAs because providers now know when a PA is needed and when it is not, meaning unnecessary PAs are not submitted."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The American Medical Association (AMA) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology's (ONC) Request for Information (RFI) regarding Electronic Prior Authorization (ePA) Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria.
*   The AMA agrees with ONC that diverse payer policies, provider workflow challenges, and technical barriers make the prior authorization (PA) process a source of burden for patients, providers, and payers, a cause of provider burnout, and a health risk for patients due to delayed care.
    *   The AMA acknowledges ONC's Strategy on Reducing Regulatory and Administrative Burden Relating to the Use of Health IT and EHRs, which recommends strengthening ePA processes.
    *   This includes leveraging health information technology (health IT) to standardize data and processes, coordinating new standards approaches, and incentivizing adoption of technology for standardized data exchange.
*   AMA's Annual Physician Surveys on PA Impact:
    *   Survey results are frequently cited in public forums and Health Information Technology Advisory Committee (HITAC) reports.
    *   2022 survey findings:
        *   93% of physicians report the PA process delays access to necessary patient care.
        *   82% of physicians report patients have abandoned treatment due to the PA process.
        *   Nearly a third of physicians report health plans rarely or never use evidence-based criteria in PA.
        *   91% of physicians report a negative impact on clinical outcomes due to the PA process.
        *   34% of physicians report PA has led to a serious adverse event for a patient in their care.
        *   Nearly 1 in 10 physicians report PA has led to patient disability/permanent bodily damage, congenital anomaly/birth defect, or death.
    *   The AMA believes the PA process clearly needs an overhaul.
*   Overarching Goal: PA Reform Beyond Automation
    *   More must be done to address the considerable burden and patient harm associated with the PA process.
    *   The AMA urges ONC to think broadly and promote comprehensive PA reform, not just view its role as a health IT certification body.
    *   Health IT is integral to health care delivery, so new standards or guides in ONC's Health IT Certification Program (Certification Program) should demonstrably improve the PA process.
    *   ONC should consider how standards will:
        *   Reduce PA volume, denials, and delays.
        *   Support targeted application of PA.
        *   Improve PA transparency.
        *   Protect continuity of patient care.
        *   Eliminate PA-related patient harm or death.
    *   Automation of PA processes and ePA alone will not accomplish these goals.
    *   The AMA agrees with the HITAC Intersection of Clinical and Administrative Data (ICAD) Task Force's 2020 recommendation that PA reform should be measurable, meaningful for all stakeholders, and have a significant impact across the entire process and range of stakeholders.
    *   PA reform requires an end-to-end evaluation of all proposed improvements and a well-orchestrated national approach.
    *   As ONC considers ePA, the AMA encourages evaluation of new Certification Program criteria through the lens of specific PA reform characteristics:
        *   How will new standards or implementation guides (IGs) assist in revising PA requirements, reviewing PA programs, and reducing PA volume?
        *   How will new standards or IGs support transparency and easy accessibility of PA requirements, criteria, rationale, and program changes for physicians and patients?
        *   How will new standards or IGs support the evolution of PA criteria based on physician input, especially for those in risk-based payment contracts?
        *   How will new standards or IGs protect continuity of care during patient treatment changes or health plan changes; minimize repetitive PA requirements; improve communication to facilitate continuity of care; and ensure patient health and safety?
    *   These characteristics reflect consensus from major healthcare provider organizations (AMA, American Hospital Association (AHA), American Pharmacists Association (APhA), Medical Group Management Association (MGMA)) and health plans (America's Health Insurance Plans (AHIP), BlueCross BlueShield Association (BCBSA)).
    *   ONC's Certification Program must leverage certified health IT to achieve comprehensive PA reform across all these critical areas, not just PA automation.
*   Specific considerations for health IT standards and guidelines:
    *   ONC should evaluate the "readiness" of all Health Level 7 (HL7) IGs and standards before including them in its Certification Program.
        *   Readiness means IGs and standards are tested in real-world environments (not just Connectathons or limited testing).
        *   Testing should occur across all provider types, specialties, sizes, and resource levels.
        *   IGs must account for semantic and syntactic interoperability between certified and non-certified health IT products.
        *   Formalized reports of Connectathon testing should be reviewed, focusing on real-world aspects.
    *   ONC should ensure IGs and standards are certifiable and health IT can be tested for conformance that ensures end-to-end interoperability.
        *   Current Certification Program testing for typical federal program compliance does not account for PA process complexity and variability.
        *   ONC should consider how its Certification Program will translate ePA standards and IG testing to support end-to-end PA process improvements.
        *   Successful ePA interoperability requires testing of health IT modules used by payers, clearinghouses, or other intermediaries, which are not typically required to use certified health IT.
*   Health IT standards and IG readiness:
    *   The AMA is concerned about CMS proposing to require physicians to use ePA in the near term, as anticipated in a revised PA proposed rule later this year.
    *   The AMA previously urged CMS in 2020 to pilot test ePA standards, review industry findings (including small/solo practices), and address lessons learned/gaps before codifying standards.
    *   These recommendations align with ONC's Strategy to work with stakeholders to support pilots for standardized electronic ordering and accelerate adoption.
    *   The AMA agrees with ONC's RFI that the Coverage Requirements Discovery (CRD), Documentation Templates and Coverage Rules (DTR), and Prior Authorization Support (PAS) IGs lack maturity and extensive production testing.
    *   Connectathon testing:
        *   Accounts for the vast majority of Da Vinci IG testing.
        *   Relies on "happy-path" scripted scenarios and predefined input data.
        *   Excludes exception handling, system ability to handle incorrect values, and validation errors.
        *   Does not test for or duplicate real-world conditions.
    *   Data privacy concerns:
        *   Several Da Vinci workgroups have only recently started considering data controls to protect patient data.
        *   Da Vinci IGs currently allow broad payer access to patient information without exception.
        *   Workgroup members had not considered limiting automatic disclosure of Electronic Health Information (EHI) for self-pay patients who have a HIPAA right to withhold data from their insurance.
        *   Some HL7 standards (e.g., CDS Hooks) needed for ePA lack controls limiting payer access.
        *   Debate exists on sharing patient EHI with payers before a service is even ordered; this is described as "payer eavesdropping."
        *   Physicians and patients should not have to choose between ePA automation and privacy rights.
        *   Payer leverage and funding in Da Vinci IG development is a major factor to consider.
    *   More testing and refinement of ePA standards and IGs are necessary.
    *   The AMA cautions against requiring ePA use to "work out the kinks" by codifying it in regulation now.
        *   This would shift industry focus to federal program compliance instead of PA reform.
        *   Standards development would focus on federal requirements, potentially "locking" ePA implementation specifications and standards, leading to forward and backward compatibility issues (similar to the Meaningful Use Program).
    *   To promote ePA standards maturity, the AMA recommends ONC coordinate with CMS in developing and promoting real-world PA testing programs:
        *   Identify specific and targeted PA scenarios where ePA would be most beneficial, after first eliminating PA requirements for routinely approved (low-value) PAs.
            *   Scenarios should include: services with highest PA request volume, urgent services, and services with high claim denial rates due to unmet PA requirements (where CRD IG could improve transparency).
            *   Include clinical documents/electronic attachment standards to reduce administrative burdens.
        *   Consider methods to positively incentivize scenario testing across healthcare provider and health plan trading partners (intermediaries, clearinghouses, pharmacy systems, certified health IT, revenue cycle, and practice management system (PMS) vendors).
            *   ONC could establish a voluntary ePA testing program (similar to pediatric care certification).
            *   CMS could promote physician participation through incentives like reporting program (e.g., Promoting Interoperability program) bonus points or optional measures.
    *   Additional considerations for Da Vinci IGs:
        *   ONC should request and review status updates from Da Vinci participants/pilot testers, including detailed Connectathon report-outs.
        *   The IGs are currently "in flux" (out for ballot, comments due, adjudication pending), making evaluation difficult as they will change. This necessitates additional time before inclusion in health IT certification or CMS program requirements.
        *   The IGs are Standards for Trial Use version 2 (STU2) and not yet normative, meaning they will continue to change, potentially with "breaking" changes causing backward compatibility issues.
        *   More real-world testing is needed in physician practices of all sizes and specialties to ensure technology functions well beyond "happy-path" demonstrations.
        *   The HITAC ePA RFI Task Group (or similar group) should continue to meet regularly to evaluate IG status, pilot results, and readiness for widespread implementation/certification.
        *   CDS Hooks, a nascent specification, has ongoing issues related to CRD triggers, particularly concerning patient EHI privacy and security (e.g., ensuring payers only receive minimum necessary info for a specific PA, not triggering for self-pay services).
*   Gaps in health IT testing needed to support ePA:
    *   PA processes extend beyond certified electronic health record technology (CEHRT) systems, often involving separate EHR, revenue cycle, and PMS (which are typically not certified).
    *   Da Vinci IGs are not granular enough for certification criteria involving multiple physician-side health IT systems.
    *   Requiring EHRs to adopt ePA certification criteria only addresses a portion of the PA process.
    *   Healthcare organizations interact with many trading partners (intermediaries, clearinghouses) to exchange PA information.
    *   CMS has oversight of health plans (e.g., Medicare Advantage) but does not require them to use health IT systems tested and certified to conform with Da Vinci IG requirements.
    *   Intermediaries and clearinghouses are not obligated to adopt Da Vinci IGs.
    *   Sporadic use across the PA process could increase administrative burden and patient harm.
    *   Lack of PA process-wide adoption, testing, and conformance is a critical gap before mandating ePA use.
    *   ONC's Certification Program (pass/fail paradigm) tests to program criteria, but Da Vinci IGs are not easily used to demonstrate conformance or test procedures due to complexity.
    *   It is unclear if ONC-Authorized Certification Bodies (ONC-ACBs) and Testing Labs (ONC-ATLs) can test ePA workflows or support post-certification reporting given the vast array of information exchange, variability, and uniqueness of thousands of national PA processes.
    *   Da Vinci IGs are not sufficiently mature or granular to support typical PA process testing and certification.
    *   Prior to including Da Vinci IGs in certified health IT and CMS requiring physician ePA participation, ONC and CMS should address:
        *   How gaps between certified and non-certified health IT (like PMS) will be addressed.
        *   How gaps in Da Vinci IG adoption and conformance will be monitored across payers and health plans.
        *   How FHIR-based API uniformity will be assured across all payers and PA programs.
        *   The role federal regulators can play in encouraging/requiring all health plan trading partners to use Da Vinci IGs consistently; if regulations are insufficient, what federal legislation is needed for intermediaries/clearinghouses to adopt certified health IT for ePA.
        *   The process for translating HL7 Da Vinci IGs into ONC pre-certification testing and post-certification reporting requirements, informed by HL7 workgroup analysis, Connectathon reports, vendor experiences, independent evaluations, and real-world pilot testing.
        *   How Da Vinci IGs will address payer goldcarding programs to communicate PA waivers/exemptions at the point of ordering.
*   The AMA reiterates support for ONC and CMS efforts to reduce PA burden. ePA can help, but it is not a substitute for PA process reform.
*   ONC and CMS have important roles, including identifying targeted ePA uses and pilot testing.
*   Requiring ePA too soon will worsen the PA process, increase burden, and lead to more patient harm if insufficiently tested standards cause errors, lost requests, and care delays.
*   ePA IGs and standards must be consistently utilized across all stakeholders, not just those using certified health IT or under CMS oversight. Testing must validate strict conformance.
*   **Request for Information: Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria - Appendix 1**
    *   **Functional Capabilities for Electronic Prior Authorization in Certified Health IT (87 FR 3477)**
        *   The AMA appreciates ONC's interest in ePA functional capabilities.
        *   ONC should consider ePA criteria that improve the overall PA process beyond simple automation.
        *   In addition to ONC's listed capabilities, health IT certification criteria should promote:
            *   Payer functional capabilities robust enough to convey comprehensive documentation requirements upfront, making subsequent requests for info the exception.
            *   Triggering of all medical/prescription PA requests at the physician or designated staff discretion.
            *   Ability for care providers to choose preferred ePA system(s), apps, or solutions based on workflow.
            *   Support for reviewing routine PA submissions/rules that are typically approved, and a "trust and verify" framework (gold carding).
            *   IGs supporting PA renewal and appeal processes, not just initial PAs.
            *   IGs supporting capture of payer data and public reporting metrics (e.g., denial percentage).
            *   Ability for physicians, at the time of ordering, to identify if a plan's PA requirement for a service is waived due to gold carding.
            *   EHR development to consider data privacy and security protections concurrently with functional capabilities, upholding minimum necessary principles.
            *   Certified health IT systems ensuring APIs only send data needed for a particular PA request (vs. exposing entire patient record) and allowing clinicians to "shut off" the system if a patient self-pays.
            *   Payers requesting supplemental info must identify its intended use and the goal of the PA.
            *   IGs supporting detailed descriptions of payer predefined rules for approval, including required data.
            *   IGs supporting granular and actionable info on PA denial reasons, needed additional data, and appeal instructions.
            *   IGs enabling collection of clinical/administrative documentation for PA from appropriate source health IT system(s) to minimize manual entry/re-entry.
    *   **Appropriateness of HL7 FHIR Da Vinci IGs (CRD, DTR, PAS) (87 FR 3478)**
        *   The AMA actively participates in HL7 Da Vinci workgroups and monitors Connectathons for CRD, DTR, and PAS IGs.
        *   The AMA agrees with ONC's RFI that these IGs lack maturity and extensive production testing.
        *   Connectathon testing relies on "happy-path" scripted scenarios, excluding real-world exceptions.
        *   Data privacy concerns remain, as IGs allow broad payer access without sufficient patient data controls.
            *   Concerns exist about payers accessing data for self-pay patients, violating HIPAA rights.
            *   Some HL7 standards (e.g., CDS Hooks) lack controls limiting payer access.
            *   "Payer eavesdropping" describes how IGs may enable payers to monitor physician workflows and capture patient data before an order is signed.
            *   Payer leverage in IG development is a major factor.
        *   More testing and refinement are necessary.
        *   The AMA cautions against requiring ePA use to "work out the kinks" by codifying it in regulation now, as this would shift industry focus from PA reform to compliance, potentially locking standards and causing compatibility issues.
        *   To address concerns and promote maturity, the AMA recommends ONC coordinate with CMS on real-world PA testing programs:
            *   Identify specific, targeted PA scenarios (high volume, urgent, frequent denials) where ePA is most beneficial, after eliminating low-value PAs.
            *   Positively incentivize scenario testing across all trading partners (providers, payers, intermediaries, clearinghouses).
            *   Consider a voluntary ePA testing program (similar to pediatric care) and CMS incentives (bonus points, optional measures).
        *   Additional considerations for Da Vinci IGs:
            *   ONC should request and review status updates and detailed Connectathon report-outs from Da Vinci participants/pilot testers.
            *   The IGs are in flux (out for ballot, comments pending adjudication), making evaluation challenging; this necessitates more time before inclusion.
            *   The IGs are STU2, not yet normative, and will continue to change, potentially with "breaking" changes.
            *   More real-world testing is needed in diverse physician practices.
            *   The HITAC ePA RFI Task Group should continue to meet regularly to evaluate IG status and readiness.
    *   **Additional Approaches to Support Electronic Prior Authorization: Healthcare Attachments (87 FR 3479)**
        *   Proper review of HL7 C-CDA and FHIR standards requires an update on current use and capabilities for PA.
        *   Recommend ONC survey C-CDA and FHIR attachment use, analyzing pros and cons.
        *   Recommend ONC survey payers to determine how many PA rules/criteria sets are mapped to C-CDA or FHIR attachments, ensuring document-based approaches meet actual payer information needs.
        *   The AMA does not support supporting multiple attachment standards due to the incredible burden and expense for physicians (e.g., FHIR Da Vinci for some, C-CDA for others).
    *   **General Request for Comments (87 FR 3480)**
        *   Repeats initial statements on PA burden, AMA survey findings, and overarching goal of PA reform.
        *   Reiterates concerns about the current PA process (delays, abandonment, lack of evidence-based criteria, negative clinical outcomes, serious adverse events/death).
        *   Reaffirms the need for ONC to view health IT holistically within PA reform, focusing on reducing volume, denials, and delays, supporting targeted PA, improving transparency, protecting continuity of care, and eliminating harm.
        *   Reiterates the consensus among major healthcare organizations on PA reform characteristics.
    *   **Certified Health IT Functionality (87 FR 3480)**
        *   The PA process extends beyond CEHRT systems within medical practices, often involving non-certified systems like PMS.
        *   Da Vinci IGs lack granularity for certification where multiple health IT systems are involved on the physician side.
        *   Requiring EHRs to adopt ePA criteria only addresses part of the PA process.
        *   Healthcare organizations interact with intermediaries and clearinghouses, which are not obligated to adopt Da Vinci IGs.
        *   Sporadic IG use will increase burden and harm patients.
        *   The lack of process-wide adoption, testing, and conformance is a critical gap.
        *   ONC's Certification Program and testing bodies (ONC-ACBs, ONC-ATLs) may not be capable of testing complex ePA workflows, given the vast variability.
        *   Da Vinci IGs are not mature or granular enough for typical PA process testing.
        *   Reiterates the questions ONC and CMS should address before mandating ePA use (gaps between certified/non-certified IT, IG adoption/conformance across payers, FHIR API uniformity, role of federal regulators for trading partners, IG translation to testing requirements, goldcarding).
    *   **Certified Health IT Functionality - HIPAA compliant workflow (87 FR 3480)**
        *   ONC's Certification Program should support the complete PA workflow, including translation from FHIR to X12 standard if X12 278 remains required for HIPAA.
        *   Requiring X12/FHIR translation adds costs and invites errors.
        *   ONC should request a comprehensive status update on FHIR-to-FHIR pilots (granted HIPAA exceptions) and compare them with X12/FHIR translation.
    *   **Certified Health IT Functionality - Structure of certification criteria (87 FR 3480)**
        *   The AMA strongly recommends incorporating all PA functional capabilities into a single module.
        *   Physicians need assurance that EHRs support a seamless, "soup-to-nuts" automated PA process without relying on ancillary components.
        *   It is unacceptable to assume physicians will "mix and match" modules.
        *   A single module minimizes payer variation, confusion, costs, and integration complexity.
        *   ONC certification criteria should require health IT vendors to provide an interoperable PA experience across vendors for full end-to-end ePA functionality.
    *   **Implementation Specifications for Prior Authorization - Readiness of Da Vinci IGs (87 FR 3480)**
        *   Repeats arguments about Da Vinci IGs' immaturity, reliance on "happy-path" testing, lack of real-world production testing, and data privacy concerns ("payer eavesdropping").
        *   Reiterates the caution against codifying ePA prematurely and the recommendation for real-world testing programs with specific scenarios and incentives.
        *   Re-emphasizes that IGs are in flux (ballot cycle, STU2 status) and need more time, and that the HITAC ePA RFI Task Group should continue its oversight.
    *   **Implementation Specifications for Prior Authorization - If not ready, propose criteria now? (87 FR 3480)**
        *   IGs suggest a path to full automation.
        *   The AMA recommends a phased, iterative approach based on IG maturity and industry compliance speed (determined by ONC environmental scan).
        *   ePA success requires all stakeholders to have functional criteria to share accurate and complete information.
        *   AMA recommends payer certification as necessary for ePA success and PA reform.
        *   Proprietary capabilities and APIs should be avoided and discouraged.
    *   **Implementation Specifications for Prior Authorization - How to handle future integrations (87 FR 3480)**
        *   Da Vinci IGs are in flux, making inclusion in certification difficult.
        *   Suggests an ongoing role for the HITAC ePA RFI Task Group for regular "check-ins" on IG maturity.
        *   The AMA does not support adopting a base standard as an initial step. Use-case-specific IGs (like Da Vinci) are necessary for uniformity.
        *   Adopting a general base standard will not support uniform development and implementation across providers, plans, and vendors.
    *   **Implementation Specifications for Prior Authorization - Legal/compliance requirements (87 FR 3480)**
        *   Repeats arguments about PA processes occurring outside CEHRT systems (PMS, etc.), lack of granularity in Da Vinci IGs, and the issue of non-certified trading partners.
        *   Reiterates the questions ONC and CMS should address (gaps in IG use/adoption, API uniformity, federal role for trading partners, IG translation to testing requirements).
    *   **Implementation Specifications for Prior Authorization - Alternative approaches (87 FR 3480)**
        *   The AMA recommends ONC adopt the NCPDP SCRIPT ePA transactions for prescription drugs as part of mandatory certification requirements.
    *   **Implementation Specifications for Prior Authorization - New IGs needed (87 FR 3480)**
        *   ONC should consider the need for patient transparency, including price and prior authorizations, as a gap in current standards development.
        *   This could be a complementary IG with separate certification/implementation.
        *   At minimum, the Patient Access API could provide PA status to patients.
        *   Reiterates that patient participation in PA process should be voluntary.
    *   **Healthcare Attachment Standards - C-CDA Attachments IG (87 FR 3481)**
        *   Proper review requires up-to-date info on current C-CDA use for PA.
        *   Recommend ONC survey C-CDA and FHIR attachment use, analyzing pros and cons.
        *   ONC should seek info from health plans on sufficiency of C-CDA and FHIR attachments for specific service PA requests.
        *   The AMA does not support multiple attachment standards due to burden and expense for physicians.
    *   **Healthcare Attachment Standards - Feasible timeline (87 FR 3481)**
        *   CDA Attachments IG or FHIR Documents must be mature and sufficiently tested before widespread adoption.
        *   ONC and CMS could consider a "soft" timeline based on quantitative testing.
        *   AMA encourages ONC to develop a proving ground for IG maturity and phased criteria, with independently evaluated and publicly reported testing results.
    *   **Healthcare Attachment Standards - Accommodate improvements over time (87 FR 3481)**
        *   Allowing multiple approaches is concerning, as different payers could require different document types via contracting.
        *   This is not sustainable, especially for smaller practices, and would be more expensive for the industry.
    *   **Healthcare Attachment Standards - Support for attachments transactions alone? (87 FR 3481)**
        *   ePA workflow is complex and spans multiple health IT solutions; a single solution like EHRs cannot support all workflows.
        *   ONC should ensure all relevant actors adopt necessary standards.
        *   ONC's Certification Program should assure physicians that all ePA components, including attachments, interoperate.
        *   An iterative approach is needed for adoption and maturity of a fully functional PA workflow.
        *   Physicians need a unified attachment process, not one specific to PA or claims.
        *   An attachment standard alone only addresses transport of clinical data and does not expose PA requirements in EHR workflows or communicate required supporting info, which are critical for automated PA.
    *   **Impact on Patients - Positive impact of ePA (87 FR 3481)**
        *   Standardized, end-to-end ePA may prevent care delays.
        *   Exposing PA requirements in EHRs at the point of care could support informed physician-patient conversations and ensure PA initiation when care is scheduled.
        *   Clear documentation identification and automated exchange may reduce care delays.
        *   Improving PA can prevent patients from abandoning treatment, which negatively impacts clinical outcomes (as shown by AMA survey).
        *   Ideally, PA data sent to payers should be codified to minimize human review.
        *   Patient-centric metrics (time to care, treatment abandonment) should be included in pilot evaluations.
        *   Patient data privacy and security must be maintained:
            *   IGs should limit, and certification should validate, patient data use to a particular PA service only.
            *   Data accessed for PA should not be used for other purposes.
            *   IGs should safeguard against overexposure of patient health records.
            *   HIPAA rights of self-pay patients not wanting EHI disclosed must be protected.
            *   Over-sharing EHI is distressing and erodes trust.
        *   Any certified technology must be affordable and accessible to physicians in all locations, settings, sizes, and resource levels to avoid exacerbating health disparities.
    *   **Impact on Patients - Reduce time, non-adherence, out-of-pocket costs (87 FR 3481)**
        *   Exposing PA requirements in EHR workflow supports informed conversations and ensures necessary data collection during visits.
        *   Assumptions that Da Vinci IGs will reduce PA processing time, treatment abandonment, and denials need validation.
        *   ONC should require patient-focused ePA piloting and testing to ensure these goals are achieved.
    *   **Impact on Patients - Additional functionality for patient participation (87 FR 3481)**
        *   Patients should not be required to participate in the PA process, as this could increase denials if they cannot comply with documentation requirements.
        *   Potential for conflicting data submissions from physicians and patients could impact timeliness and outcomes.
        *   Patients should, however, be able to opt-in to receiving updates on in-process PAs.
    *   **Impact on Providers - Reduce burden for healthcare providers (87 FR 3481)**
        *   2021 AMA survey data:
            *   Average 41 PAs/physician/week.
            *   Weekly PA workload consumes almost two business days of physician and staff time.
            *   88% of physicians report high or extremely high PA burdens.
            *   40% of physicians hired staff exclusively for PAs.
        *   2021 CAQH Index estimates $437 million annual medical industry savings from fully automated ePA ($87M for plans, $350M for providers) based on X12 278.
            *   Providers could save 16 minutes per transaction (23 min manual vs. 7 min electronic).
            *   CAQH data doesn't account for initial technology investment or electronic attachment savings.
        *   Caveats:
            *   Automation is only one part of fixing PA; overall volume of PA for drugs/services must be addressed.
            *   Digitizing current PA rules will take years; manageable workload needed for developers.
            *   Certification requirements must not impose undue financial burdens on smaller practices/payers, especially during a pandemic, to avoid exacerbating health disparities.
            *   EHR privacy and security issues for physicians: confidence that payers only access relevant EHR parts for PA.
            *   Concerns extend to non-physician providers (e.g., DME suppliers without EHRs) and complex multi-provider PA workflows.
            *   ONC must ensure technology allows "pass-offs" between physicians and staff (e.g., saving/delegating PAs) to avoid increasing physician administrative tasks.
        *   Available cost savings estimates do not address Da Vinci IGs (only X12 278). No info on initial technology investment costs for FHIR-based ePA.
        *   AMA recommends ONC require pilot sites/early implementers to publicly report ROI metrics (time spent, time to care, electronic PA % capacity, cost savings, direct/indirect costs) for various implementation scenarios.
    *   **Impact on Providers - Use across patient panels; incentives (87 FR 3481)**
        *   Physicians will need positive incentives given significant cost and time (training, workflow alteration) to adopt ePA.
        *   Many physicians lack sophisticated software for FHIR and may need intermediary services.
        *   Additional support needed for staff to capture patient info per Da Vinci IG requirements (e.g., free text EHR fields vs. FHIR-enabled resources).
