            *   If providers had more specific information when a denial is generated, it would hold health plans accountable (e.g., denied but information was already provided) and help providers target the missing information rather than blindly engaging in peer-to-peer discussions or writing lengthy letters of medical necessity.
    *   4. Automation Opportunity:
        *   We experience authorization automation for other lines of service, particularly diagnostic imaging/radiology.
            *   In this space, providers leverage nationally recognized, pre-order decision support technology, select a test to be performed, and are scored on the clinical appropriateness of the exam for the patient’s condition (i.e., diagnosis code).
            *   The presence of this decision support tool is being leveraged to replace redundant prior authorizations where the same or similar information is entered into a health plan/vendor portal for authorization and review.
        *   Similar to radiology services, DFCI providers have decision support tools that aid in oncology treatment selections.
        *   However, we have yet to see the industry automate authorization processes in this service line.
        *   DFCI asks ONC to consider requiring that all health plans and vendors allow for automated authorization submissions across service lines, in addition to standardizing the criteria that providers can leverage.
    *   5. Technology Limitations:
        *   a. Access for Out of Network Providers:
            *   Please consider allowing out of network providers access to health plan technologies (e.g., online submission portals).
            *   Many health plans only allow in-network providers to leverage online technologies.
            *   As a highly specialized regional cancer center, DFCI encounters patients going out of their network to be seen here.
            *   In doing so, DFCI cannot submit electronic prior authorization requests.
            *   When this technology is not available, teams must utilize antiquated communication methods (e.g., phone or fax).
            *   This creates delays in authorization reviews by the health plans.
            *   Accessing modern authorization portals is critical to improving prior authorization turnaround times.
        *   b. Technology Downtime Guidelines:
            *   Consider a consistent methodology for managing prior authorizations when established technologies cannot be leveraged (e.g., system outages).
            *   It is not always clear how an authorization should be submitted when preferred technologies are down.
            *   Establishing a standardized method would clarify any uncertainty providers have when authorization requests cannot be submitted through normal channels.
    *   6. Vendor-Health plan Relationships:
        *   Frequently, health plans have assigned medical/utilization management to a third-party vendor.
        *   DFCI does encounter instances where the responsibilities of the health plan and the vendor are unclear.
        *   Providers are left to navigate these complex relationships when attempting to secure an authorization, which often leads to delays.
        *   To minimize administrative delays, it is critical that all parties have a clear understanding of the roles and responsibilities of these vendors and their relationship to the plan.
    *   7. Benefits Limitations:
        *   a. Out of Network Services:
            *   DFCI is requesting health plans evaluate a patient’s benefit structure when reviewing an authorization request.
            *   DFCI encounters health plans that will authorize treatments for patients who do not have benefits for the service.
            *   While we verify benefits before submitting authorization requests, we ask that health plans ensure patients have benefits for a treatment before issuing authorizations.
        *   b. Transplant Services:
            *   DFCI is requesting the ability to confirm patient’s transplant benefits via a portal, including lodging, dental, and donor services.
    *   8. Payer-preference restrictions:
        *   Health plans have deployed three major payer-preference restrictions that impact DFCI patients: product preferences (e.g., biosimilars), sourcing preferences (e.g., pharmacy benefit managers (PBM) restrictions), and rendering location preferences (e.g., site of care).
        *   While we can appreciate the cost-savings these preferences can provide, payer-preferences are challenging for providers and authorization staff.
        *   Frequently we are left to investigate alternate treatment options for patients with little guidance from the health plan; ultimately delaying patient care.
        *   Can ONC consider the impact of payer-preference restrictions in their review of prior authorizations?
        *   It would be helpful to establish standard criteria on payer-preferred restrictions and include appropriate health plan support when services are restricted.
    *   9. Patient support/Case management:
        *   Health plans inconsistently utilize case management teams to support patients.
        *   When provided, health plan employed case management teams are a valuable resource to patients and the care team.
        *   They help patients understand the coverage for their treatments, benefit restrictions, authorization denials issued by the plan, or out-of-pocket costs being applied.
        *   When health plans require preferred products or site of service restrictions, case management teams can help patients and providers select adequate locations for patients to receive care.
        *   Unfortunately, many health plans do not provide case management services, placing the onus on patients and providers to interpret health plan policies and ensure care is covered.
        *   Please consider establishing a defined set of services that require health plan case management.
    *   10. Emerging and New to Market Therapies:
        *   Standards should be established to support emerging therapies and new to market drugs or treatments.
        *   Health plans are inconsistent in how they manage authorizations for new to market therapies.
        *   Some plans support an authorization process for new treatments.
        *   However, DFCI frequently experiences health plans do not have criteria to manage the utilization review of these services, and in some cases, place a moratorium on coverage.
        *   The variation in coverage and authorization for new to market therapies creates confusion and has the potential to delay patient care.
    *   11. Radiation oncology:
        *   DFCI has an incredibly low rate of clinical prior authorization denials for radiation oncology services.
        *   However, health plans continue to tightly manage this service and frequently deny claims despite authorizations being in place.
        *   This only increases a provider’s burden to received adequate reimbursement.
        *   Additionally, decision support tools are frequently deployed to ensure appropriate ordering occurs.
        *   Please consider leveraging this information and or providing relief to providers leveraging these tools.
    *   12. Post-claim Denials:
        *   a. Authorization-related denials:
            *   Frequently, health plans deny claims for not being pre-authorized despite the provider having an authorization on file.
            *   Providers must manually communicate (e.g., telephone call) authorization numbers to the plan for claims to be reprocessed and ultimately paid.
            *   Please consider reviewing reimbursement workflows for services requiring authorization.
            *   It would be interesting to understand how frequently payers deny a service because of an authorization “issue,” only for it to be re-processed and paid.
            *   This review might help inform decisions that improve this process such as reduced claim edits for authorized services, and/or support incentive-based authorization relief programs.
        *   b. Medical necessity-related denials:
            *   Health plans also deny claims for medical necessity despite the resource-intensive pre-service authorization reviews completed by both parties.
            *   Consistent with post-claim authorization-related denials mentioned above, providers must manually communicate (e.g., telephone call) authorization numbers.
            *   We frequently need to restate clinical information supplied during the authorization process.
            *   These denials also require more costly clinical resources to ensure the denials are appropriately addressed.
            *   Please also consider medical necessity related denials when reviewing reimbursement workflows.
            *   It becomes difficult to justify additional clinical resources when services were clinically reviewed and authorized pre-service.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, a comprehensive cancer center, urges ONC to implement performance-based prior authorization standards, mandate automation, and address myriad complexities and inconsistencies in health plan requirements to reduce administrative burdens and prevent delays in patient care.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Dana-Farber Cancer Institute
- **Type:** Organization
- **Role/Expertise:** Michael L. Reney, Executive Vice President, Chief Finance & Business Strategy Officer; an NCI-designated comprehensive cancer center.
- **Geographic Scope:** National (based in Boston, MA, but serves patients nationally as a specialized cancer center)
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly impacted by administrative complexities and delays in health plan prior authorization requirements for cancer treatments, which divert staff from patient care and can lead to adverse medical events for patients.
structuredSections.corePosition: As a comprehensive cancer center, we are significantly burdened by the expanding and inconsistent prior authorization requirements from health plans, which cause delays in critical patient care and pull our clinical staff away from their primary duties. We advocate for streamlined, standardized, and automated prior authorization processes, including performance-based relief for providers, clearer decision guidelines, and improved technology access for all providers, to ensure timely and appropriate treatment for patients.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Implement performance-based standards that reduce or eliminate prior authorization requirements for providers who demonstrate appropriate treatment patterns (e.g., <1% denial rate).
- Establish standardized prior authorization performance metrics for providers (e.g., approval/denial ratings, peer-to-peer overturn rates) to enable understanding of performance and leverage for authorization relief.
- Develop consistent prior authorization decision guidelines across health plans for:
    - Approvals: Standardize what is authorized (e.g., entire regimens vs. specific drugs) and how "units" are authorized (e.g., visit basis vs. billing units).
    - Denials: Require specific information for denials beyond generic statements to enable targeted provider responses and accountability.
- Require all health plans and vendors to allow automated authorization submissions across all service lines, similar to existing solutions for diagnostic imaging/radiology, and standardize the criteria providers can leverage.
- Address technology limitations by:
    - Allowing out-of-network providers access to health plan online submission portals.
    - Establishing a consistent methodology for managing prior authorizations during technology downtimes (e.g., system outages).
- Clarify roles and responsibilities between health plans and third-party utilization management vendors to minimize administrative delays.
- Improve benefits limitations processes by:
    - Requiring health plans to evaluate a patient’s benefit structure and ensure benefits exist before issuing authorizations.
    - Providing portal access to confirm patient's transplant benefits, including lodging, dental, and donor services.
- Consider the impact of payer-preference restrictions (e.g., biosimilars, PBM restrictions, site of care) and establish standard criteria with appropriate health plan support when services are restricted.
- Establish a defined set of services that require health plan case management to support patients and providers in understanding coverage and navigating restrictions.
- Create standards to support emerging and new-to-market therapies, ensuring consistent authorization processes and coverage criteria across health plans.
- Leverage existing decision support tools and low denial rates in radiation oncology to provide administrative relief to providers.
- Review reimbursement workflows to address post-claim denials for services that were pre-authorized:
    - Reduce claim edits for authorized services.
    - Support incentive-based authorization relief programs based on low denial rates.
    - Prevent the need for providers to manually re-communicate authorization details or clinical information.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Administrative complexities of health plan prior authorization requirements for cancer drugs have significantly increased.
    - Prior authorization is now required for all drugs, including established first-line, low-cost treatments, not just high-dollar oncology drugs.
    - This expansion forces administrative and clinical staff to interact with health plans, pulling providers away from direct patient care.
- Complexity of health plans’ prior authorization programs includes:
    - Needing to understand unpublished employer group-specific drug policies.
    - Navigating complex health plan-prior authorization vendor relationships.
    - Coordinating retail pharmacy drug shipments before patients can start time-sensitive treatments.
- Barriers frequently lead to:
    - Delays in patient care.
    - Miscommunications from health plans.
    - Adverse medical events requiring complex medical interventions (e.g., hospital admissions).
- Lack of clarity and consistency in prior authorization decision guidelines:
    - Inconsistent authorization of entire regimens versus specific drugs and varying "units" of authorization, leading to financial exposure for providers.
    - Generic denial responses from health plans (e.g., “The service did not meet medical policy”) without specific reasons, necessitating blind engagement in peer-to-peer discussions or lengthy letters of medical necessity.
- Lack of automation for oncology authorization processes, despite existing decision support tools for treatment selection, unlike other service lines (e.g., diagnostic imaging/radiology).
- Technology limitations, specifically out-of-network providers lacking access to online submission portals, forcing use of antiquated methods (phone/fax) and causing delays.
- Uncertainty regarding prior authorization submission methods during technology downtimes/system outages.
- Unclear responsibilities between health plans and third-party utilization management vendors, creating navigation challenges and delays for providers.
- Health plans authorizing treatments for patients who do not have benefits for the service, despite provider benefit verification efforts.
- Challenging payer-preference restrictions (product, sourcing, rendering location preferences) that delay patient care by requiring investigation of alternate options with little health plan guidance.
- Inconsistent provision of patient support/case management by health plans, placing the burden of interpreting policies and ensuring coverage on patients and providers.
- Inconsistent and often lacking criteria for managing authorizations for emerging and new-to-market therapies, leading to confusion, coverage moratoriums, and potential delays in patient care.
- Despite low prior authorization denial rates for radiation oncology services, health plans continue to tightly manage and frequently deny claims for these services, increasing provider burden for reimbursement.
- Post-claim denials are common even when prior authorization is on file or pre-service medical necessity reviews were completed, requiring manual re-communication of information and costly clinical resources.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- The prior authorization team at Dana-Farber Cancer Institute has "grown substantially" because health plans now require authorization for all drugs, including established first-line, low-cost treatments, where previously only high-dollar oncology drugs required it.
- Encountering administrative barriers can lead to "adverse medical events requiring complex medical interventions (e.g., hospital admissions)" due to delays in starting time-sensitive treatments.
- DFCI faces unique challenges in understanding "unpublished employer group-specific drug policies" and navigating "complex health plan-prior authorization vendor relationships."
- Despite having decision support tools for oncology treatment selections, the industry has not automated authorization processes for this critical service line, unlike diagnostic imaging/radiology where pre-order decision support technology is leveraged to replace redundant prior authorizations.
- Out-of-network providers (like DFCI for some patients) are often denied access to health plan online submission portals, forcing them to rely on "antiquated communication methods (e.g., phone or fax)," which creates significant delays.
- A notable recurring issue is health plans denying claims for services not being pre-authorized, even when the provider has an authorization on file, requiring manual communication of authorization numbers for reprocessing.
- Similarly, claims are often denied for medical necessity despite resource-intensive pre-service authorization reviews, requiring costly clinical resources to re-state clinical information and address the denials.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "These requirements pull our providers away from the bedsides and from the lifesaving care they have been trained to give."
- "Too frequently DFCI encounters these barriers, and the results can lead to delays in care, miscommunications from the health plan, and in some cases adverse medical events requiring complex medical interventions (e.g., hospital admissions)."
- "It becomes difficult to justify additional clinical resources when services were clinically reviewed and authorized pre-service."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology’s (ONC) Request for Information on Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria.
*   While the Academy acknowledges the intention of prior authorization (PA) requirements set by payers to control costs and ensure payment accuracy, we echo concerns that variable and inconsistent payer policies, provider workflow challenges, and technical barriers create a burdensome environment.
    *   This burden affects patients, providers, and payers.
    *   It is a cause of burnout for providers.
    *   It is a health risk for patients when it delays their care.
*   Current standards for electronic prior authorization (ePA) do not work as intended for pediatrics.
    *   The pediatric population is often overlooked or not considered during development and implementation.
*   In general, ePA standards do not currently best serve the needs of pediatric patients for several reasons.
    *   There is a lack of knowledge and understanding about the needs of children.
    *   Many payer policies, including which services require PA, are not appropriately designed for children.
    *   Adult rules for PA are often inappropriately applied to the pediatric population.
    *   Regulations should be put in place to require payers to have appropriate pediatric expertise in crafting their policies around care that requires PA.
    *   Payers should also have “same specialty” expertise for appeals to reduce healthcare disparities in children.
*   The AAP offers feedback and recommendations for consideration, specifically concerning the impacts of potential changes to ePA within the ONC Health IT Certification Program on both patients and providers.

*   **Impact on Patients**
    *   **How could potential changes to the Certification Program to better support prior authorization positively impact healthcare consumers?**
        *   For children, especially those covered by Medicaid, insurance plans and coverage change constantly.
        *   Medicaid plans often do not support real-time validation of insurance coverage within the electronic health record (EHR).
        *   Many Medicaid plans are not integrated with real-time insurance verification, requiring practice teams to log onto a website to check eligibility and then manually enter that information into the EHR.
        *   Even commercial plans with integrated insurance verification/validation may note that the response does not guarantee coverage.
        *   Until there is a universal solution for real-time, dependable insurance verification/validation that payers are required to adhere to, any additional downstream authorizations that rely upon accurate insurance validation are not dependable and add to administrative burden.
        *   Further delays in the harmonization or expansion of ePA systems will complicate their use by physicians and others submitting PAs.
        *   An increased burden due to reduced efficiency of PAs will aggravate the problem of trust with the insurance and health information technology (HIT) ecosystem.
    *   **How could potential changes reduce the time for patients to receive needed healthcare services, reduce patient non-adherence, and/or lower out-of-pocket costs?**
        *   Electronic PA for children, even for medications, often results in delays in care.
        *   Because health systems have up to a certain number of days to respond to a PA request, delays in care can result in worsened outcomes for patients.
        *   This also has healthcare disparity implications because it is especially onerous in Medicaid plans which often change their formularies and PA rules several times during the calendar year.
        *   As children enroll or disenroll from insurance plans and have secondary coverages, it is not sustainable for the practice team.
        *   The Academy recommends implementing changes that allow for the automation of simple PAs, or first pass-triage.
            *   This would drastically decrease the time required to submit and process PAs.
            *   This would hopefully reduce the burden on clinical, clerical, and insurance staff.
            *   This will translate into time saved for patients.
            *   This would hopefully ensure the timely resolution of PAs in their favor, impacting both non-adherence and up-front or out-of-pocket costs for medication and services.
            *   This is particularly important for families with pediatric patients, as they often have to navigate multiple systems for both their own healthcare and that of their child.
            *   Reducing the burden of PA on the family through automation or electronic means (particularly the re-use of bundled electronic documents) may offer a means to decrease the stress on pediatric patients and their families.
    *   **Besides the provider to payer interactions discussed in this RFI, is there additional functionality that could be added to the Certification Program that would better support patients’ participation in the prior authorization process?**
        *   The AAP recommends making algorithms used by PA software to automate approval and processing more transparent as an additional functionality.
            *   This would ensure that both providers and patients can assemble appropriate documentation or information with an eye towards the system that is consuming said documentation and information.

*   **Impact on Providers**
    *   **To what degree is availability of electronic prior authorization capabilities within certified health IT likely to reduce burden for healthcare providers who currently engage in prior authorization activities?**
        *   In reviewing ONC’s current proposal, the Academy encourages greater consideration of actual clinical and provider workflows when it concerns ePA.
        *   There are several clinical decision pieces that are not evident in this proposal.
        *   Clinicians are the ones deciding on testing or treatment.
            *   Not only should they be given information about what requires PA, but they should also be given information about alternatives that would not require PA.
            *   This would be similar to e-prescribing where alternative formulary medications are presented to the prescriber at the exact decision point where alternatives can be chosen and discussed with the patient (including out-of-pocket expenses, alternative venues for testing, etc.).
            *   Until care provided is as robust as the medication e-prescribing system, this adds additional work and does not help clinicians make appropriate care decisions when the patient is in front of them.
        *   If PA takes two days to come back, it adds an additional burden to reach out to the patient and discuss potential alternatives or get more information needed to complete the PAs.
        *   There may have been already tried therapies that the payer is not aware of because the patient was covered by a different payer at the time.
            *   Without a shared database, the practice team needs to spend valuable time advising the payer that the required regimen was already tried, albeit when coverage was for a different payer.
        *   It is also difficult for the practice team when a particular treatment was covered without necessary PA, but now that the patient has moved insurances, to continue the same treatment with proven success may require PA.
            *   This is especially difficult for children who move across states and are covered by Medicaid.
            *   For example, one state’s Medicaid program may be paying for care or testing, but if the child moves across state lines, their care may be delayed because the new state’s Medicaid program requires PA.
        *   The Academy acknowledges that the availability of ePA capabilities within certified health IT is likely to support workflow redesign to reduce the burden on staff and healthcare providers, especially if attention is paid to the integration of PA modules with electronic medical record (EMR) data collection/processing.
        *   Within the current workflow, clinicians are overwhelmed with administrative messaging about many things.
            *   Adding an alert two days later about PA denial, appeal, or approval means that the clinician must then go back and review their intent and care plan.
            *   This cannot be done by administrative staff, requires additional input from clinicians, and rarely can be completed in one session.
            *   The Academy would recommend implementing an electronic capability that allows a form’s progress to be saved and additional information to be input at a later time.
        *   Many payers have layers of appeals in their PA process that put additional burdens on the practice team and cause delays in care.
            *   Every payer is different and has different requirements for what needs to be included in PA requests.
            *   Requiring insurance companies to have universal PA requirements and similar, transparent processes across payers may alleviate some burden.
            *   It is difficult for practices and providers to follow different rules set by different payers, especially when a patient may be dropped from or switch insurance plans during the process.
    *   **To what degree are healthcare providers likely to use these new capabilities across their patient panels? Will additional incentives or requirements be needed to ensure healthcare providers effectively use these capabilities? What accompanying documentation or support would be needed to ensure that technology capabilities are implemented in ways that effectively improve clinical workflows?**
        *   The answer to this question is dependent on the purchasing of software and services.
        *   This is best facilitated by the incentivization of providers and facilities to upgrade their IT infrastructure.
        *   Given the higher burden on practitioners outside of large organizations (such as single physician practices), incentives should support those independent practices to purchase and integrate software into their existing workflow.
        *   Accompanying documentation and support pertaining to EMR integration and exemplar workflow solutions would be of use.
        *   Ultimately, effective improvement is dependent on the clinical informatics capacity of individual practices.
        *   This problem is not going to be solved by documentation, but can be solved by more effectively supporting clinical informatics education and downstream solutions.
        *   The Academy suggests that these new capabilities would likely be used if they were automated and did not require extensive data entry or chart review.
            *   For example, step therapy for drug approval should be able to import the medication list, including dates therapies were tried and stopped.
        *   Since PAs save insurers money, those savings should be shared by the providers doing the work.
        *   If EHRs are held to the standard of having certified technology available to process PA application programming interfaces (APIs), there is no equivalent certification or penalty for payers to not follow the standard.
            *   These standards only work when both sides of the partnership are held by certification to the same standard and there is penalty for non-adherence.
            *   For now, all of the burden falls on the practice team and takes away from providing clinical care.
    *   **What estimates can providers share about the cost and time (in hours) associated with adopting and implementing electronic prior authorization functionality as part of care delivery processes?**
        *   The Academy acknowledges that a training period will likely be needed to adopt and implement ePA functionality as part of care delivery processes.
        *   Once completed, the time involved will depend on how robust the automation of the system can be made.
        *   If providers do not have to search to see if a PA is required (or, worse, only find out days later when the pharmacy notifies them), but instead can trigger the request in real time while the patient is present and get an immediate response, which is actionable in terms of prescribing, the burden will be real but small.
        *   However, any savings must be shared, or there will be no incentive to do even a small amount of additional work.

*   **Additional Comments:**
    *   The AAP strongly urges ONC to explicitly consider the needs of the pediatric population in this process.
    *   Until ePA works well for children, including for medications, this will only add administrative work to overburdened practices who are trying to jump through too many different hoops for payers.
    *   This administrative burden is taking away from caring for their patients.
    *   It is critically important for PA information to be current and kept up to date.
    *   Formularies and rules change so often that providers cannot keep up.
    *   This must be the responsibility of the payer or other entity requiring the PA.
    *   A response to a PA request, once provided, must be honored, and cannot be denied later because the provider was not given accurate, up-to-date information.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The American Academy of Pediatrics urges ONC to reform electronic prior authorization, emphasizing the unique, often overlooked needs of pediatric patients and advocating for automated, transparent, and equitably enforced standards to reduce provider burden and ensure timely patient care.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP)
- **Type:** Advocacy Group
- **Role/Expertise:** Represents 67,000 primary care pediatricians, pediatric medical sub-specialists, and pediatric surgical specialists dedicated to the health, safety, and well-being of infants, children, adolescents, and young adults.
- **Geographic Scope:** National (Headquarters in Itasca, IL; Washington D.C. office)
- **Stake in Issue:** Represents pediatric healthcare providers and advocates for the health and well-being of infants, children, adolescents, and young adults, who are directly impacted by the efficiency, consistency, and appropriateness of prior authorization processes.
structuredSections.corePosition: We acknowledge that prior authorization aims to control costs but echo concerns that variable policies, workflow challenges, and technical barriers create a significant burden for patients and providers, causing burnout and health risks due to care delays. We emphasize that current electronic prior authorization standards are particularly ill-suited for pediatrics, a population frequently overlooked during system development and implementation.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Require payers to have appropriate pediatric expertise when crafting prior authorization policies.
- Require “same specialty” expertise for prior authorization appeals to reduce healthcare disparities in children.
- Implement changes that allow for the automation of simple prior authorizations or first-pass triage.
- Make algorithms used by prior authorization software for automated approval and processing more transparent to providers and patients.
- Ensure that information about what requires prior authorization, and available alternatives that would not, is provided to clinicians at the decision point, similar to e-prescribing.
- Implement an electronic capability that allows a prior authorization form’s progress to be saved and additional information to be input at a later time.
- Require insurance companies to have universal prior authorization requirements and similar, transparent processes across payers.
- Incentivize providers and facilities (especially independent practices) to upgrade their IT infrastructure to better support electronic prior authorization capabilities.
- Ensure that savings generated by prior authorizations are shared by the providers doing the work.
- Establish equivalent certification or penalties for payers to ensure adherence to electronic prior authorization standards, just as EHRs are held to standards for processing APIs.
- Make payers responsible for keeping prior authorization information (formularies, rules) current and up to date.
- Ensure that a response to a prior authorization request, once provided, must be honored and cannot be denied later if the provider was given inaccurate or outdated information.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Prior authorization requirements, while intended to control costs, are a significant burden for patients, providers, and payers, leading to provider burnout and health risks due to delayed care.
- Current electronic prior authorization standards are not appropriately designed for children, with adult rules often inappropriately applied to the pediatric population.
- Constant changes in insurance plans and coverage for children, especially those covered by Medicaid, create significant administrative challenges.
- Many Medicaid plans do not support real-time validation of insurance coverage within the EHR, requiring manual checks and data entry.
- Lack of real-time, dependable insurance verification makes downstream authorizations unreliable and adds administrative burden.
- Delays in harmonizing or expanding electronic prior authorization systems will complicate their use and aggravate trust issues within the insurance and health information technology ecosystem.
- Electronic prior authorization for children, even for medications, frequently results in delays in care, potentially leading to worsened outcomes.
- The practice of health systems having a certain number of days to respond to prior authorization requests can cause harmful delays.
- Frequent changes in formularies and prior authorization rules by Medicaid plans multiple times a year create an onerous and unsustainable burden for practice teams, particularly impacting health equity.
- Current proposals do not adequately consider actual clinical and provider workflows, specifically lacking the integration of information about prior authorization requirements and alternatives at the point of care.
- The two-day response time for prior authorizations adds burden by requiring clinicians to re-engage with patients for alternatives or additional information.
- Lack of a shared database means practices must spend valuable time informing payers about previously tried therapies under different insurance plans.
- The need for prior authorization to continue proven treatments when a patient switches insurance, especially for children moving across state lines with Medicaid, causes care delays.
- Clinicians are overwhelmed with administrative messaging, and delayed alerts about prior authorization denials or approvals require time-consuming re-review of care plans that cannot be delegated.
- Multiple layers of appeals required by different payers, coupled with variable requirements, place additional burdens on practice teams and cause care delays.
- The current system places all the burden of prior authorization on the practice team, taking away from providing clinical care, without equivalent accountability or penalties for payers.
- Formularies and rules change so often that providers cannot keep up, but the responsibility for this critical information being current is often placed on the provider, not the payer.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- The unique vulnerability of pediatric patients, especially those on Medicaid, who experience constant changes in insurance plans and coverage, leading to significant administrative hurdles and care delays.
- The observation that “Adult rules for prior authorization are often inappropriately applied to the pediatric population,” highlighting a systemic oversight in policy design.
- The challenge posed by Medicaid plans often not supporting real-time insurance verification within EHRs, forcing manual lookups and undermining the reliability of electronic authorization.
- The impactful comparison of a desired ePA system to the e-prescribing system, where alternative formulary medications and cost implications are presented to the prescriber at the exact decision point.
- The specific real-world example of a child moving across state lines where care (e.g., a previously covered test) is delayed because the new state’s Medicaid program requires prior authorization.
- The insight that while prior authorizations save insurers money, providers doing the work receive no share of those savings, leading to a lack of incentive for provider adoption.
- The critical observation that standards for EHRs to process prior authorization APIs exist, but there’s “no equivalent certification or penalty for payers to not follow the standard,” creating an imbalanced burden on providers.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- “In particular, the current standards for electronic prior authorization do not work as intended for pediatrics – a population that is often overlooked or not considered during development and implementation.”
- “Until there is a universal solution to the problem of having real time dependable insurance verification/validation that payers are required to adhere to, any additional downstream authorizations that rely upon accurate insurance validation are not dependable and can add to additional practice administrative burden.”
- “These standards only work when both sides of the partnership are held by certification to the same standard and there is penalty for non-adherence. For now, all of the burden falls on the practice team and takes away from providing clinical care.”

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   87 FR 3477: We fully support this standard for interoperability between payers and providers and creating standards and certification requirements for systems that will enable this to occur.
    *   This is not only needed, it’s absolutely necessary to reduce the delays in care that we are seeing from the lack of standards and manual intervention that must be done.
    *   Not only do providers have to use manual mechanisms to communicate with payers, there are different requirements and mechanisms of communication with every payer for every service, and any system development is non-standard and complex.
    *   This non-standard data exchange creates:
        *   Delays in care
        *   Requires costly resources/staff to complete the work
        *   Leaves physicians/providers and patients frustrated and in some instances hopeless to fix the broken system to get the care they need in a timely fashion.
    *   This electronic exchange standard and functionality should also include that the payer provide:
        *   Whether PCP referral is required
        *   If non-contracted or non-covered
        *   Peer to peer requirement if denied
    *   The query/communication data/language between payers and providers must be standard and required by all payers and vendors to reduce complexity, reduce patient care delays and even adverse outcomes and resources (on both sides).
    *   Utilizing FIRH 278 and/or HL7 should be a mandatory standard for payers and providers, and required for Health Information Systems (HIS)/Electronic Health Records (EHRs) to provide this functionality "out of the box" and with ease.
*   87 FR 3478: Utilizing the standard data exchange should be a non-negotiable requirement for payers and providers.
    *   This will reduce cost on both sides and create more timely and effective responses.
    *   This will have a positive impact on patient care and expedited access to care.
*   87 FR 3481: This will expedite access to care for patients and reduce confusion for patients and providers.
    *   The variation in the way payers currently communicate with providers is very costly, time consuming and requires dense resources to manage all the different ways in which the payers communicate, which trickles to the consumer.
    *   This will have a direct impact on reducing costs to payer and providers, as well as create more timely access for patients to receive the care they need.
*   87 FR 3479: IP notifications, discharge notification, communicate L&I (Labor & Industries), accident forms, and comments related to Healthcare Attachments should be included.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: An anonymous individual with healthcare operational experience strongly advocates for mandatory, standardized electronic data exchange between healthcare payers and providers to eliminate manual processes, reduce costs, and expedite patient access to care.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Anonymous
- **Type:** Individual
- **Role/Expertise:** Healthcare Professional with experience in operational challenges of payer-provider communication and health information systems.
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly impacted by the current fragmented and manual healthcare communication systems, which cause delays in care, increase costs, and lead to frustration for providers and patients.
structuredSections.corePosition: We fully support the proposed standard for interoperability between payers and providers and the creation of standards and certification requirements for systems that enable this. This is absolutely necessary to reduce delays in care, high costs, and frustration stemming from the current lack of standards and reliance on manual interventions in healthcare communication.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Mandate a standard for interoperability between payers and providers.
- Implement certification requirements for systems that enable this interoperability.
- Ensure electronic exchange functionality includes payer-provided information on:
    - PCP referral requirements
    - Non-contracted or non-covered status
    - Peer-to-peer review requirements if a service is denied
- Standardize query/communication data and language between payers and providers, requiring its use by all payers and vendors.
- Mandate the utilization of FIRH 278 and/or HL7 as a standard for payers and providers.
- Require Health Information Systems (HIS)/Electronic Health Records (EHRs) to provide this functionality "out of the box" and with ease.
- Make standard data exchange a non-negotiable requirement for payers and providers.
- Include IP notifications, discharge notifications, L&I (Labor & Industries) communication, accident forms, and healthcare attachments in the standard data exchange.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Current lack of standards and reliance on manual intervention leads to significant delays in patient care.
- Providers face different communication requirements and mechanisms with every payer for every service.
- System development for payer-provider communication is non-standard and complex.
- Non-standard data exchange requires costly resources and staff.
- Physicians, providers, and patients experience frustration and, in some instances, hopelessness due to the broken system.
- Lack of standardized communication can lead to adverse patient outcomes.
- Variation in payer-provider communication methods is very costly, time-consuming, and requires dense resources to manage, which ultimately impacts consumers.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Direct observation that "different requirements and mechanisms of communication with every payer for every service" create a highly complex and burdensome environment for providers.
- Personal insight that the fragmented system leads to "frustration and in some instances hopelessness to fix the broken system to get the care they need in a timely fashion" for both physicians/providers and patients.
- Recognition that the current system's inefficiency "trickles to the consumer" in terms of cost and delayed care.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "This is not only needed, it’s absolutely necessary to reduce the delays in care that we are seeing from the lack of standards and manual intervention that must be done."
- "This non-standard data exchange creates delays in care, requires costly resources/staff to complete the work and leaves physicians/providers and patients frustrated and in some instances hopeless to fix the broken system to get the care they need in a timely fashion."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The American Association for Homecare (AAHomecare) is pleased to submit comments on the Department of Health and Human Services Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology's Request for Information: Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria.
*   AAHomecare is the national association representing durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics and supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers, manufacturers, and other stakeholders in the homecare community.
    *   Our members provide medically necessary DMEPOS items and services to patients in their homes.
*   AAHomecare supports maintaining and expanding the prior authorization (PA) program for certain DMEPOS items.
    *   PA has been successful in reducing provider burden and improving program integrity.
    *   AAHomecare is very supportive of a PA process that is efficient, electronic, and timely.
*   Prior authorization can be beneficial for certain DMEPOS product categories for all stakeholders, including patients, prescribers, and DMEPOS suppliers.
    *   Effective PA procedures improve the DMEPOS ordering process and reduces audit and appeal burden for suppliers and patients.
    *   An effective PA program should:
        *   Establish medical necessity for a DMEPOS item before the beneficiary receives the item.
        *   Include expedited procedures in case a referral source determines a beneficiary needs an item the same day.
        *   Rely on electronic communication that includes model documentation forms to facilitate the transmission of medical necessity information.
        *   Ensure beneficiaries have timely access to DMEPOS items subject to PA.
        *   Ensure that the ordering practitioner knows when he or she must submit records supporting a request or whether there are missing or deficient records he or she must supplement.
        *   Establish the beneficiary’s medical necessity for the DMEPOS item for as long as he or she needs the item even if the beneficiary moves or changes suppliers.
        *   Establish a beneficiary’s medical necessity for supplies, accessories and repairs of base DME with an affirmative prior approval for as long as the beneficiary needs the base equipment even if the beneficiary moves or changes suppliers.
*   Although AAHomecare is a strong supporter of PA for certain DME products, such as power mobility devices (PMDs), this program may not be beneficial for all product categories, such as urological, ostomy and surgical dressings.
    *   In addition, for products that need to be delivered immediately, a PA program could cause significant delays in needed care for patients.
    *   This tailored, individualized care means each beneficiary's care plan will differ based on their unique needs.
    *   The timing of the authorization process is a critical factor in determining which product categories should be considered.
    *   While a ten-day turnaround time may work for PMDs, it will not work for other product categories.
*   Prior authorization reduces provider burden and supports CMS program integrity.
    *   AAHomecare is a strong supporter of program integrity measures.
    *   PA for certain DMEPOS items will improve program integrity efforts and reduces audit and appeals costs for both suppliers and the Medicare program.
    *   PA for PMD has proven to be successful for CMS by reducing improper payments, the evidence can be seen in CMS’ Comprehensive Error Rate Testing (CERT) reports.
        *   In the 2012 Medicare Fee-for-Service Supplemental Improper Payment Data report (date of service: 07/01/2010-06/30/2011), CMS reported an 84.6% improper payment rate for PMDs.
        *   The significant drop seen between the 2014 report and 2015 report coincides with the start of PMD PA Demonstration.
        *   The CERT improper payment rate steadily decreased since the implementation of the PMD PA Demonstration, as illustrated in the provided graph.
*   Prescriber needs to be the driver of prior authorization.
    *   To implement an effective PA program, the PA request must be initiated and completed by the ordering prescriber.
    *   The issues with affirming prior approvals lie with the prescriber’s records.
    *   Therefore, the prescriber should obtain the authorization prior to writing an order and sending it to a supplier.
    *   In other situations where a PA is required, the ordering physician seeks the authorization for procedures such as an MRI.
    *   CMS should avoid positioning suppliers in the middle and make it clear the ordering prescriber is responsible for completing the PA process.
*   CMS should track prior authorization by anticipated response.
    *   The PA non-affirmation rate should not be a reflection of supplier activity or compliance.
    *   Frequently, suppliers submit a PA request anticipating a non-affirmation decision.
    *   Suppliers go through the process of submitting a request to demonstrate to the prescriber and/or the beneficiary that the order did not meet the coverage requirements.
    *   Some suppliers note on the request that they do not expect an affirmation of prior approval.
    *   AAHomecare recommends CMS include a field on the PA request where prescribers and/or suppliers can mark their expectation of a non-affirmation.
    *   In addition, AAHomecare recommends CMS track requests and report on affirmation/non-affirmation by product category based on the expected decision.
    *   Tracking submissions will provide CMS insightful information on needed provider education.
*   Prior authorization process should be uniform across all payers.
    *   AAHomecare recommends uniformity in the PA process across all payers.
    *   Currently, there are different processes, formats, timeframes, and products that are required to be prior authorized.
    *   For example, some payers require PA for all rentals and purchases over $500 while others have specific products that need to be prior authorized.
    *   Payers are also inconsistent with items that require PA.
        *   One payer requires PA for portable oxygen systems (HCPCS E1390 and E1392), but not on other portable systems (HCPCS E0434 and E0431), but not for stationary oxygen systems (HCPCS E1390).
        *   Some contractors have an Authorization Exclusion List based on diagnosis, which further complicates the requirements.
    *   Being required to follow all of the different requirements is burdensome for suppliers and providers.
    *   Below are some additional examples of differing requirements from payers:
        *   Some payers only require PA for over established quantities for disposable supplies.
        *   Some payers require PA for E1392 & E0431 but not E1390.
        *   Most payers only authorize positive airway pressure devices (E0601) for 3 months and then requires PA to for the remaining rental months.
        *   For one national payer, PAP supplies need authorization for the initial order with the rental but not for future supply orders.
        *   Some payers have an Authorization Exclusion List based on diagnosis.
*   Prior authorized items should not be subject to audit for medical necessity.
    *   The expansion of PA should exempt any claims for items that receive PA from subsequent routine audits, unless probable fraud and abuse is suspected.
    *   This would dramatically reduce the burden placed on the Medicare audit and appeals system by redirecting the focus of audits to criminal activities rather than lawful suppliers.
*   AAHomecare appreciates the opportunity to provide these comments.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The American Association for Homecare supports expanding electronic prior authorization for certain DMEPOS items, provided the process is prescriber-driven, uniform across payers, and exempts approved items from routine audits to reduce burden and improve program integrity.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
-   **Name/Organization:** American Association for Homecare (AAHomecare)
-   **Type:** Trade Association
-   **Role/Expertise:** National association representing durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics and supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers, manufacturers, and other stakeholders in the homecare community. Comments submitted by Kimberley S. Brummett, VP, Regulatory Affairs.
-   **Geographic Scope:** National
-   **Stake in Issue:** Represents organizations that provide medically necessary DMEPOS items and services to patients in their homes, directly affected by prior authorization processes, administrative burden, and program integrity initiatives related to federal regulations.
structuredSections.corePosition: AAHomecare strongly supports maintaining and expanding an efficient, electronic, and timely prior authorization (PA) process for *certain* durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and supplies (DMEPOS) items, as PA has proven successful in reducing provider burden and improving program integrity. However, we advocate for critical adjustments to the current system, including shifting PA responsibility to prescribers, ensuring uniformity across payers, and exempting prior-authorized items from routine audits to further streamline care delivery and reduce administrative overhead.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
-   Establish medical necessity for a DMEPOS item before the beneficiary receives the item.
-   Include expedited procedures for cases where a beneficiary needs an item the same day.
-   Rely on electronic communication that includes model documentation forms to facilitate the transmission of medical necessity information.
-   Ensure beneficiaries have timely access to DMEPOS items subject to PA.
-   Ensure that the ordering practitioner knows when to submit records supporting a request or whether there are missing or deficient records he or she must supplement.
-   Establish the beneficiary’s medical necessity for the DMEPOS item for as long as he or she needs the item, even if the beneficiary moves or changes suppliers.
-   Establish a beneficiary’s medical necessity for supplies, accessories, and repairs of base DME with an affirmative prior approval for as long as the beneficiary needs the base equipment, even if the beneficiary moves or changes suppliers.
-   Implement PA programs only for product categories where they are truly beneficial and do not cause significant delays in patient care.
    -   Consider the timing of the authorization process as a critical factor for determining which product categories should be considered for PA.
    -   For instance, a ten-day turnaround time may work for power mobility devices (PMDs), but it will not work for immediately needed items like urological, ostomy, or surgical dressings.
-   Require the ordering prescriber to initiate and complete the PA request.
    -   The prescriber should obtain authorization prior to writing an order and sending it to a supplier.
    -   CMS should avoid positioning suppliers in the middle of the PA process and clearly assign responsibility to the ordering prescriber.
-   CMS should track prior authorization by anticipated response.
    -   Include a field on the PA request where prescribers and/or suppliers can mark their expectation of a non-affirmation.
    -   Track requests and report on affirmation/non-affirmation by product category based on the expected decision, as this will provide insightful information on needed provider education.
-   Ensure uniformity in the PA process across all payers, encompassing processes, formats, timeframes, and products that require prior authorization.
-   Exempt any claims for items that receive PA from subsequent routine audits, unless probable fraud and abuse is suspected.
    -   This would dramatically reduce the burden placed on the Medicare audit and appeals system.
    -   It would redirect the focus of audits to criminal activities rather than lawful suppliers.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
-   Prior authorization programs may not be beneficial for all product categories, particularly those requiring immediate delivery (e.g., urological, ostomy, surgical dressings), potentially causing significant delays in needed patient care due to turnaround times.
-   The current system often positions suppliers in the middle of the prior authorization process, despite the root issues with affirming prior approvals stemming from the prescriber’s records.
-   The PA non-affirmation rate can unfairly reflect supplier activity or compliance, as suppliers frequently submit requests anticipating a non-affirmation to demonstrate to prescribers and/or beneficiaries that an order does not meet coverage requirements.
-   The current lack of uniformity in PA processes across different payers creates significant administrative burden for suppliers and providers due to varying processes, formats, timeframes, and products requiring prior authorization.
    -   Examples include differing requirements for rentals/purchases over certain amounts, inconsistent PA for specific HCPCS codes (e.g., portable vs. stationary oxygen systems), and varying authorization durations (e.g., positive airway pressure devices only authorized for 3 months initially).
    -   Some payers' use of Authorization Exclusion Lists based on diagnosis further complicates compliance.
-   Subjecting prior authorized items to subsequent routine audits (unless fraud is suspected) unnecessarily burdens the Medicare audit and appeals system and diverts resources from combating actual criminal activities.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
-   The significant success of the Power Mobility Device (PMD) PA Demonstration in reducing improper payment rates. CMS reported an 84.6% improper payment rate for PMDs in 2012, which steadily decreased following the demonstration's implementation, indicating PA's effectiveness in improving program integrity for specific categories.
-   The insight that suppliers often intentionally submit PA requests expecting a "non-affirmation" to formally demonstrate to prescribers and beneficiaries that an order does not meet coverage requirements, highlighting a need for CMS to track these anticipated responses.
-   Detailed examples illustrating the complex and burdensome inconsistencies in prior authorization requirements across various payers, including specific HCPCS codes for oxygen systems, varied authorization durations for PAP devices, and differing rules for disposable supplies, which underscore the need for uniformity.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
-   "The significant drop seen between the 2014 report and 2015 report coincides with the start of PMD PA Demonstration. The CERT improper payment rate steadily decreased since the implementation of the PMD PA Demonstration."
-   "The expansion of PA should exempt any claims for items that receive PA from subsequent routine audits, unless probable fraud and abuse is suspected. This would dramatically reduce the burden placed on the Medicare audit and appeals system by redirecting the focus of audits to criminal activities rather than lawful suppliers."
-   "Being required to follow all of the different requirements is burdensome for suppliers and providers."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   RadNet appreciates the opportunity to provide input regarding electronic prior-authorization standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria for the ONC Health IT Certification Program.
*   We understand this feedback may inform future rulemaking to enable providers to interact with health plans for automated, electronic completion of prior-authorization tasks.
*   RadNet welcomes ONC’s efforts to improve the prior-authorization experience.

*   **About RadNet**
    *   RadNet, Inc. is the leading national provider of freestanding, fixed-site diagnostic imaging services in the United States by number of locations and annual imaging revenue.
    *   Our goal is to deliver high-quality, conveniently accessible care in the most cost-effective manner, serving as an alternative to higher-priced hospital and health system-based imaging providers.
    *   RadNet has a network of over 360 owned and/or operated outpatient imaging centers across Arizona, California, Maryland, Florida, Delaware, New Jersey, and New York.
    *   Many centers are in or near underserved communities, providing at-risk populations with access to high-quality imaging care.
    *   Our nearly 800 radiologists and approximately 8,600 employees perform an estimated nine million imaging procedures annually.
    *   RadNet also provides radiology information technology solutions, including an ONC-certified radiology information system (RIS) through its eRAD subsidiary, teleradiology professional services, and other related products to the diagnostic imaging industry.
    *   RadNet is a global leader in using artificial intelligence (AI) for early detection and diagnosis of breast, lung, and prostate cancers through its DeepHealth AI division.

*   **General Comments**
    *   **I. Prior-Authorization is a Significant Problem**
        *   In its current, mostly manual form, prior-authorization leads to delays in patient care and adds burdens, financial hardships, and higher costs for providers.
        *   RadNet has significant first-hand experience with prior-authorization.
            *   We perform nearly nine million procedures annually; approximately 20 percent (1.8 million) are advanced diagnostic imaging modalities (e.g., CT, MRI) which typically require prior-authorization.
            *   We have made major investments in technology and other workflow solutions in response to prior-authorization.
            *   Despite these investments, a significant percentage of our denials are related to prior-authorization.
            *   RadNet has had to invest substantially in additional staff resources to work these denials and attempt to collect from insurers.
            *   It has been a source of frustration that we continue to absorb the risk, burden, and expense of this process while seeing no benefit in return.
        *   Patient care is negatively impacted by prior-authorization through unnecessary delays, both directly and indirectly.
            *   Directly, patient care is delayed while the requested imaging study awaits approval from the payer or benefits manager, which can take several days.
            *   Indirectly, miscommunication causes delays in patient care.
                *   Patients present to schedule imaging studies without necessary prior approval, leading to postponement of the study until approval is received, or the study proceeds with RadNet bearing the risk of non-payment.
                *   Conversely, patients present with prior-authorizations having been performed, only to find the authorization was not required, wasting time, money, and pulling resources from patient care.
    *   **II. Avoid Being Overly Prescriptive; Provide Flexibility and Multiple Pathways**
        *   We strongly advise the ONC to refrain from being overly prescriptive in any standards, specifications, or criteria associated with prior-authorization within the Certification Program.
        *   Instead, the ONC should:
            *   (1) Establish standards for prior-authorization information (e.g., requirements, status, details) and their storage in certified health IT systems.
            *   (2) Permit flexible and multiple pathways, an “all of the above” approach, to share this information between health plans, benefit managers, providers, IT vendors, etc., by whatever means (e.g., APIs, X12 278s).
        *   By focusing on the intent and not any specific technology, ONC can capitalize on, rather than inhibit, innovations already happening in this space and allow parties to develop solutions that work best for them and not require unused capabilities.

*   **Specific Comments**
    *   **I. Functional Capabilities for Electronic Prior-Authorization in Certified Health IT**
        *   **A. Core Capabilities (General)**
            *   RadNet supports six of the seven core functional capabilities proposed for electronic prior-authorization with modification, but questions the need for functionality #7, “Effectively capture and persist digital signatures …”
            *   Regarding proposed functionality #7, electronic signatures are not widely or typically used in the prior-authorization process; therefore, we question its need or seek additional clarification of its purpose.
        *   **B. Comments on Specific Proposed Core Functionalities**
            *   **1. RFI: Core Functionality #2, “Query a payer API for prior-authorization requirements for each item and service and identify in real-time specific rules and documentation requirements.”**
                *   RadNet: The API query functionality for prior-authorization requirements should not be limited to payers, but should also include benefit managers and others as appropriate.
                    *   Payers often outsource their prior-authorization processes to third-parties, such as benefits managers (e.g., radiology benefit managers).
                    *   Providers, vendors, and others will need the ability to query the payer’s API plus the APIs of any third-parties acting on the payer’s behalf in the prior-authorization process.
                    *   We agree that prior-authorization should be real-time and for each service rather than processed in batches.
            *   **2. RFI: Core Functionality #3, “Collect clinical and administrative documentation needed to complete prior-authorization documentation (electronic forms or templates) from a health IT system.”**
                *   RadNet: Certified health IT systems should have the capability to store prior-authorization information, but its format should be left to industry and users.
                    *   As described in our General Comments, the ONC should avoid overly prescriptive technical solutions.
                    *   Instead, the ONC should focus on identifying what prior-authorization data should be collected and how they are stored.
                    *   Currently, there are multiple ways to share this information, and the ONC should avoid focusing on only one.
            *   **RFI: Core Functionality #4, “Electronically submit completed documentation for prior-authorization to a payer’s API, along with supporting information.”**
                *   RadNet: The submission of prior-authorization documentation and supporting information should not be limited to any specific entity or technology.
                    *   We agree that prior-authorization documentation and supporting information need to be transmitted electronically.
                    *   However, there should be flexibility in terms of the “who” and “how.”
                    *   For example, providers or their agents could create their own APIs that contain prior-authorization documentation for the payer to query.
                    *   Blockchain or other forms of digital ledgers could be another way of sending prior-authorization documentation.
            *   **3. RFI: Core Functionality #5, “Receive a response from a payer regarding approval, denial (including a reason for denial), or need for additional information.”**
                *   RadNet: Certified health IT systems should be able to receive a prior-authorization response from whatever the source, not just a payer.
                    *   As previously mentioned, it is common for commercial insurers to outsource their prior-authorization processes to third-party vendors, such as benefit managers (e.g., radiology benefit managers or RBMs).
                    *   Therefore, certified health IT systems must have the capability to receive prior-authorization responses from payers and their agents.
                    *   Also, to improve authorization coordination, prior-authorization responses should be shared between providers involved in the patient’s care.
            *   **4. RFI: Core Functionality #6, “Query a payer’s system for updates on a pending prior-authorization request and have a reason returned as to why a request is still pending.”**
                *   RadNet: Certified health IT systems should be able to query whatever system is needed for an update on pending prior-authorizations and their rationale.
                    *   The ONC should focus on the sharing of prior-authorization updates and rationales in general and not one particular source (payers) because there could be multiple entities for this information.
                    *   For example, providers may want to exchange this information.
        *   **C. Implementation Specifications to Support Electronic Prior-Authorization Capabilities**
            *   RadNet: Da Vinci should not be the only way for storing and sending prior-authorization information.
                *   RFI: The ONC seeks comment on adopting three implementation guides (IGs) for use in certified health IT systems: (1) HL7® FHIR® Da Vinci Coverage Requirements Discovery (CRD) Implementation Guide, (2) HL7® FHIR® Da Vinci Documentation Templates and Coverage Rules (DTR) Implementation Guide, and (3) HL7® FHIR® Da Vinci Prior Authorization Support (PAS) Implementation Guide.
                *   RadNet: Da Vinci is a promising way to automate the exchange of prior-authorization information.
                *   However, it should not be the only method of exchange.
                *   This flexibility is important because Da Vinci still has limited use-cases (e.g., unaware of progress for outpatient radiology).
        *   **D. Additional Approaches to Support Electronic Prior-Authorization: Healthcare Attachments**
            *   RadNet: The ONC should consider multiple standards for transmitting healthcare attachments, including DICOM®.
                *   RFI: The ONC seeks comments on how the Certification Program can support the transmission of healthcare attachments using current standards (HL7 C-CDA or HL7 FHIR) or other approaches.
                *   RadNet: CDA and FHIR would support the exchange of healthcare attachments for prior-authorization, but they are not alone.
                *   For example, DICOM® (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) is the international standard for exchanging medical images and related information (including radiology reports).
                *   DICOM® is well-established (first published in 1993) and in widespread use in the United States and internationally.
                *   Again, we recommend that the ONC focus on the “what” and leave the “how” to the users and developers.
        *   **E. Impact on Patients**
            *   RadNet: Electronic prior-authorization standards can improve patient care by minimizing delays and improving communication.
                *   RFI: The ONC seeks feedback on how changes to the Certification Program that better support prior-authorization or other functionalities can have a positive impact on patient care.
                *   RadNet: Traditional prior-authorization’s reliance on legacy technology and solutions (e.g., fax machines, portals) causes patient care to be delayed through long wait-times for approvals and the chance for miscommunication.
                *   Delays awaiting approvals reduce patient adherence to recommended tests and procedures.
                *   Electronic (automated) prior-authorization holds the promise to accelerate the approval process, which should result in timelier care and improved compliance.
                *   By reducing or eliminating much of the cost associated with traditional prior-authorization, payers can pass savings to beneficiaries through lower premiums.
                *   The Certification Program should include other transaction scenarios beyond payer-provider, such as, but not limited to, benefit managers, ordering providers, rendering providers (e.g., radiology), and patients.
                *   This ensures all relevant parties are aware and can be kept apprised of requests as they make their way through the prior-authorization process.
        *   **F. Impact on Providers**
            *   RadNet: The current prior-authorization process is burdensome, costly, and a major source of frustration and miscommunication for providers.
            *   Optimizing electronic workflows, advancing new standards, and automating prior-authorization processes have the potential for reducing burden, costs, and delays in patient care.
            *   **1. RFI: To what degree is availability of electronic prior authorization capabilities within certified health IT likely to reduce burden for healthcare providers who currently engage in prior authorization activities?**
                *   RadNet: Electronic prior-authorization can reduce the burden for healthcare providers in three specific areas: (1) medical necessity determinations, (2) coordination, and (3) manual administrative workflow.
                    *   Medical necessity determinations should be a seamless automated transaction.
                        *   Payers frequently ask us for additional documentation supporting medical necessity as a condition for payment.
                        *   Typically, such documentation resides in the ordering clinician’s EHR, not ours, and must be requested.
                        *   This unfairly puts the onus on us to do work that should have happened before the exam was ordered.
                        *   Upon receiving a compliant order and authorization, radiology and other referral-based specialties should be able to rely that medical necessity has been determined and the exam can proceed and will be reimbursed appropriately.
                    *   Electronic prior-authorization should improve authorization coordination between the payer, ordering professional, and rendering provider (e.g., RadNet).
                        *   Currently, the clinician who receives the final response is the one who submitted the prior-authorization request, usually the patient’s treating provider.
                        *   Information regarding the rendering provider is typically part of the prior-authorization request.
                        *   But, payers and benefits managers do not automatically notify the rendering provider who requires the authorization to perform the procedure and be paid.
                        *   In the absence of automated notifications, the ordering provider has to manually transfer prior-authorization approval information or the rendering provider has to hunt-down authorizations from the payer’s web-portal.
                        *   This is inefficient, introduces potential for error, and patients may present for studies without the required approvals.
                        *   As a result, time and money are wasted, and resources are diverted from patient care.
                    *   Electronic prior-authorization should allow for the elimination of much of the manual workflows associated with the current process such as accessing provider portals for updates, looking-up a patient’s out-of-pocket costs, sending documentation by fax, data entry, etc.
            *   **2. RFI: To what degree are healthcare providers likely to use these new capabilities across their patient panels?**
                *   RadNet: Twenty percent of our patients require prior-authorization, and this percentage is increasing.
                    *   We perform nearly nine million procedures annually; approximately 20 percent (1.8 million) are advanced diagnostic imaging modalities (e.g., CT, MRI) which typically require prior-authorization.
                    *   Some payers are beginning to require prior-authorization for other imaging exams (e.g., ultrasound).
            *   **3. RFI: Will additional incentives or requirements be needed to ensure healthcare providers effectively use these capabilities?**
                *   RadNet: Providers will be incentivized if electronic prior-authorization results in faster and less expensive transactions.
                    *   Providers do not need incentives if changes to the Certification Program result in prior-authorization transactions that are easier, faster, and less expensive than what can be achieved through traditional means (phone calls, portals).
