- "One Utilization Management Program Manager from a large health system in the Midwest said told MCG upon implementing an EHR-based authorization request / response solution 'We really are very excited about this Collaborative Care work and [implemented payer] is one of our top payers in terms of auth volume. We think that this is the right way to go, and it will relieve a tremendous amount of administrative burden for our teams.'"
- "Another provider reviewer for prior authorizations on the West Coast said, 'I spend about half my time submitting new authorization requests; the other half of my time is spent looking for status updates on previously submitted authorizations.'"

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The Connecticut Hospital Association (CHA) is a not-for-profit membership organization representing over 90 hospitals and health-related organizations.
    *   CHA’s mission is to advance the health of individuals and communities by leading, representing, and serving hospitals and healthcare providers across the continuum of care.
*   Private health plans manage most healthcare coverage in Connecticut, including employer-sponsored, Medicare Advantage, and exchange coverage.
    *   These plans rely on prior authorization to gate-keep access to medically necessary services.
    *   While prior authorization's stated intent is to ensure safe and appropriate care, many health plans apply requirements in ways that are costly, burdensome, and disruptive to patient care.
*   CHA strongly supports creating a usable, scalable, and efficient solution to reduce prior authorization impacts on patients and providers.
    *   CHA urges the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) to pilot the proposed technologies and workflows before taking regulatory steps.
        *   This is to minimize unintended negative consequences, such as an inadvertent increase in costs or burden.
    *   CHA encourages ONC to work with the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) and Congress to protect patients by enacting aligned standards for health plans regarding timely and appropriate conduct of prior authorization.
*   Background
    *   Health plans commonly use inconsistent administrative protocols and a "dizzying array" of timelines and requirements for prior authorization requests, reviews, approvals, and communication.
        *   These variations add burden as providers and staff must follow different rules for each plan, which can change frequently and vary by plan, product, and vendor.
    *   Today’s prior authorization processes are inefficient.
        *   Authorizations are often conducted by phone with long wait times, sometimes several hours.
        *   Clinical information is shared with initial reviewers who then pass the physician to additional clinical reviewers.
        *   These processes disregard the value of clinical staff time and resources.
        *   Burdensome processes, multifarious requirements, and manual processing result in failures of authorization or adherence, leading to loss of reimbursement.
    *   Despite extensive time and resources spent, prior authorization requests are often returned multiple times for additional information, and further delayed by slow health plan responses or failure to respond outside traditional business hours.
    *   During the public health emergency, with workforce shortages and strained health system capacity, there is insufficient bandwidth to comply with cumbersome administrative procedures.
        *   Hospitals often employ multiple full-time staff whose sole role is to manage prior authorization requests.
        *   Prior authorization processes exacerbate workforce challenges and contribute to physician and staff burnout.
        *   Expending staff resources on administrative requirements is unreasonable, especially during unprecedented challenges in recruiting and retaining healthcare workers.
    *   These inefficiencies incur enormous costs.
        *   A large national system spends $15 million per month in administrative costs related to managing health plan contracts, including two to three full-time staff dedicated to monitoring rule changes.
        *   These costs are incurred by providers but ultimately contribute to healthcare cost growth borne by employers, taxpayers, and patients.
    *   CHA applauds ONC’s proposal to establish electronic prior authorization standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria as part of the ONC Health IT Certification Program.
        *   Standardization and automation are urgently needed and long overdue.
*   Functional Capabilities
    *   ONC proposes core functional capabilities for certified Health IT Modules:
        *   Identify when prior authorization is applicable, using clinical decision support and/or user input, and receive notifications of changes.
        *   Query a payer API for prior authorization requirements and identify real-time rules and documentation.
        *   Collect clinical and administrative documentation from a health IT system.
        *   Electronically submit completed documentation to a payer’s API with supporting information.
        *   Receive a response from a payer regarding approval, denial (with reason), or need for additional information.
        *   Query a payer’s system for updates on a pending request and receive a reason for delay.
        *   Effectively capture and persist digital signatures, enable data integrity, and support features for payer administrative requirements.
    *   CHA endorses all proposed core capabilities but requests additional functionality:
        *   Include identifying when pre-determination is recommended and support the process for obtaining it.
        *   If a service is denied, enable online scheduling of a peer-to-peer review with the health plan’s clinical staff.
        *   Support for concurrent reviews for inpatient services to update authorizations for full diagnoses and service levels.
        *   Ability to update requested authorization post-surgical or other procedures to document and solicit approval for adjusted diagnoses and procedure codes.
        *   Consider episode-driven authorization workflow (e.g., admission to inpatient and discharge to post-acute).
        *   Ensure functionality supports the full cycle denial and appeals process.
        *   Extend appeals functionality to appeals resulting from prepayment and postpayment audits.
*   Impact on Patients and Providers
    *   Aggressive prior authorization practices adversely affect patient care by restricting access to covered services.
    *   Health plans continually change rules, often mid-contract, leading to uncertainties and delays that jeopardize timely patient care.
    *   Delays are most common after hours or on weekends when health plans lack staff for routine reviews.
    *   Keeping patients in the emergency department or inpatient beds while waiting for a PA decision is not in the patient's best interest.
    *   Patients who no longer require acute care should be transferred promptly to post-acute care.
        *   Delays in transfer negatively affect recovery progress.
    *   Hospitals report patients spending multiple extra days in the hospital due to health plan delays in approving transfer to post-acute care.
        *   Example: Patients ready for discharge on Friday after business hours often wait until Monday due to lack of weekend PA reviews.
    *   Standardized electronic prior authorization transactions have the potential to save significant time and resources and speed up care delivery.
    *   However, potential benefits are limited if payers are not accountable to timeliness and related performance standards.
    *   CHA strongly encourages ONC to work with CMS and Congress to establish performance standards for ERISA self-funded health plans, Medicare Advantage, Medicaid Managed Care, and health insurance exchange plans.
*   CHA recommends that such prior authorization standards include, but not be limited to, the following:
    1.  Process prior authorization requests 24 hours a day, seven days a week, including holidays.
    2.  Designate a staff member as the primary contact person for each contracted hospital during normal business hours.
    3.  Respond to each prior authorization request as expeditiously as the covered person’s health condition requires and no later than:
        *   48 hours for any expedited prior authorization request.
        *   One hour for any prior authorization request submitted by a hospital for post-stabilization and maintenance services for emergency patients not yet discharged.
    4.  Refrain from delaying a decision by requiring additional information not reasonably available when the request was submitted.
    5.  Accept standard documentation to support initial reviews, concurrent reviews, and appeals.
*   CHA strongly supports prior authorization reform, including electronic processes to streamline the arduous process, improve patient care, and reduce provider burnout.
    *   CHA further supports standards to ensure payer accountability regarding authorization timeframes and policies to prevent patient care from being jeopardized by health plan review.
*   CHA supports proposed technological advancements for improved safety, quality, and efficiency.
    *   However, incorporating new technology is extremely resource-intensive for hospitals and providers.
        *   It requires systematic updates, testing, personnel education and training, workflow adjustments, and potential policy changes, all while performing standard revenue cycle functions.
    *   CHA recommends testing and piloting these solutions to ensure the investments and workflow changes are worthwhile.
*   Thoughtful and sustained action is needed to ensure health plans’ administrative processes do not impede patients’ ability to receive timely, quality, medically necessary care in a clinically appropriate setting.
    *   This is especially critical during the ongoing global pandemic, with new variants, surges, vaccine administration, workforce shortages, and critical testing/treatment capacity.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The Connecticut Hospital Association strongly supports electronic prior authorization and standardization but urges piloting new technologies and calls for federal mandates on health plan accountability and timeliness to reduce burdens on providers and prevent patient harm.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Connecticut Hospital Association
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Represents over 90 hospitals and health-related organizations; advances health by leading, representing, and serving healthcare providers.
- **Geographic Scope:** State (Connecticut)
- **Stake in Issue:** Member hospitals and healthcare providers face significant financial and operational burdens, workforce challenges, and compromised patient care due to current prior authorization practices.
structuredSections.corePosition: "We strongly support the creation of a useable, scalable and efficient solution to help reduce prior authorization impacts on patients and providers. However, we urge the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) to pilot the technologies and workflows described in the rule prior to taking any regulatory steps necessary to minimize unintended negative consequences, such as an inadvertent increase in costs or burden in the healthcare system."
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Pilot the proposed electronic prior authorization technologies and workflows prior to regulatory steps to minimize unintended negative consequences (e.g., increased costs or burden).
- Work with CMS and Congress to enact aligned, enforceable standards for health plans related to the timely and appropriate conduct of prior authorization for all plan types (ERISA self-funded, Medicare Advantage, Medicaid Managed Care, health insurance exchange plans).
    - These standards should include, but not be limited to:
        - Process prior authorization requests 24 hours a day, seven days a week, including holidays.
        - Designate a staff member as the primary contact person for each contracted hospital during normal business hours.
        - Respond to each request expeditiously, no later than 48 hours for expedited requests, and one hour for hospital requests for post-stabilization and maintenance services for emergency patients not yet discharged.
        - Refrain from delaying decisions by requiring additional information not reasonably available when the request was submitted.
        - Accept standard documentation to support initial reviews, concurrent reviews, and appeals.
- Enhance the proposed Health IT Module functional capabilities to include:
    - Identifying when pre-determination is recommended and supporting the process for obtaining it.
    - Functionality for online scheduling of peer-to-peer reviews with health plan clinical staff if a service is denied.
    - Support for concurrent reviews for inpatient services to update authorizations to reflect the full range of pertinent diagnoses and service levels.
    - The ability to update requested authorization post-surgical or other procedures to document and solicit approval for adjusted diagnoses and procedure codes.
    - Consideration of episode-driven authorization workflow (e.g., from admission to discharge).
    - Ensuring functionality supports the full cycle denial and appeals process.
    - Extending appeals functionality to appeals resulting from prepayment and postpayment audits.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- **Excessive Burden and Inefficiency:**
    - Health plans use inconsistent administrative protocols, timelines, and requirements that are "unnecessary at best" and "unconscionable" during public health emergencies.
    - Prior authorizations are frequently conducted by phone with long wait times (several hours), requiring clinical information to be shared multiple times.
    - This results in burdensome processes, multifarious requirements, and manual processing leading to failures of authorization or adherence, and loss of reimbursement.
- **Workforce Strain and Burnout:**
    - Cumbersome administrative procedures consume "inordinate resources" and exacerbate workforce shortages and staff burnout, with hospitals employing multiple full-time employees solely for prior authorization.
    - Expending staff resources on these administrative requirements is unreasonable, especially during ongoing challenges recruiting and retaining essential healthcare workers.
- **Financial Costs:**
    - Inefficiencies exact an "enormous cost," with one large national system spending $15 million per month on administrative costs related to managing health plan contracts.
    - These costs, while directly incurred by providers, ultimately contribute to healthcare cost growth for employers, taxpayers, and patients.
- **Compromised Patient Care and Access:**
    - Aggressive prior authorization practices adversely affect patient care by restricting access to medically necessary services.
    - Health plans frequently change rules unilaterally, even mid-contract, creating uncertainty and delays that jeopardize timely patient care decisions.
    - Delays are most common after hours or on weekends when health plans lack review staff, leading to patients waiting in emergency departments or inpatient beds unnecessarily.
    - Patients spending multiple extra days in the hospital due to health plan delays in approving transfers to post-acute care negatively affects their recovery.
- **Limited Effectiveness of Electronic Solutions Without Accountability:**
    - The potential benefits of standardized electronic prior authorization will be "of limited value if payers are not accountable to timeliness and related standards of performance."
- **Resource-Intensive Technology Implementation:**
    - Incorporating new technology is "extremely resource-intensive" for hospitals and providers, requiring systematic updates, testing, personnel education and training, workflow adjustments, and potential policy changes, all while maintaining revenue cycle functions.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Hospitals often dedicate multiple full-time employees solely to managing health plan prior authorization requests, highlighting the immense administrative burden.
- A specific financial impact: A large national healthcare system reportedly spends $15 million per month in administrative costs related to managing health plan contracts, including staff dedicated to monitoring rule changes.
- Illustrative patient impact: Patients ready for discharge after "business hours" on a Friday are often forced to wait all weekend in the hospital for prior authorization approval to transfer to a more appropriate level of post-acute care, negatively affecting their recovery.
- The observation that prior authorization processes at times "disregard the value of clinical staff time and resources."
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "While the stated intent of prior authorization is to help ensure patients receive safe and appropriate care, many health plans apply prior authorization requirements in ways that are costly, burdensome, and disruptive to patient care."
- "During a time of national emergency where workforce shortages and strained health system capacity have been persistent challenges, there is simply insufficient bandwidth to comply with such cumbersome administrative procedures."
- "Even so, the potential benefits of such standardization will be of limited value if payers are not accountable to timeliness and related standards of performance."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The American College of Cardiology (ACC) is pleased to respond to the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) Request for Information (RFI) on Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria.
*   The ACC envisions a world where innovation and knowledge optimize cardiovascular care and outcomes.
*   As the professional home for the entire cardiovascular care team, the mission of the College and its more than 54,000 members is to transform cardiovascular care and improve heart health.
*   The ACC bestows credentials upon cardiovascular professionals, leads in health policy, standards, and guidelines, provides professional medical education, disseminates cardiovascular research, operates national registries, and offers cardiovascular accreditation.
*   **Recent Efforts to Advance Electronic Prior Authorization Processes**
    *   The ACC greatly appreciates the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) and ONC’s efforts to understand and address the increasingly burdensome prior authorization (PA) process.
    *   As an original collaborator on the American Medical Association (AMA) Prior Authorization and Utilization Management Reform Principles, the College and its members understand the burdens and inefficiencies associated with the insurance approval process.
        *   This process requires dedicated staff, time away from direct patient care, and involves endless requests, telephone calls, faxes, denials, and appeals.
    *   Standardizing data exchange is vitally important to streamlining the PA process.
    *   ACC members and staff spend countless hours and resources verifying payer authorization requirements via web portals, calling hotlines, and completing request forms.
    *   Each payer has its preferred process, sometimes multiple processes, for submitting PA requests.
    *   Decision responses can vary (approval, denial, request for additional information, or physician-to-physician discussion).
    *   Variability intensifies with contracted third-party PA vendors, which have their own portals and interfaces.
    *   Standardizing and minimizing variability will improve clinicians’ and staff's ability to manage PA work.
    *   Due to the volume of PAs needed, many offices secure authorization just before the patient visit or appointment.
    *   Frequently, responses are not returned in the established time, leading to appointment/procedure cancellation, rescheduling, or multiple visits.
    *   Reducing the burden and complexity of PA outcomes would provide more efficient and predictable patient care delivery.
    *   Standardized electronic prior authorization (e-PA) is one way ONC can reduce one of the most taxing burdens facing clinicians and ensure timely patient access to care.
    *   Despite potential procedural improvements from standardized e-PA, the ACC conveys strong concern and opposition to the ever-growing PA requirements across payers.
    *   Over the last ten years, insurers, including Medicare Advantage, have substantially increased PA use, creating significant barriers to medically necessary care that negatively impact patients and providers.
        *   According to a Regulatory Relief Coalition survey, PA approvals typically take 2-14 days, but some can take 15 to more than 31 days, sometimes forcing patients to abandon treatment.
        *   This survey also indicated PA requirements impose significant administrative burdens, with most physicians contending with 11-40 PA requests weekly.
    *   The ACC encourages continued action to fully address PA administrative burden by being more judicious with PA deployment.
    *   Patients receiving services that are nearly universally approved are unlikely to benefit from PA.
    *   High approval rates after inordinate delays drive the perception among clinicians that PA's goal is to discourage healthcare resource consumption rather than ensure high-quality care.
    *   The College understands the need to establish high-quality, evidence-based cardiovascular care and address potential over- and under-utilization.
    *   The College has a long history of developing practice guideline recommendations, appropriate use criteria, and expert consensus documents.
    *   However, the ACC implores the payer community to create a more selective application of PA and to review existing requirements for elimination.
    *   Regardless of process improvements through e-PA, clinician burdens will only increase as the number of PA requirements continues to increase.
*   **Functional Capabilities for Electronic Prior Authorization in Certified Health IT**
    *   ONC seeks comment on functional capabilities for e-PA to be included in certified health IT, representing minimum requirements to reduce burdens.
    *   A 2020 WEDI Prior Authorization survey found only 18% of providers could evaluate if authorization is required without initiating a PA request.
    *   It should be a bare minimum requirement that healthcare providers and staff can electronically determine if a PA is required and the specific requirements for each item/service without initiating a request.
    *   Any required capabilities for Certified Health IT must be thoughtfully deployed into established clinical workflows, not layered on top of previously deployed certified technology, which can hamper workflow and damage existing systems.
    *   External applications and portals that interface with existing Health IT systems may technically meet e-PA capability requirements but will do little to reduce provider burdens and do not meet the intent of needed reforms.
    *   While it may be easier for payers to develop API functionality with external portals, ONC must consider the realities providers face with modern EHR and Health IT systems.
        *   Connecting to external sources requires additional, time-consuming steps, complicating rather than automating the process.
        *   Connecting to external sources introduces additional cybersecurity risks.
        *   This approach increases overall costs for physicians and staff in both time and money.
    *   Instead, ONC should consider adding usability and workflow incorporation metrics into the certification program for Certified EHR Technology (CEHRT).
    *   The ACC has called on ONC to re-evaluate EHR reporting criteria and the submission process, focusing on improving security, usability, and interoperability through user-reported criteria and developer-reported data.
    *   The College believes e-PA standards and certification criteria offer an opportunity for ONC to take concrete steps to reduce clinician burdens, including decreasing fatigue caused by poor workflow, pop-ups, and alerts.
*   **Implementation Specifications to Support Electronic Prior Authorization Capabilities**
    *   It is essential that ONC require PA standards at the certification level due to the complexity, cost, and skill level required for implementing standards-based approaches to electronic health systems.
    *   Larger practices, hospitals, and organizations have IT staff for implementation guides, but smaller offices, independent practices, and community clinics often lack this expertise or funds.
        *   They often rely on off-the-shelf CEHRT systems and ONC-mandated capabilities.
    *   For e-PA to be effective and equitable in all clinical settings, ONC must require the highest levels of functional capabilities during the certification process and should not rely on staff-level implementation.
    *   ONC should work with CMS to ensure certification requirements align with interoperability efforts and address market gaps.
    *   CMS previously considered requiring PA decision information to be available to patients through patient access application programming interfaces (APIs).
    *   The ACC encourages ONC and CMS to make information available to patients via APIs and establish criteria to ensure this is possible.
    *   The College consistently advocates for expanded patient access to and ownership of their health data, including PA decisions, and relevant health and administrative information should be accessible to patients.
    *   The College believes ONC should work with CMS in future rulemaking to address gaps in past rulemaking.
        *   This includes expanding patient access API requirements to Medicare Advantage plans.
        *   It also includes the inclusion of prescription drug and covered outpatient drug information.
    *   Inclusion of Medicare Advantage payers in patient access APIs is necessary to ensure the largest payers streamline PA, adopt standards, and drive provider involvement.
    *   Including prescription and outpatient drug information in sharing requirements is equally important to preserve care continuity for new patients.
    *   Medications are a growing part of disease management, and patients should have access to such information.
*   **Request for Comments: Impact on Patients**
    *   As stated, the ACC advocates for expanded patient access to and ownership of their health data, including PA decisions.
    *   Relevant health and administrative information in PA policies and determinations should be accessible to both patients and providers.
    *   Implementing policies that make previous "black box" decisions available creates transparency for patients to understand test/treatment options and provides security that payers will cover necessary care.
    *   Transparency will help providers focus on patient needs and health rather than administrative hurdles for coverage.
    *   For patient care to improve, ONC must work with CMS to ensure payers cannot deny API use or access based on whether the provider using the API is under contract with the payer.
    *   This aligns with Congress’, CMS’, and ONC’s intent to end information blocking.
    *   Historically, contracts have been blockades to information exchange, reducing interoperability.
    *   It is important to promote policies encouraging essential information exchange and allowing patients and providers access to important health information in PA processes.
    *   In previous rulemaking, CMS proposed allowing impacted payers to implement a patient opt-in process for provider access API data sharing.
    *   While the College understood the intent, the ACC expressed concerns that providers may not know which patients have opted in, especially with dual-eligible patients, where information could be unnecessarily segmented.
    *   ONC and CMS must thoughtfully consider ways to allow patients to control data access while still allowing providers to access important health information necessary for continuity of care and treatment.
*   **Conclusion**
    *   On behalf of the ACC and its 54,000 members, the ACC commends ONC for engaging with stakeholders to improve PA processes.
    *   For cardiovascular professionals, PA is an unfortunate requirement of daily medical practice, with most tests, procedures, and medications needing prior approval.
    *   Most practices have numerous full-time employees (FTEs) devoted to processing PA requests.
    *   While many payers provide real-time decision-making, ACC members report an increase in PA use, time spent by clinicians/staff, and resulting delays or cancellations in needed care.
    *   Mandated, payer-directed PA for diagnostic imaging and medications is a huge barrier to patient access in today’s cardiovascular practice.
    *   As ONC considers future rulemaking for PA processes and certification requirements, the ACC is committed to driving PA reform to improve patient care and reduce administrative burden.
    *   The ACC welcomes future conversations with ONC staff on policies that can truly impact patient care delivery.
    *   The ACC again asks ONC to work closely with CMS and other regulatory agencies to ensure policies governing PA and payer requirements align with technical standards promulgated for CEHRT developers.
    *   True interoperability will only occur when all actors in the healthcare system are held to the same standards.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The American College of Cardiology (ACC), representing over 54,000 cardiovascular professionals, supports electronic prior authorization standardization but strongly opposes the increasing volume of prior authorization requirements which create significant administrative burdens and impede patient access to medically necessary care, urging ONC and CMS to prioritize usability, interoperability, and equitable data access for all stakeholders.

- **Type:** Advocacy Group
- **Role/Expertise:** Professional home for the entire cardiovascular care team, leads in health policy, standards, and guidelines; provides professional medical education, disseminates cardiovascular research, operates national registries, and offers cardiovascular accreditation. Represents over 54,000 members.
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Their members (cardiovascular professionals) are directly impacted by burdensome and inefficient prior authorization processes, leading to significant administrative costs, delays in care, and negative impacts on patient health outcomes.
structuredSections.corePosition: We appreciate efforts to standardize electronic prior authorization (e-PA), which is vitally important for streamlining processes. However, we convey strong concern and opposition to the ever-growing prior authorization requirements themselves, as they create significant barriers to medically necessary care that negatively impact patients and providers, regardless of process improvements. We urge for a more judicious and selective application of prior authorization.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Standardize data exchange to streamline the prior authorization process.
- Minimize variability in payer-specific prior authorization processes.
- Be more judicious with the deployment of prior authorization, especially for services with high approval rates.
- Review existing prior authorization requirements for elimination.
- Require certified health IT to enable electronic determination of prior authorization necessity and specific requirements *without* initiating a request.
- Ensure any required capabilities for Certified Health IT are thoughtfully deployed into established clinical workflows, avoiding layering on top of existing systems.
- Add usability and workflow incorporation metrics into the certification program for Certified EHR Technology (CEHRT).
- Require prior authorization standards at the certification level, not relying on staff-level implementation, to ensure equitable and effective e-PA in all clinical settings.
- Work with CMS to ensure certification requirements align with efforts to promote interoperability and address market gaps.
- Make prior authorization decision information available to patients via APIs.
- Expand patient access API requirements to include Medicare Advantage plans.
- Include prescription drug and covered outpatient drug information in any API sharing requirements.
- Ensure payers cannot deny use of or access to APIs based on whether the provider is under contract with the payer, to prevent information blocking.
- Thoughtfully consider ways to allow patients to control access to their data while still allowing providers to access important health information necessary for continuity of care and treatment.
- Work closely with CMS and other regulatory agencies to ensure policies governing prior authorization and payer requirements align with technical standards promulgated for CEHRT developers.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- The ever-growing prior authorization requirements across payers are creating significant barriers to medically necessary care for patients.
- The variability and complexity of payer-specific prior authorization processes (including third-party vendors) intensify administrative burdens for clinicians and staff.
- Prior authorization processes require dedicated staff, significant time away from direct patient care, and involve endless requests, telephone calls, faxes, denials, and appeals.
- Delays in prior authorization responses frequently lead to appointment or procedure cancellations, rescheduling, or multiple patient visits.
- Long approval times (typically 2-14 days, sometimes 15-31+ days) force patients to abandon treatment altogether.
- The perception among clinicians is that high approval rates after inordinate delays suggest the goal of prior authorization is to discourage healthcare consumption rather than ensure high-quality care.
- External applications and portals, while technically meeting functional requirements, do little to reduce provider burdens, complicate workflows, introduce cybersecurity risks, and increase costs.
- Smaller practices, independent practices, and community clinics often lack the IT expertise or funds to implement complex standards-based approaches effectively, relying solely on CEHRT vendors.
- Payers denying API access based on provider contract status creates information blocking, hindering interoperability.
- The proposed patient opt-in process for provider access APIs could lead to providers not knowing which patients have opted in, complicating care, especially for dual-eligible patients with segmented information.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- ACC members and staff spend "countless hours and resources" verifying payer authorization requirements via web portals, hotlines, and forms, illustrating the extensive administrative burden.
- The statistic from a 2020 WEDI Prior Authorization survey: only 18% of providers reported having the capability to evaluate if an authorization is required *without* initiating a request.
- A survey by the Regulatory Relief Coalition revealed that prior authorization approvals typically take 2-14 days, but some can take 15 to more than 31 days, sometimes forcing patients to abandon treatment altogether.
- The same survey indicated most physicians must contend with between 11 and 40 prior authorization requests in any given week.
- The observation that high approval rates after inordinate delays drive the perception among clinicians that prior authorization aims to discourage healthcare resource consumption rather than ensure high-quality care.
- Many offices secure authorization just before a patient visit, leading to appointment/procedure cancellations or rescheduling when responses are delayed.
- The current system results in "black box decisions" that lack transparency for patients and providers.
- Most cardiovascular practices have "numerous FTEs devoted to processing prior authorization requests."
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The ACC must convey strong concern and opposition to the ever-growing prior authorization requirements for medical procedures, diagnostic tests, and medications across payers."
- "High approval rates after inordinate delays drive the perception among clinicians that the goal of prior authorization is to discourage appropriate consumption of healthcare resources rather than to ensure high-quality care."
- "True interoperability will only occur when all actors in the health care system are held to the same standards."

submitter: North Carolina Academy of Family Physicians

structuredSections.detailedContent:
-   The North Carolina Academy of Family Physicians (NCAFP), representing 4,300 family physicians, responds to the Request for Information (RFI): Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria.
-   **Prior authorization continues to be a leading cause of physician burden and therefore NCAFP is strongly supportive of efforts to reform and streamline the prior authorization process.**
    -   Family physicians must navigate prior authorization requirements that add significant cost and time burdens.
    -   American Medical Association (AMA) survey of physicians found:
        -   88 percent reported high or extremely high administrative burden.
        -   Practices complete an average of 41 prior authorizations per physician per week.
        -   Practices spend an average of almost two business days a week completing prior authorizations.
        -   40 percent reported securing additional staff exclusively for prior authorization.
    -   NCAFP's own survey indicated:
        -   Prior authorizations were a top area of concern for administrative burden and burnout.
        -   Independent or rural practices reported even higher frustration levels.
        -   The frustration has been exacerbated since the implementation of Medicaid Managed Care in North Carolina.
    -   Prior authorization requirements have grown, including throughout the COVID-19 pandemic.
        -   A recent poll of medical groups found 81 percent felt prior authorization requirements increased since 2020, with no flexibility from health plans during strained access to care.
        -   The burden is compounded by the labor shortage during the pandemic, affecting practices' ability to recruit and retain staff for prior authorization requests.
-   These requirements negatively impact practice workflows and physician wellbeing.
    -   **Prior authorization can harm patients by delaying care, confusing patients, and resulting in reduced adherence to treatment or even discontinuation of treatment, all resulting in increased morbidity and mortality.**
    -   In the same AMA survey, 34 percent of physicians reported that prior authorization has led to a serious adverse event for a patient in their care.
        -   Additional outcomes reported include patient hospitalization, secondary intervention to prevent permanent damage, and permanent bodily damage or death.
    -   Overall, 91 percent of physicians report prior authorization has a negative impact on patient clinical outcomes.
    -   Even when physicians complete prior authorization requirements, payers often do not respond sufficiently or in a timely manner.
        -   California-based L.A. Care failed to address a backlog of more than 9,000 prior authorization requests and over 67,000 complaints or appeals.
        -   These unresolved requests led to negative impacts on patients, including delays in needed care, increased emergency department visits, and even death.
    -   Evidence indicates that prior authorization requirements may be discriminatory and worsen health disparities.
        -   A study examining access to treatment for HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis and a white paper examining the disproportionate impact on cardiovascular care for Black and other patients of color were cited.
        -   Concerns exist that prior authorization requirements can worsen health disparities and create barriers to care for medically underserved patients, patients of color, LGBTQ+ patients, patients in rural areas, and those at risk for poor health outcomes.
    -   Federal oversight and action are needed to address the negative impacts of prior authorizations on patients and physicians.
-   NCAFP applauds ONC for working to automate prior authorizations to lessen physician burden.
    -   **However, this burden and the resulting care delays cannot be eliminated, or even meaningfully reduced, by automating the existing workflows and volumes of prior authorizations.**
    -   Electronic prior authorization (ePA) is just one step in addressing the flaws of utilization management practices.
    -   Comprehensive reform is needed to reduce the volume of prior authorizations and ensure patients’ timely access to care.
    -   NCAFP urges collaboration with CMS and other relevant agencies to review prior authorization from all angles and reduce unnecessary complexity.
    -   NCAFP looks forward to working with HHS to:
        -   Increase oversight of prior authorization.
        -   Reduce the volume of prior authorizations.
        -   Increase transparency of prior authorization requirements.
        -   Streamline prior authorization processes.
-   **Certified Health IT Functionality**
    -   **Do the functional capabilities described include all necessary functionality? Additional capabilities? Should any be excluded or focus on a more limited set?**
    -   NCAFP appreciates the functional capabilities included in the RFI and recommends including all of them in certified health IT to successfully facilitate electronic prior authorization.
    -   ONC should ensure standards make up-to-date prior authorization requirements for all health plans readily available to the physician, minimizing screen navigation.
    -   Specific capabilities recommended:
        -   Identifying when prior authorization is applicable using clinical decision support.
        -   Receiving notification of any changes in applicability.
        -   Identifying specific rules and documentation requirements.
        -   Collecting clinical and administrative documentation needed to complete prior authorization.
        -   Receiving a response from a payer on approval, denial, or need for additional information.
    -   These capabilities are consistent with the joint Prior Authorization and Utilization Management Reform Principles, which NCAFP supports.
        -   These principles state prior authorization and utilization management review processes should be clinically valid, fair, transparent, and efficient.
        -   Utilization management should be based on accurate and up-to-date clinical criteria, and ePA functionalities should make this information readily available to the ordering physician and patients (if a patient access API is widely adopted or required by CMS).
        -   Standards should clearly indicate the expected timeline for a response, ensure physicians can easily access payers’ rationale for denying a request, and outline the appeal process and documentation needed.
-   **Implementation Specifications for Prior Authorization**
    -   **What is the current readiness of the three FHIR-based Da Vinci IGs for adoption as part of certification criteria for health IT?**
    -   NCAFP recommends ONC continue to drive the adoption of standards and implementation guides (IGs) that have been proven in the real-world.
    -   Standards and IGs are important for facilitating the availability and adoption of ePA processes.
    -   Implementing standards before they have been fully vetted and tested can worsen physician burden and care delays.
    -   Only standards and IGs proven effective and adoptable in real-world testing should be candidates for mandatory certification and utilization, including Da Vinci standards.
    -   Real-world testing is the best way to understand if current standards truly automate prior authorization from end to end in a way that reduces administrative burden.
    -   Robust real-world testing can mitigate issues seen with other health IT standards implementation, such as:
        -   Repeated delays in regulatory mandates due to industry readiness or adoptability issues.
        -   Availability of only partial solutions (mandated standards are insufficient for intended market change).
        -   Introduction of physician burden and other unintended consequences.
    -   Include all types of practice settings in real-world testing, from system-based practices to Federally Qualified Health Centers to independent primary care practices.
-   **Impact on Patients**
    -   **How could potential changes reduce the time for patients to receive needed healthcare services, reduce patient non-adherence, and/or lower out-of-pocket costs?**
    -   Adopting ePA standards may help improve patients’ timely access to care.
    -   Existing survey data indicates that prior authorizations cause care delays, abandonment of needed care, and even adverse events.
    -   Implementing prior authorization standards could shorten the wait time between a request and approval.
    -   This could reduce costs if a patient can receive treatment on the same day instead of having to return and pay an additional copay.
    -   However, ePA alone will not eliminate care delays or other negative health impacts.
        -   Patients will likely still wait for treatment.
        -   Payers will likely continue to deny evidence-based services and require peer-to-peer reviews or appeals, which are time-intensive and delay care.
        -   Patients will experience delays if standards are adopted and required before they are fully functional and effective in the real world.
    -   More comprehensive reform is needed to mitigate the negative impacts of prior authorization on patients.
-   **Impact on Physicians**
    -   **To what degree is availability of electronic prior authorization capabilities within certified health IT likely to reduce burden for healthcare providers?**
    -   Once standards are fully implemented and functional, automating prior authorization will help streamline the process, provided EHR vendors and payers have fully adopted and made them readily available.
        -   For example, ePA will minimize navigating across different screens and websites to determine requirements.
    -   That said, ePA alone will not substantially reduce burden for physicians and clinicians.
        -   Physicians will still need to fill out forms, answer payer questions, and complete peer-to-peers or appeals.
    -   While supportive of advancing ePA standards, NCAFP recommends ONC increase its focus on developing and driving the adoption of standards that support high-quality, evidence-based care.
        -   Additional efforts are needed to efficiently bring clinical and financial knowledge to the point of care within the workflow of ordering and decision-making in real-time.
        -   This would support the physician at the time of order creation instead of determining if a service already recommended warrants coverage, which delays care and interferes with the patient-physician relationship.
        -   Clinical decision support (CDS) may help alleviate some burdens but should not be considered a solution or alternative to prior authorization.
    -   NCAFP urges ONC to work with CMS to establish guardrails for payers’ ability to access EHR data and influence medical decision-making.
        -   Concern exists regarding payers automating queries into Certified Electronic Health Record Technology (CEHRT) or inserting information at the point of care (e.g., CDSHooks card), which influences medical decision-making.
        -   A framework is needed to govern how automated queries can be used by payers, as well as additional measures to require transparency.
        -   Guardrails are needed for the ability to study and audit the use of these functions.
        -   ONC should consider a similar framework and data segmentation for how payers ultimately use data pulled for prior authorizations.
        -   Example: This data should never be used when renewing a contract with a physician practice.
-   **To what degree are healthcare providers likely to use these new capabilities across their patient panels? Will additional incentives or requirements be needed? What accompanying documentation or support would be needed?**
    -   Vendor and payer adoption of specific standards must be a prerequisite for any required adoption by physicians.
    -   Physicians depend on EHR vendors to provide functionality and on payers to adopt standards.
    -   Given the significant burden, physicians are expected to be eager to adopt new ePA standards if they enhance efficiency and reduce care delays.
    -   NCAFP recommends against mandating the use of ePA standards by clinicians, as mandating could diminish incentives for CEHRT vendors and payers to implement efficient solutions.
    -   Vendors will charge physician practices for updating EHR systems. The cost could hinder adoption.
    -   HHS should prioritize reducing the cost of adoption to physician practices.
-   **What estimates can providers share about the cost and time (in hours) associated with adopting and implementing electronic prior authorization functionality?**
    -   Adopting and implementing ePA functionality goes beyond just purchasing and implementing new technology.
    -   Practices must factor in additional costs and time for:
        -   Staff trainings.
        -   Increased staff levels or compensation.
        -   Program support and troubleshooting.
    -   Physicians already spend almost two business days per week on prior authorization; implementation of new functionality will intensify that burden as practices adjust.
    -   To optimize adoption of future standards, ONC is urged to minimize the costs passed onto physician practices when updating EHRs for new ePA standards.
-   NCAFP thanks for the opportunity to comment and stands ready to partner with HHS to streamline and reform prior authorization.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The North Carolina Academy of Family Physicians, representing 4,300 family physicians, strongly supports electronic prior authorization as a necessary step but emphasizes that comprehensive reform is essential to reduce the overall volume of prior authorizations, ensure robust real-world testing of standards before mandating, and establish guardrails against undue payer influence to genuinely alleviate physician burden and improve patient access.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
-   **Name/Organization:** North Carolina Academy of Family Physicians (NCAFP)
-   **Type:** Advocacy Group
-   **Role/Expertise:** Represents 4,300 family physicians in North Carolina; advocates for family medicine and patient care quality.
-   **Geographic Scope:** State (North Carolina)
-   **Stake in Issue:** Family physicians and their practices are directly impacted by prior authorization requirements, experiencing significant administrative burden, increased costs, and negative impacts on patient care and outcomes, including burnout.
structuredSections.corePosition: Prior authorization continues to be a leading cause of physician burden and negatively impacts patient care, so we strongly support efforts to reform and streamline this process, including automation. However, automating existing workflows alone is insufficient; comprehensive reform is critically needed to reduce the sheer volume of prior authorizations, ensure timely patient access to care, and safeguard the patient-physician relationship from undue payer influence.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
-   Include all functional capabilities described in the RFI in certified health IT to successfully facilitate electronic prior authorization.
    -   Ensure standards make up-to-date prior authorization requirements for all health plans readily available, minimizing screen navigation.
    -   Specifically, this includes identifying when prior authorization is applicable, notifying of changes, identifying rules and documentation, collecting necessary documentation, and receiving timely payer responses.
    -   Standards should indicate expected response timelines, provide easy access to denial rationales, and outline appeal processes.
-   Drive the adoption of standards and implementation guides (IGs) that have been proven in real-world testing.
    -   Only standards and IGs, including Da Vinci standards, that are proven effective and adoptable in real-world testing should be candidates for mandatory certification.
    -   Conduct robust real-world testing across all practice settings (e.g., system-based, FQHCs, independent primary care) to verify true end-to-end automation and burden reduction.
-   Collaborate with CMS and other relevant agencies to comprehensively review prior authorization from all angles and reduce unnecessary complexity.
    -   Increase oversight of prior authorization practices.
    -   Reduce the overall volume of prior authorizations required.
    -   Increase transparency of prior authorization requirements.
    -   Streamline prior authorization processes.
-   Increase focus on developing and driving the adoption of standards that support high-quality, evidence-based care by bringing clinical and financial knowledge to the point of care in real-time within the workflow.
-   Work with CMS to establish guardrails for payers' ability to access EHR data and influence medical decision-making.
    -   Develop a framework governing how automated queries can be used by payers, requiring transparency.
    -   Implement measures for studying and auditing the use of functions where payers insert information at the point of care (e.g., CDSHooks cards).
    -   Create a framework, including data segmentation, for how payers can use data pulled for prior authorizations, ensuring it is not used for purposes like contract renewal with physician practices.
-   Do not mandate the use of electronic prior authorization standards by clinicians without first ensuring vendor and payer adoption of specific standards.
    -   Mandating clinician use could diminish incentives for vendors and payers to implement efficient and effective solutions.
-   Prioritize reducing the cost of adoption for physician practices for implementing new prior authorization functionality.
    -   Minimize costs passed onto practices when updating EHRs, as practices incur additional expenses for staff training, increased staffing, and troubleshooting beyond technology purchase.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
-   **High Administrative Burden:** Prior authorization is a leading cause of physician burden, with an average of 41 prior authorizations per physician per week and almost two business days per week spent on these activities, contributing significantly to burnout.
-   **Exacerbated by Policy Changes:** The implementation of Medicaid Managed Care in North Carolina has intensified members' frustration with prior authorizations.
-   **Increased Requirements During Pandemic:** Prior authorization requirements increased during the COVID-19 pandemic, compounding existing burdens and labor shortages, despite the strain on healthcare access.
-   **Negative Patient Outcomes:** Prior authorizations cause significant patient harm, including care delays, confusion, reduced treatment adherence, treatment discontinuation, increased morbidity and mortality, and serious adverse events (reported by 34% of physicians), with instances leading to hospitalization and even death.
-   **Untimely Payer Responses:** Payers frequently fail to respond to prior authorization requests in a timely manner, as evidenced by a backlog of over 9,000 requests and 67,000 complaints from one California-based payer, resulting in severe patient impacts.
-   **Worsening Health Disparities:** Prior authorization requirements may be discriminatory and worsen health disparities, disproportionately affecting medically underserved populations, patients of color, LGBTQ+ patients, and those in rural areas.
-   **Limitations of Automation Alone:** Electronic prior authorization (ePA) alone will not substantially reduce physician burden or eliminate care delays, as it does not address the fundamental issue of excessive prior authorization volume or the time-intensive appeal processes.
-   **Risks of Premature Standard Adoption:** Implementing standards before they are fully vetted and tested in real-world settings can worsen physician burden, lead to care delays, result in partial solutions, and introduce unintended consequences.
-   **Payer Influence on Clinical Decision-Making:** Concerns exist regarding payers' ability to automate queries into certified EHRs or insert information at the point of care, potentially influencing physicians' medical decision-making processes without adequate guardrails or transparency.
-   **Financial Burden of Adoption:** EHR vendors will charge practices for system updates to implement new prior authorization standards, adding significant costs for practices beyond technology purchase, including expenses for staff training, increased staffing, and troubleshooting, which could hinder adoption.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
-   The comment provides specific quantitative evidence of the administrative burden, citing AMA survey data (e.g., 88% high/extremely high burden, 41 PAs per physician per week, 2 business days per week spent on PAs, 40% practices needing dedicated PA staff) and corroborating it with their own NCAFP survey, particularly highlighting increased frustration in independent and rural practices.
-   It offers a concrete, local example of how policy changes exacerbate existing issues, specifically mentioning that Medicaid Managed Care implementation in North Carolina has worsened members' frustration with prior authorizations.
-   The comment powerfully illustrates the severe patient consequences of prior authorization failures by citing the case of California-based L.A. Care, which failed to address a massive backlog of requests and complaints, leading to "delays in needed care, increased emergency department visits, and even death."
-   It introduces the critical insight that prior authorization requirements may be discriminatory and worsen health disparities, referencing studies that examined impacts on HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis and cardiovascular care for patients of color.
-   The comment provides a nuanced and important counter-perspective, stating that while automation is supported, "this burden and the resulting care delays cannot be eliminated, or even meaningfully reduced, by automating the existing workflows and volumes of prior authorizations. Electronic prior authorization is just one step..." emphasizing the need for comprehensive reform to reduce *volume*.
-   It raises a forward-thinking concern about data governance and physician autonomy by recommending "guardrails" for payers' ability to access EHR data and influence medical decision-making at the point of care, specifically mentioning "CDSHooks cards" and stressing that data should "never be used when renewing a contract with a physician practice."
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
-   "Not only do these requirements negatively impact practice workflows and physician wellbeing, but evidence also shows prior authorization can harm patients by delaying care, confusing patients, and resulting in reduced adherence to treatment or even discontinuation of treatment, all resulting in increased morbidity and mortality."
-   "However, this burden and the resulting care delays cannot be eliminated, or even meaningfully reduced, by automating the existing workflows and volumes of prior authorizations. Electronic prior authorization is just one step in addressing the flaws of utilization management practices and comprehensive reform is needed to reduce the volume of prior authorizations and ensure patients’ timely access to care."
-   "By allowing payers to automate query into CEHRT or to insert information at the point of care (e.g., CDSHooks card), they are able to influence physicians’ medical decision-making process."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   **RE: Request for Information: Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria**
    *   On behalf of the American Society of Nuclear Cardiology (ASNC), thank you for the opportunity to provide feedback.
    *   ASNC is a 4,500-member professional medical society.
        *   It provides a variety of continuing medical education programs related to nuclear cardiology and cardiovascular computed tomography.
        *   It develops standards and guidelines for training and practice.
        *   It promotes accreditation and certification within the nuclear cardiology field.
        *   It is a major advocate for furthering research and excellence in nuclear cardiology and cardiovascular computed tomography.
*   **Implementation Specifications to Support Electronic Prior Authorization Capabilities**
    *   ASNC is pleased that the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) and the Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT (ONC) recognize the immense resources and administrative burden that go into prior authorization processes each year.
        *   In addition to administrative hassle, physicians report care delays and treatment abandonment as serious consequences of prior authorization on patient care.
    *   We support the aims of the ONC’s Health IT Certification program and applaud the effort to standardize processes around ordering services.
    *   We understand that the certification is a voluntary program that health IT developers can obtain for their products.
    *   However, we have concerns regarding the da Vinci Coverage Requirements Coverage Implementation Guide.
        *   The purpose of the implementation guide is to “define workflow whereby clinical IT systems can query coverage requirements from payer IT systems at the time treatment decisions are made.”
        *   ONC cites several categories of information that it envisions as the kinds of data that may be shared using an implementation guide, such as:
            *   Updated coverage information.
            *   Alternative preferred/first line/lower-cost services/products.
            *   Updated clinical information for clinical decision support.
    *   We have concerns about the use of lists of alternative or preferred services within the implementation guide.
        *   ASNC maintains its long-held position that where two services are clinically appropriate, clinical decision making regarding the test or service for a given patient should remain solely with the physician.
        *   This decision should be based on:
            *   The patient’s condition.
            *   The physician’s clinical judgment.
            *   The quality of the imaging services available in that locale.
    *   Finally, we urge CMS and ONC to continue in a dialogue with national medical societies like ASNC.
        *   This dialogue should aim to improve the prior authorization process with electronic prior authorization standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria.
        *   It should also ensure that efforts to streamline processes can be implemented in a variety of settings and do not add additional administrative morass to an already complex program.
    *   ASNC is pleased to provide comment and would welcome any questions or requests for additional information.
        *   Contact Georgia Lawrence, Director, Regulatory Affairs at glawrence@asnc.org.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The American Society of Nuclear Cardiology supports efforts to streamline electronic prior authorization but strongly opposes implementation guides that suggest alternative services, emphasizing that clinical decision-making must remain solely with the physician.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** American Society of Nuclear Cardiology (ASNC)
- **Type:** Professional Medical Society
- **Role/Expertise:** Represents 4,500 members specializing in nuclear cardiology and cardiovascular computed tomography, providing continuing medical education, developing standards and guidelines, promoting accreditation and certification, and advocating for research and excellence.
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Advocates on behalf of its physician members who face significant administrative burden, care delays, and treatment abandonment due to prior authorization processes, particularly concerned about policies that could infringe on physician clinical autonomy.
structuredSections.corePosition: We are pleased that CMS and ONC recognize the immense administrative burden of prior authorization and support efforts to standardize processes through electronic prior authorization. However, we have significant concerns about proposed implementation guides, particularly those that suggest lists of alternative or preferred services. We maintain that clinical decision-making for a patient's care must remain solely with the physician, based on their clinical judgment and the patient's specific condition.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Continue a dialogue with national medical societies like ASNC to improve the prior authorization process with electronic prior authorization standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria.
  - Ensure that efforts to streamline processes can be implemented in a variety of settings.
  - Ensure that these efforts do not add additional administrative morass to an already complex program.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Concerns regarding the "da Vinci Coverage Requirements Coverage Implementation Guide."
  - Specifically concerned about the use of lists of "alternative preferred/first line/lower-cost services/products" within the implementation guide.
  - Believes that such lists would undermine the physician's sole authority in clinical decision-making when multiple services are clinically appropriate.
- Current prior authorization processes lead to significant administrative burden for physicians.
  - Physicians report care delays as a serious consequence.
  - Physicians report treatment abandonment as a serious consequence for patients.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Physicians report that prior authorization processes result in serious consequences for patient care, specifically care delays and treatment abandonment.
- The society holds a "long-held position" that clinical decision-making should remain *solely* with the physician, based on patient condition, clinical judgment, and local service quality, even when multiple services are clinically appropriate.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "In addition to administrative hassle, physicians report care delays and treatment abandonment as serious consequences of prior authorization on patient care."
- "ASNC maintains its long held position that where two services are clinically appropriate, clinical decision making regarding the test or service for a given patient should remain solely with the physician based on the patient’s condition, physician’s clinical judgement, and quality of the imaging services available in that locale."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   Cigna welcomes the opportunity to respond to the request for information (RFI) seeking input regarding electronic Prior Authorization (ePA) standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria that could be adopted within the Office of the National Coordinator (ONC) Health IT Certification Program.
*   Cigna Corporation is a global health service organization focused on improving health, well-being, and peace of mind, with over 175 million customer relationships in 30+ countries.
    *   Within the United States, Cigna provides medical coverage to approximately 14 million Americans in the commercial group health plan market, predominantly self-insured.
    *   We serve about 235,000 people in the individual Affordable Care Act insurance segment.
    *   We also serve more than 4.5 million people through Medicare Advantage, Medicare Prescription Drug Program, and Medicare Supplemental products.
    *   Our health services business, Evernorth, manages total healthcare outcomes.
        *   Evernorth's Pharmacy+ includes specialty pharmacy (Accredo Health Group Inc.), home delivery pharmacy (ESI Mail Pharmacy Service Inc. and Express Scripts Pharmacy Inc.), and drug distribution (Priority Healthcare Distribution Inc. d/b/a CuraScript SD Specialty Distribution).
        *   Included in Evernorth is eviCore, a specialty benefits management company that evaluates healthcare service requests against evidence-based medical guidelines to ensure patients receive the right care.
*   Cigna supports efforts to streamline healthcare information sharing among payers, providers, and beneficiaries to reduce burden and improve beneficiary health.
    *   Prior Authorization (PA) allows health insurers to promote safe, timely, evidence-based, affordable, and efficient care by reducing inappropriate or low-value services.
    *   Cigna continuously works to streamline information sharing; for example, eviCore adjudicates nearly 100,000 PA requests daily, with 70% of decisions rendered at the time of request or within 1 hour, and 98% within three business days.
    *   Increased interoperability will improve PA decision speed and accuracy.
    *   Cigna and eviCore work with providers to integrate tools into clinical workflows and identify needs for training or workflow integration.
    *   Provider adoption of ePA can be increased by using tools like eviCore’s intelliPath®, an ePA solution that simplifies and automates PA with connectivity, real-time decisions, and simplified clinical surveys.
        *   intelliPath® integrates with major electronic health record (EHR) vendors, reducing provider costs and delays.
    *   PA programs ensure medically necessary care in appropriate settings per evidence-based guidelines.
    *   A 2020 AHIP survey found that over 90% of commercial enrollees are in plans limiting PA to less than 25% of medical services, and close to 85% limit PA to less than 10% of prescription medications.
    *   Pharmacy PA addresses drug-on-drug interactions, quantity limitations, addiction risk mitigation, or step therapy rules, typically happening electronically and in real time.

*   Comments on RFI on “ePA Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification”
    *   Cigna is supportive of ONC’s efforts to use Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) to facilitate ePA.
    *   Cigna actively participates in industry standards development, including:
        *   Health Level Seven (HL7) FHIR at Scale Task Force Accelerator, driving implementation guides (IGs).
        *   Da Vinci project, driving payer-provider value-based care IGs using HL7 FHIR.
        *   mCODE (minimal Common Oncology Data Elements) FHIR IG, specifically contributing to CodeX PA in Oncology Use Case to improve PA for breast and prostate cancers, exploring Da Vinci IGs with mCODE for expedited, automated, integrated PA.
    *   Cigna participated in AHIP’s Fast Prior Authorization Technology Highway (Fast PATH) initiative, which found that clinicians using ePA for most PAs had the greatest burden reduction.
        *   Further gains require increasing provider adoption and sustained use of ePA.
    *   Cigna agrees that industry-wide adoption of ePA based on existing national standards has the potential to streamline and improve the process.
        *   However, successful implementation requires collaboration and coordination across many stakeholders in a complex ecosystem.
        *   One-sided requirements (e.g., only requiring payers to build APIs) will not achieve broad adoption.
        *   Health insurers can build APIs, but they have minimal value if EHR vendors do not build necessary connections and clinicians are not incentivized to adopt them.
        *   To ensure wide-scale adoption, ONC should work with CMS and stakeholders to outline a roadmap for ePA that leverages the Certification Program, Information Blocking rule, and Interoperability rules for consistent requirements on all stakeholders.
        *   This roadmap should demonstrate a phased approach to ePA implementation, ensuring standards and IGs are tested and mature and allowing time for technology development.
        *   ONC and CMS should phase in ePA starting with highest priority and volume needs.

*   Cigna Recommends:
    *   ONC develop a process to test ePA processes, ensuring integration of multiple proprietary systems, including utilization management (UM) providers.
    *   ePA processes occur only through FHIR, with a complete transition to an end-to-end FHIR solution.
        *   The HIPAA 278 transaction standard has low adoption because it often does not allow for all necessary supporting clinical information.
        *   Enabling FHIR through a "lowest common denominator" HIPAA 278 transaction will not achieve desired outcomes due to translation burdens.
    *   ONC and CMS support policy changes for the PA process to occur completely within the FHIR framework, without HIPAA X12 transactions or temporary regulatory exceptions.
        *   If HIPAA X12 transactions remain, it would be challenging to synchronize changes in X12 278 with FHIR resources (CRD, DTR, PAS).
        *   FHIR is built on a modern technology framework, making it easier to find qualified technical staff and providing widely available, often free, tooling, which lowers barriers and costs.
    *   In the interim, ONC and CMS allow X12 translation for current users, while expanding the pilot program and permitting exemptions from X12 transactions for those fully moving to FHIR.
*   ePA has the potential to reduce PA burden but will not address all concerns, such as coverage guidelines set by employer groups.
    *   The Fast PATH initiative found ePA did not impact approval rates, indicating that while it improves transparency and decision time, the underlying rules for PA do not change.

*   Questions on ePA Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria
    *   I. Certified Health IT Functionality
        *   Cigna urges CMS and ONC to establish specific requirements for EHR developers to include ePA functions in Certified Electronic Health Record Technology (CEHRT) and for providers to use such technology as part of the Merit-based Incentive Payment System (MIPS) and information blocking regulations.
        *   Cigna Recommends:
            *   ONC establish specific requirements for EHR developers to include these functions in CEHRT and for both providers and EHR developers as part of the Information Blocking regulations.
            *   ONC expand required functions to address identity resolution and advance industry adoption of ONC FAST solutions for security and directory issues in ePA.
            *   ONC consider additional functionality to make ePA more person-centered, including notifying patients of PA status, allowing opt-in/out for notifications, and providing patient access to PA data.
            *   ONC ensure ePA solutions do not duplicate or complicate other consumer-facing tools like the Advanced Explanation of Benefits from the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021.
            *   ONC implement a process to test ePA and ensure its ability to work across multiple systems (providers, insurers, UM companies).
            *   ONC work with CMS to include incentives for providers within the Advancing Care Information Performance category of MIPS to use ePA technology, parallel to payer API requirements.
        *   Cigna supports ONC Functional Capabilities with feedback and identifies missing elements:
            *   Required functions/capabilities within ePA:
                *   Identify when ePA is applicable, using clinical decision support (CDS) or user input, and send/receive change notifications.
                *   Query a payer API for ePA requirements and identify real-time rules/documentation; this should also include querying a provider’s health IT system to identify patient data that might alleviate ePA.
                *   Collect clinical and administrative documentation (electronic forms/templates) from a health IT system.
                *   Electronically submit completed documentation to a payer's API with supporting information.
                *   Receive a response from a payer regarding approval, denial (with reason), alternate recommendations, or need for additional information.
                *   Query a payer's system for updates on a pending ePA request and receive a reason for pending status.
                *   Effectively capture and preserve digital signatures, ensure data integrity, and support features for payer administrative requirements (though provenance tracing of electronic transactions may negate explicit need).
            *   Missing functions/capabilities that should be added:
                *   Ability for patients to query ePA status for visibility and transparency.
                *   Develop suitable process/technology for transition from paper/legacy HIPAA 278 to FHIR-based approaches, with industry agreement on ePA classification for phased implementation.
                *   Ability to clearly identify whether a pharmacy service is covered under pharmacy or medical benefit as a dependency for consistent implementation.
                *   Ability for FHIR at Scale standards to enable lookup of provider/payer API FHIR endpoints and security standards for scaled FHIR use (both underway/balloted in HL7), and underlying infrastructure for scaled API access.
    *   Should ONC adopt certification criterion for PA that accounts for full HIPAA compliant workflow (including FHIR to X12 translation) or only up to translation point?
        *   Cigna Recommends:
            *   ONC only adopt certification criteria for workflows up to the point of translation and utilize modern formats (e.g., JSON) for data standards.
            *   Identify an approach to move away from the X12 HIPAA 278 transaction legacy process.
                *   HIPAA 278 is insufficient for complex health treatment dialog, often acting as a batch transaction that doesn't support conversational exchange.
                *   It frequently requires basic data that doesn't meet the need, leading to "pending for review" status.
                *   It's often treated as a claim-enabling transaction but needs to be clinical.
        *   Removing the HIPAA 278 "ceiling" would reduce PA automation complexity.
        *   Dissatisfaction with HIPAA 278 has created many varied portals that disrupt provider workflow, yet are required by HIPAA.
