        *   Healthcare attachments are used for a wide range of operations and administrative workflows beyond prior authorization. Are either of the standards discussed above commonly used in other administrative or operations transactions? Would there be a burden or benefit to using either, or both, standards in light of other administrative or operations workflows? Are there additional standards or implementation specifications ONC should consider that are in common use for healthcare attachments used in other administrative or operations workflows?
            *   Both CDA-based attachments and FHIR-based documentation exchange are used in other transactions such as electronic submission of medical documentation, audit reviews, utilization management, and quality measurement for external reporting.
            *   While it is clear that FHIR-based document exchange is appropriate for some transactions (such as ePA), it is not clear at this point the degree to which FHIR-based document exchange can be used for other transactions, such as claims, with the same level of effectiveness as HL7 CDA.
            *   Thus, we recommend adopting FHIR-based standards for ePA to promote operational efficiency in that transaction.
            *   We do not believe there would be a burden to using FHIR-based documentation exchange for some transactions and CDA-based attachments for other transactions, as the workflows and methods for achieving these various transactions are different.
    *   Impact on Patients
        *   How could potential changes to the Certification Program to better support prior authorization positively impact healthcare consumers?
            *   Potential changes to the ONC Health IT Certification Program to better support ePA could positively impact health care consumers by improving efficiencies in the PA process which may lead to more prompt access to the appropriate level of care and improved patient care outcomes.
        *   How could potential changes reduce the time for patients to receive needed healthcare services, reduce patient non-adherence, and/or lower out-of-pocket costs?
            *   When properly implemented, the CRDs outlined in the DaVinci implementation guides will provide alternative options for providers to direct patients to lower out-of-pocket services.
        *   Besides the provider to payer interactions discussed in this RFI, is there additional functionality that could be added to the Certification Program that would better support patients' participation in the prior authorization process?
            *   As noted in the introduction of this letter, we recommend ONC consider additional functionality related to transparency of prior authorization requirements that would encourage patient participation in health care planning and the prior authorization process.
            *   This includes providers being able to notify patients when a PA request has been submitted, allow patients to opt-in or -out of notifications regarding PA requests, notify patients when and what data was provided to support a PA request, and align the PA process with advanced EOB requirements under the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2021.
    *   Impact on Providers
        *   To what degree is availability of electronic prior authorization capabilities within certified health IT likely to reduce burden for healthcare providers who currently engage in prior authorization activities?
            *   Availability of ePA will reduce administrative burden on providers and support staff by streamlining communications and exchange of information necessary for approval of services.
            *   CMS and ONC should consider appropriate incentive provisions for providers to reward the use of ePA, such as inclusion in the Merit-based Incentive Payment System (MIPS) program, Promoting Interoperability program, Appropriate Use Criteria program, and other programs.
        *   To what degree are healthcare providers likely to use these new capabilities across their patient panels? Will additional incentives or requirements be needed to ensure healthcare providers effectively use these capabilities? What accompanying documentation or support would be needed to ensure that technology capabilities are implemented in ways that effectively improve clinical workflows?
            *   Providers will utilize new capabilities if they are easier to use, more effective and more efficient than online portals or other methods currently in use.
        *   What estimates can providers share about the cost and time (in hours) associated with adopting and implementing electronic prior authorization functionality as part of care delivery processes?
            *   No Comments.
    *   Impact on Developers
        *   No Comments.
    *   Payer Implementation
        *   How could the Certification Program support the technology needs of healthcare payers in implementing electronic prior authorization? Should ONC consider payer workflows in the development of certification criteria to support the potential use of certified Health IT Modules by healthcare payers? Would the availability of certified Health IT Modules supporting these workflows reduce the burden for healthcare payers of engaging with healthcare providers in prior authorization processes?
            *   We recommend that ONC take the following actions to support the needs of health care payers in implementing ePA:
                *   Outline a roadmap for ePA that leverages the Certification program, Information Blocking rule, and Interoperability rules to communicate consistent requirements for all stakeholders.
                *   Establish specific requirements for EHR developers to include ePA functions in their technologies as part of the Certification Program, and for both providers and EHR developers as part of the Information Blocking regulations.
                *   Work with CMS to include incentives for providers within the Advancing Care Information Performance within the MIPS program to use ePA technology.
                *   Work with CMS to allow health insurers to include the costs associated with compliance of ePA requirements as Quality Improvement Activity (QIA) expenses in the Medical Loss Ratio (MLR) calculations.
        *   To what extent would healthcare payers be likely to use these certified Health IT Modules if they were available? To what extent are health IT developers likely to seek certification for Health IT Modules supporting payer workflows if these certification criteria were available?
            *   Health care payers are likely to use the certified Health IT Modules if they are available and improve PA and UM authorization automation.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Kaiser Permanente, a large integrated healthcare delivery system that is both provider and payer, supports electronic prior authorization (ePA) but urges ONC to adopt an end-to-end FHIR-based solution with mandatory testing, consistent implementation guidelines, and consumer-centric features to ensure true interoperability and reduce burden.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Kaiser Permanente
- **Type:** Organization
- **Role/Expertise:** The largest private integrated health care delivery system in the U.S., delivering health care to over 12 million members. Comprised of providers and payers, uniquely positioned to understand prior authorization challenges and benefits. Jamie Ferguson is Vice President, Health IT Strategy and Policy.
- **Geographic Scope:** National (U.S.), specifically operating in eight states and the District of Columbia.
- **Stake in Issue:** As an organization that functions as both a healthcare provider and a health plan, Kaiser Permanente is directly impacted by prior authorization processes and standards, influencing both administrative burden and patient care delivery.
structuredSections.corePosition: We generally support the inclusion of a core set of functional capabilities for electronic prior authorization (ePA) in the ONC Health IT Certification program to advance administrative simplification, automation, and standardization. However, we urge ONC to consider the impact of new ePA-related EHR functional capabilities on health plan information systems, and not just on providers, prior to adoption, advocating strongly for an end-to-end FHIR-based solution and greater consumer transparency.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Work with the industry and standards development organizations (SDOs) to develop operating guidelines that specify the prior authorization process and implementation.
- Add the following consumer-centered functional capabilities to ePA:
    - Give providers the ability to notify patients when a prior authorization request has been submitted.
    - Allow the patient to opt-in or -out of notifications regarding prior authorization requests.
    - Notify patients when and what data was provided to support a prior authorization request.
    - Align the prior authorization process with the 2021 Consolidated Appropriations Act’s (CAA) Advanced EOB (AEOB) requirements.
- Support adoption of the latest versions of the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci implementation guides for coverage discovery (CRD), documentation template and coverage rule (DTR) and prior authorization support (PAS).
- Support development and adoption of common terminology and nomenclature for CRD, DTR, and PAS standards, along with HL7 Clinical Quality Language and FHIR questionnaires.
- Work with SDOs and the industry to update, test, and finalize any recommended standards, codes, nomenclature, and guides prior to national adoption.
- Fully test the latest version of the three HL7 FHIR Da Vinci implementation guides across different health plan scenarios prior to adoption.
- Specify the same version of the implementation guides to be used by both EHR systems and health plans to prevent interoperability risks.
- Ensure that CDS Hooks has reached an appropriate level of maturity and is fully tested prior to adoption.
- Implement regulatory action to permit ePA to occur via end-to-end FHIR transactions.
- In the interim, CMS should consider using discretionary enforcement to allow ePA via either end-to-end FHIR or X12 so entities are not required to implement both standards in the same process.
- Include all necessary functional capabilities for ePA in certified Health IT Modules.
- The certification criterion should account for the full, HIPAA-compliant workflow with the expectation payers share information regarding previous PA decisions.
- Combine certain ePA functions into single certification criteria where beneficial, such as:
    - Identifying when PA is applicable, querying a payer’s API for PA requirements, and querying a payer’s system for updates.
    - Collecting internal clinical/administrative documentation and electronically submitting it, and receiving responses.
    - Capturing persisting digital signatures and enabling data integrity over time.
- CMS should facilitate and participate in testing of the FHIR-based Da Vinci IGs in Medicare, Medicaid, and other public programs.
- Set a timeframe for full national adoption of IGs no sooner than 12 months after the final rule is issued.
- Propose certification criteria based on the FHIR Release 4 normative standard with an implementation timeline of no less than 12 months.
- Develop a series of transitional steps for adopting newer versions of the base standard and implementation guides progressively, ensuring backward compatibility.
- Adopt FHIR Documents specifically for use in ePA, and HL7 C-CDA R2 and CDA Attachments Implementation Guide for other transactions like claims.
- ONC should wait until the FHIR-based Da Vinci implementation guides have been fully tested in different provider and payer use cases before adopting or proposing certification criteria to support healthcare attachments transactions for prior authorization.
- ONC should outline a roadmap for ePA leveraging the Certification program, Information Blocking rule, and Interoperability rules to communicate consistent requirements for all stakeholders.
- Establish specific requirements for EHR developers to include ePA functions in their technologies as part of the Certification Program, and for both providers and EHR developers as part of the Information Blocking regulations.
- Work with CMS to include incentives for providers to use ePA within programs like MIPS, Promoting Interoperability, and Appropriate Use Criteria.
- Work with CMS to allow health insurers to include ePA compliance costs as Quality Improvement Activity (QIA) expenses in Medical Loss Ratio (MLR) calculations.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- The potential impact of new ePA-related EHR functional capabilities on health plan information systems needs to be considered, not just their impact on providers, prior to adoption.
- Variability in implementation processes for ePA will contravene efforts to achieve interoperability.
- The latest versions of the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci implementation guides have not been fully tested across different health plan scenarios.
- Interoperability risks may occur if different versions of implementation guides are adopted for EHR systems and health plans.
- The Clinical Decision Support (CDS) Hooks functionality has a limited level of maturity and still requires testing to support ePA workflows.
- Supporting both FHIR and X12 standards in the same prior authorization process, as potentially required under HIPAA, may lead to data integrity issues due to data loss during translation (e.g., from FHIR Questionnaire to X12 278 and 275).
- It is costly and inefficient for providers and payers to maintain and execute both FHIR and X12 standards in the same process, and translate back-and-forth.
- It is less clear whether the use of FHIR Documents will be equally effective and efficient if used in other health care transactions such as claim submission and payment, compared to HL7 CDA.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- As a healthcare organization comprising both providers and payers, we are uniquely situated to understand the complexities, challenges, and benefits associated with prior authorization.
- We have observed that "variability in implementation processes will contravene efforts to achieve interoperability," highlighting the need for standardized guidelines beyond just functional capabilities.
- Our experience suggests that maintaining and translating between both FHIR and X12 standards within the same ePA process is both "costly and inefficient for providers and payers" and can lead to "data integrity issues."
- We find that "it is simpler to implement one system for ePA versus multiple systems with different certified functionalities," indicating a preference for consolidated, comprehensive certification criteria.
- We anticipate that "the FHIR-based Da Vinci implementation guides will better accommodate improvements over time to meet evolving payer and provider needs," signifying confidence in FHIR's long-term adaptability.
- Our dual role reveals that while "FHIR-based document exchange is appropriate for some transactions (such as ePA), it is not clear at this point the degree to which FHIR-based document exchange can be used for other transactions, such as claims, with the same level of effectiveness as HL7 CDA," demonstrating a nuanced understanding of standard applicability.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "As a health care organization comprised of providers and payers, we are uniquely situated to understand the challenges and benefits associated with prior authorization (PA)."
- "We are concerned that variability in implementation processes will contravene efforts to achieve interoperability."
- "We are concerned that supporting both FHIR and X12 standards in the same process, as might be required under current HIPAA transaction standard requirements, may lead to data integrity issues."
- "Additionally, it is costly and inefficient for providers and payers to maintain and execute both standards in the same process, and translate back-and-forth between FHIR and X12."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   Allina Health is responding to the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria Request for Information (RFI).
    *   The RFI aims to inform ONC's policymaking and obtain stakeholder feedback as it considers promulgating regulations on electronic prior authorization standards.
    *   Allina Health offers comments for ONC’s consideration as it evaluates this arena and the possible need for future regulations.
*   Allina Health's organizational profile:
    *   Dedicated to the prevention and treatment of illness and enhancing the greater health of individuals, families, and communities throughout Minnesota and western Wisconsin.
    *   Serves communities by providing exceptional care through illness prevention, health restoration, and comfort to patients.
    *   A not-for-profit health care system with 29,000 employees.
    *   Cares for patients from beginning to end-of-life through 90+ clinics, 11 hospitals, 15 outpatient pharmacies, specialty care centers, and specialty medical services including home care, senior transitions, hospice care, and emergency medical transportation services.
    *   Focused on eliminating systemic health disparities and unnecessary variations in quality of care to improve community health.
*   Background
    *   Prior authorization is a process whereby a provider, on behalf of a patient, requests approval from the patient’s insurer before delivering a treatment or service.
    *   Although designed to help ensure patients receive optimal care, implementation of prior authorization requirements has been inconsistent and confusing, created barriers and delays in care, and driven up costs for the health care system.
    *   In 2020, ONC identified many of these challenges with the prior authorization process in its Strategy on Reducing Regulatory and Administrative Burden Relating to the Use of Health Information Technology (IT) and Electronic Health Records (EHRs), including:
        *   Difficulty in determining whether an item or service requires prior authorization.
        *   Difficulty in determining payer-specific prior authorization requirements for those items and services.
        *   Inefficient use of provider and staff time to navigate communications channels such as fax, telephone, and various web portals.
        *   Unpredictable and lengthy amounts of time to receive payer decisions.
    *   ONC is looking to leverage its existing Certification Program to address the shortcomings of prior authorization.
*   Certified Health IT Functionality
    *   The ONC Health IT Certification Program is a voluntary program that defines requirements for health IT and the process by which health IT may become evaluated, tested (if required), certified, and maintain its certification.
        *   Launched in 2010 to support the Promoting Interoperability Programs, the Program has now expanded to other government and non-government programs.
        *   It functions to allow health IT developers to obtain ONC certification for their health IT products by meeting established standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria adopted through rulemaking by the Secretary of Health and Human Services (HHS).
    *   In this RFI, ONC offers minimum criteria it is considering for certification of technology vendors that would enable entities to easily identify components of prior authorization, seeking feedback on appropriate minimum capabilities needed for certified health IT systems to successfully interact with payers to complete prior authorization tasks.
    *   ONC lists the following as a core set of functionalities for which to consider certification:
        *   Identify when prior authorization is applicable.
        *   Query a payer’s application programming interface (API) for prior authorization requirements.
        *   Collect clinical and administrative documentation needed for completion.
        *   Electronically submit documentation to a payer’s API.
        *   Receive response from a payer regarding the decision.
        *   Query a payer’s system for updates.
        *   Effectively capture digital signatures (or other indications of provider review).
    *   Allina Health believes the listed functionalities are an appropriate baseline for ONC to consider for certification.
    *   We hope ONC’s Certification Program will drive developers to incorporate the necessary functionalities and capabilities to ease the burden associated with navigating prior authorization processes.
    *   Our primary challenges with managing prior authorization include the following:
        *   Consistently identifying when a prior authorization is required.
        *   Variation in the specificity of information needed and means of communicating the information.
        *   Intensive staffing resources needed to manage the volume of prior authorization documentation.
    *   As a large hospital system with many specialty care centers and outpatient care settings, we employ hundreds of full-time employees who work extensively on completing prior authorization cases.
        *   The tasks required vary from payer to payer.
        *   Certain aspects of prior authorization demand the most resources from providers and staff.
        *   A major problem we encounter is simply identifying when a prior authorization is required, as standards and policies among payers change constantly, and the inquiry work is done manually each time a procedure or service is performed.
    *   We implore ONC and its agency partners to develop standards that require payers to be more transparent and forthcoming in which services require authorization under the plans they offer.
        *   This could include electronically pushing a list of services that require prior authorization to the provider’s EHR.
        *   Additionally, health IT standards should promote easily-navigable APIs allowing providers to efficiently query which services require prior authorization under a patient’s plan.
        *   Instances of having to obtain retroactive authorization or submit an appeal for denied claims to payers can largely be avoided if the initial information for authorization requirement is communicated seamlessly, efficiently, and accurately.
*   Embracing FHIR-based APIs
    *   Health care providers that utilize Certified EHR Technology (CERHT) can share health information with external groups through the use of FHIR APIs, including many of the data elements relevant for payer case coordinators to review prior authorization requests.
    *   Health IT developers have invested significant resources so relevant data can be available and easily retrieved using FHIR APIs.
    *   In our experience, however, many health plans have not adopted technology to leverage the capabilities of FHIR APIs, which would allow them to seek out health information instead of relying on their own portals that often require duplicative manual data entry.
    *   Additionally, we echo recommendations made by Epic, one of our partners in health IT development, deployment, and end-use, that payers should embrace CERHT including the use of FHIR-based APIs.
    *   We believe that health plans should be required to attempt to retrieve clinical data needed to approve a prior authorization request by using the USCDI FHIR APIs that providers have already implemented in their systems.
        *   This will minimize the need for duplicative documentation and reduce redundancy and the need for follow-up.
        *   If the information needed to evaluate a prior authorization request is not readily available in that dataset, only then could the payer follow up with the provider to request it.
    *   We believe this recommendation will help relieve some of the burden of the prior authorization process and expend the full capabilities that Certified EHR Technology can offer.
*   Future Rulemaking on Electronic Prior Authorization
    *   In December 2020, CMS issued a proposed rule pertaining to new requirements for the exchange of information between providers and health plans.
        *   Though the rule was withdrawn from the Federal Register, there were provisions that could be revisited to address the prior authorization issues identified by HHS.
    *   To reiterate comments we made on the Reducing Provider and Patient Burden by Improving Prior Authorization Processes, and Promoting Patients’ Electronic Access to Health Information proposed rule, timely prior authorization decisions are an important beneficiary protection.
    *   If HHS decides to promulgate rules in this area, we encourage HHS to enact regulations that require payers to respond to prior authorization requests (standard and expedited) in a timely manner, such as the 72-hour and 7-day timeframes offered in the withdrawn proposed rule.
    *   Allina Health also reiterates our support for public reporting of prior authorization performance data.
        *   In general, we support reporting quality and patient experience measures to better inform patient care and decision-making.
        *   In that same spirit, we are supportive of public reporting of measures pertaining to prior authorization, similar to the legislation enacted in Minnesota to address ongoing challenges with prior authorization.
    *   As ONC collaborates with agency partners to develop rules on electronic prior authorizations, we urge the consideration of existing states’ laws if they are more stringent than the regulations that may be developed by HHS.
        *   For instance, if CMS proposes revised timelines for authorization determinations, we believe it imperative that if state laws require adhering to a more stringent timeframe, CMS explicitly clarify that state laws are not superseded if they contain shorter processing and appeal timelines, public reporting, and other patient protections.
*   Approaches to Support Electronic Prior Authorization: Healthcare Attachments
    *   ONC seeks comments on additional approaches to support electronic prior authorization regarding health care attachments.
    *   The RFI states several standards initiatives within the last five years focused on advancing standards and functionality supporting clinical documents for a broad range of use cases, including for attachments within prior authorization and other administrative workflows.
        *   These initiatives include the HL7 FHIR-based implementation guides.
    *   Allina Health generally supports a standardized functionality within certified health IT for querying what supporting documentation is required, as well as a standardized process for transmitting the attachments.
        *   While we prefer health plans implement FHIR-based APIs as industry standard, we emphasize the importance in allowing flexibility to health IT developers so they may craft solutions to the needs and preferences of providers.
    *   Additionally, as ONC noted in the RFI, the standards initiatives being developed currently lack maturity.
        *   Before any standard can be selected for inclusion in the Certification Program, adequate testing must be performed to reduce the chances of future issues.
    *   If ONC issues standards for electronic prior authorization in the Health IT Certification Program, we remind the agency of the resource intensity required for implementation.
        *   Proper latitude should be given to developers to individualize products for providers.
        *   Sufficient time should be given to allow the necessary builds to be completed.
    *   Currently, prior authorization workflows and standards vary by payer.
        *   When it comes to the transmission of attachments and sensitive health care information, many large health plans have their own portals for submission, while others still require utilization of fax machines for transmitting documents.
        *   Regulatory standards could help drive an improved baseline for processes and transmitting information.
    *   Done correctly, this could provide an opportunity for payers and providers to align on how best to develop practices for electronic prior authorization.
        *   Payers may benefit from more streamlined processes that incorporate more automation while ensuring they are paying for procedures and services covered per the patient’s health plan.
    *   ONC has a unique opportunity to address an ongoing issue that both providers and payers want help with.
    *   ONC should encourage providers to use Certified health IT solutions as well as incentivize payers to implement Certified health IT functionalities into their prior authorization workflows and gateways.
    *   Lastly, as HHS considers implementation and mechanisms for enforcing electronic prior authorization workflow improvements, we caution the agency that if not properly executed, problems will persist.
        *   Without appropriate incentives or mature and tested standards, a voluntary Certification Program will fail to have any measurable impact on the prior authorization problems plaguing the health care system.
        *   If the process for electronic prior authorization is seamless but doesn’t have proper regulatory guardrails, we may see unintended consequences such as payers requiring even more services be subject to prior authorization.
        *   This would be an unintentional outcome, adding more burden to patients and care teams by requiring additional resources to complete administrative tasks that should instead be used to help care for patients.
*   Conclusion
    *   Allina Health thanks ONC for the opportunity to provide comments on the Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria Request for Information.
    *   Our comments focus on aspects of the minimum capabilities of Certified health IT, and highlight areas for the agency to focus as it considers how to implement regulations aimed at improving electronic prior authorization processes.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Allina Health, a large not-for-profit health system, supports ONC's efforts to standardize electronic prior authorization via certified health IT but urges stronger mandates for payers to adopt FHIR APIs, ensure timely decisions, publicly report data, respect stricter state laws, and proceed carefully to avoid unintended burdens.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Allina Health
- **Type:** Healthcare Provider
- **Role/Expertise:** Not-for-profit health care system; represented by Brian Vamstad, PhD, Manager, Regulatory Affairs.
- **Geographic Scope:** Local/Regional - primarily Minnesota and western Wisconsin.
- **Stake in Issue:** Directly affected by the administrative and financial burdens of inconsistent and inefficient prior authorization processes, employing "hundreds of full-time employees" to manage these tasks, which diverts resources from direct patient care.
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe that while ONC's proposed functionalities for certified health IT are an appropriate baseline for improving electronic prior authorization, stronger regulatory action is needed to mandate payer adoption of standardized, FHIR-based APIs. Our experiences highlight significant administrative burdens from inconsistent payer requirements and manual processes, emphasizing the need for transparent, efficient, and timely prior authorization decisions that do not inadvertently increase administrative tasks.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Develop standards that require payers to be more transparent and forthcoming about which services require authorization under their plans.
    - This could include electronically pushing a list of services requiring prior authorization to the provider’s EHR.
    - Health IT standards should promote easily-navigable APIs allowing providers to efficiently query prior authorization requirements.
- Require health plans to attempt to retrieve clinical data needed for prior authorization approval by using the USCDI FHIR APIs already implemented by providers.
    - Only if information is not readily available in that dataset could the payer follow up with the provider to request it.
- Enact regulations that require payers to respond to prior authorization requests (standard and expedited) in a timely manner, such as the 72-hour and 7-day timeframes offered in the withdrawn proposed rule.
- Support public reporting of prior authorization performance data.
- Urge consideration of existing states’ laws if they are more stringent than federal regulations that may be developed by HHS.
    - Explicitly clarify that state laws are not superseded if they contain shorter processing and appeal timelines, public reporting, and other patient protections.
- Allow flexibility to health IT developers so they may craft solutions to the needs and preferences of providers when implementing standardized functionality for healthcare attachments.
- Perform adequate testing of any standard before selecting it for inclusion in the Certification Program to reduce future issues.
- Provide proper latitude to developers to individualize products for providers and sufficient time for necessary builds to be completed during implementation of new standards.
- Encourage providers to use Certified health IT solutions.
- Incentivize payers to implement Certified health IT functionalities into their prior authorization workflows and gateways.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Inconsistent and confusing implementation of prior authorization requirements creates barriers and delays in care and drives up costs for the health care system.
- Difficulty in consistently identifying when prior authorization is required due to constantly changing payer standards and policies, requiring manual inquiry work.
- Variation in the specificity of information needed and the means of communicating that information across different payers.
- Intensive staffing resources are needed to manage the high volume of prior authorization documentation.
- Many health plans have not adopted technology to leverage FHIR API capabilities, forcing reliance on their own portals that often require duplicative manual data entry.
- Current standards initiatives for healthcare attachments lack maturity, necessitating adequate testing before inclusion in the Certification Program.
- The significant resource intensity required for implementing new electronic prior authorization standards.
- The risk that a voluntary Certification Program, without appropriate incentives or mature and tested standards, will fail to have any measurable impact on existing prior authorization problems.
- The potential for unintended consequences, such as payers requiring even more services to be subject to prior authorization, if electronic prior authorization processes are seamless but lack proper regulatory guardrails, leading to increased administrative burden on patients and care teams.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- As a large hospital system, Allina Health employs hundreds of full-time employees who work extensively on completing prior authorization cases, underscoring the substantial administrative burden and resource drain.
- Experience indicates that despite significant health IT developer investments in FHIR APIs, many health plans have not adopted this technology, forcing providers to continue using less efficient, manual-entry portals.
- The insight that instances of retroactive authorization or denied claim appeals could largely be avoided if initial information about authorization requirements were communicated seamlessly, efficiently, and accurately.
- The observation that current prior authorization standards and workflows vary significantly by payer, with some still requiring fax machines for transmitting sensitive health information.
- The perspective that ONC has a unique opportunity to address a persistent issue that both providers and payers want help with, suggesting a shared interest in finding solutions.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "As a large hospital system with many specialty care centers and outpatient care settings, we employ hundreds of full-time employees who work extensively on completing prior authorization cases."
- "Without appropriate incentives or mature and tested standards, a voluntary Certification Program will fail to have any measurable impact on the prior authorization problems plaguing the health care system."
- "If the process for electronic prior authorization is seamless but doesn’t have proper regulatory guardrails, we may see unintended consequences such as payers requiring even more services be subject to prior authorization."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   Executives for Health Innovation (EHI) strongly supports efforts to advance electronic prior authorization (ePA) and commends ONC for its continued attention on this issue.
    *   ONC’s Certification Program, while important, has not yet included health payer health IT systems.
    *   EHI urges ONC to continue to work with the Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to advance a coordinated approach to ePA.
    *   EHI agrees with ONC’s inquiries regarding the maturity of the referenced implementation specifications.
    *   Adding ePA requirements to the Certification Program is not the only option to advance ePA.
    *   EHI urges ONC to fully consider and explore both the maturity of the implementation specifications and other policy tools available to all of HHS to advance ePA in a way that reduces burden for all stakeholders.
    *   If ONC decides to move forward with Certification requirements, EHI has provided responses to specific questions.
*   EHI recognizes the valuable work of its member organization, Health Level Seven (HL7) International, in developing and advancing the implementation specifications in this Request for Information (RFI).
    *   Generally, EHI supports HL7's comments and continued input on implementation guides, specifications, and standards.
*   Certified Health IT Functionality
    *   In general, EHI believes the functional capabilities included in the RFI represent most functionality.
    *   Additional functionality that should be included:
        *   Ability for providers to verify which third-party administrator (TPA)/payer to submit the PA to and determine the member’s eligibility and benefits.
            *   For example, some group health plans (both insured and self-funded) use different benefit administrators (e.g., medical, pharmacy, behavioral health) and the system should be able to determine which TPA has coverage responsibility for the particular items or services.
        *   Ability to support multiple discreet PA requests (e.g., line items) that may have quantities and/or service periods as part of a single PA request.
            *   Providers may need the ability to request exceptions to plan rules for certain items or services.
            *   Payers may need the ability to partially approve and partially deny an authorization request.
        *   Providers and payers need the ability for bi-directional communications on particular issues within a PA request until resolved.
            *   For example, accredited plans need to be able to request clinical information.
            *   Payers should have the ability to indicate when a PA is submitted the information necessary for a decision on the request.
        *   Allowance for all involved care team members to query the payer on status of a PA request.
            *   This includes checking whether the request is pending and if additional data is needed for a decision.
*   Should ONC adopt a certification criterion for prior authorization that accounts for the full, HIPAA compliant workflow for prior authorization transactions including translation from FHIR to the X12 standard? Or should ONC adopt certification criteria that include only the workflows up to the point of translation? What ongoing challenges will stakeholders face if there is a need to translate between HIPAA-adopted standards and other standards that have only been adopted under the Certification Program used to support prior authorization transactions? How should HHS address alignment between standards adopted for HIPAA transactions and standards adopted under the Certification Program?
    *   ONC should adopt certification criteria that only include workflows up until translation.
    *   ONC’s criteria should focus on FHIR-based solutions.
*   Impact on Patients
    *   Advancing ePA in a way that reduces burden for providers and patients can help respond positively to some of the most worrisome pitfalls of the current system.
        *   These pitfalls include patient care delays and non-adherence.
    *   According to the 2021 American Medical Association Prior Authorization Physician Survey:
        *   93% of physicians report care delays for treatments that require PA.
        *   82% report that PA can lead to treatment abandonment.
*   Impact on Providers
    *   According to the 2021 AMA survey:
        *   88% of physicians describe the burden associated with PA as high or extremely high.
        *   Physicians and their staff spend an average of 13 hours per week completing PAs.
    *   While ePA alone will not address all burden associated with PA, according to the 2021 CAQH Index:
        *   ePA presents a $437 million annual savings opportunity to the medical industry.
        *   ePA could save clinicians and their staff an estimated 16 minutes per transaction.
    *   EHI believes streamlining the process through a coordinated approach to system requirements on both EHRs used by clinicians and health IT systems used by payers can help alleviate a significant amount of burden associated with PA.
*   Conclusion
    *   EHI looks forward to continuing to work with ONC to ensure a coordinated approach to ePA that reduces burden on providers, payers, and patients and improves timely access to care.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Executives for Health Innovation (EHI) supports advancing electronic prior authorization (ePA) to reduce healthcare burden and delays, urging ONC to coordinate with CMS, focus certification on FHIR and pre-translation workflows, and explore all policy tools.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Executives for Health Innovation (EHI)
- **Type:** Organization
- **Role/Expertise:** Health innovation advocacy group, representing diverse stakeholders including health IT companies, providers, payers, and research institutions, focused on advancing health IT and reducing healthcare burden.
- **Geographic Scope:** National (Headquartered in Washington, DC, influencing federal policy)
- **Stake in Issue:** Advocates for policies that streamline healthcare processes through technology, aiming to reduce burden and improve patient access, directly impacting their member organizations and the broader healthcare system.
structuredSections.corePosition: EHI strongly supports efforts to advance electronic prior authorization (ePA) and commends ONC for its attention to this issue. We believe that advancing ePA in a way that reduces burden for providers and patients can positively respond to the pitfalls of the current system, including patient care delays and non-adherence. A coordinated approach to system requirements on both clinician EHRs and payer health IT systems is crucial for alleviating significant PA burden.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- ONC should continue to work with the Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to advance a coordinated approach to ePA.
- ONC should fully consider and explore both the maturity of the implementation specifications and other policy tools available to all of HHS to advance ePA in a way that reduces burden for all stakeholders.
- If ONC decides to move forward with Certification requirements, they should:
    - Adopt certification criteria that only include workflows up until translation.
    - Focus ONC’s criteria on FHIR-based solutions.
    - Include additional functional capabilities for certified Health IT Modules:
        - Ability for providers to verify which third-party administrator (TPA)/payer to submit the PA to and determine the member’s eligibility and benefits.
        - Ability to support multiple discreet PA requests (e.g., line items) that may have quantities and/or service periods as part of a single PA request, including partial approvals/denials and exceptions.
        - Ability for bi-directional communications between providers and payers on particular issues within a PA request until resolved, including requests for clinical information and indications of necessary decision information.
        - Allowance for all involved care team members to query the payer on status of a PA request, including checking for pending status and additional data needs.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- The current prior authorization (PA) system leads to significant patient care delays and non-adherence, with 93% of physicians reporting delays and 82% reporting treatment abandonment due to PA.
- The burden associated with PA is extremely high for healthcare providers, with physicians and their staff spending an average of 13 hours per week completing PAs.
- There are ongoing challenges for stakeholders if there is a need to translate between HIPAA-adopted standards (like X12) and other standards (like FHIR) adopted under the Certification Program, highlighting a need for alignment.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Data from the 2021 American Medical Association Prior Authorization Physician Survey reveals that 93% of physicians experience care delays for treatments requiring PA, and 82% report that PA can lead to treatment abandonment.
- The same AMA survey indicates 88% of physicians describe PA burden as high or extremely high, with an average of 13 hours per week spent by physicians and their staff on PAs.
- The 2021 CAQH Index estimates ePA could lead to a $437 million annual savings opportunity for the medical industry and save clinicians and their staff an estimated 16 minutes per transaction.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "According to the 2021 American Medical Association Prior Authorization Physician Survey, 93% of physicians report care delays for treatments that require PA and 82% report that PA can lead to treatment abandonment."
- "ePA presents a $437 million annual savings opportunity to the medical industry and could save clinicians and their staff an estimated 16 minutes per transaction."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   Allscripts is pleased to submit its response to the Request for Information (RFI) on Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria (RIN 0955-AA04).
    *   Allscripts provides clinical and business solutions for ambulatory, acute, and post-acute settings.
    *   Its platform is relied upon by tens of thousands of physicians and over 2,000 hospitals.
    *   Comments are informed by three decades of experience partnering with and deploying software to this vast network of providers.
*   **I.d Functional Capabilities for ePA in Certified Health IT**
    *   **Comments Requested on Core Set of Capabilities in Certified Health IT**
        *   The RFI asks whether the following are appropriate minimum capabilities for certified health IT systems to interact with payer systems for ePA:
            *   Identify when prior authorization is applicable for an item or service, using clinical decision support and/or user input, and receive notifications of changes.
            *   Query a payer API for prior authorization requirements for each item and service and identify real-time rules and documentation.
            *   Collect clinical and administrative documentation (electronic forms or templates) from a health IT system.
            *   Electronically submit completed documentation for prior authorization to a payer’s API, with supporting information.
            *   Receive a response from a payer regarding approval, denial (with reason), or need for additional information.
            *   Query a payer’s system for updates on a pending prior authorization request and receive a reason for why it is pending.
            *   Effectively capture and persist digital signatures (or other indications of provider review and assent), enable data integrity, and support other features for payer administrative requirements.
    *   **Allscripts’ Commentary on these capabilities:**
        *   The goal of real-time activity while a provider orders patient care is admirable but highly aspirational due to variability, complexity, and dependency on changes for both payer and provider sides.
            *   Electronic prescribing preauthorization is still challenging after many years and only gained traction when price transparency was included, which is not part of these requirements.
        *   The enormous shift in workflow cannot be overstated; it is difficult to imagine a provider waiting with a patient for a payer to respond.
            *   Patient care occurs 24/7/365, but payers do not operate on the same schedule.
            *   Expecting this process to be done while a patient waits would be inefficient and frustrating.
            *   Significant human intervention could still be required, delaying care even if approved.
            *   It is unclear how transactions with a third-party supplier would occur.
        *   These concepts essentially ask providers to perform payment-related work as part of clinical treatment workflows.
            *   To work without completely upending efficient workflows, non-disruptive processes must be enabled within the clinical workflow.
            *   Significant potential for unintended consequences exists, which regulators must be aware of.
                *   Providers may be motivated to submit for easily approved treatments, rather than potentially more beneficial but harder-to-authorize options.
                *   Providers might need to send multiple treatment options together because submitting orders separately and waiting sequentially is not feasible.
        *   Patients are unique, and providers rely on treatment experiences; it is difficult to envision a satisfactory approval system relying solely on preset data tables and payer algorithms.
        *   A workable system requires extensive coordination between clinical and payer systems on the front end of the patient encounter, which is not widely in place today.
            *   Many existing back-end payment processes may be brought forward, requiring modification and extensive testing to work in a live, evolving clinical interaction.
        *   Allscripts is not suggesting ePA cannot work, but it will be highly complex and risks patients receiving substandard care as providers navigate the system in real-time.
        *   **It is absolutely necessary that standards be defined and codified before vendors attempt to create software.**
            *   Currently, there is no standardization across payer organizations regarding prior authorization requirements.
            *   All stakeholders must be equally responsible from a certification perspective for the overall solution.
            *   With thousands of vendor participants, rules must be firmly established, and enough time allowed for development, testing, and deployment.
*   **I.e Implementation Specifications to Support ePA Capabilities**
    *   **Documentation Templates and Coverage Rules (DTR) Implementation Guide**
        *   The IG describes a process where the provider's IT system auto-populates as many FHIR QuestionnaireResponse fields as possible, then alerts the provider to information gaps for manual completion.
        *   This requires standard content from health plans to permit auto-population from the EHR during clinician workflow.
            *   Absent standardization, development work would be massive and likely require future rework.
            *   Complying with this IG requires significant development for all EHRs.
    *   **Discussion of Standards**
        *   Concerns regarding the DTR and CRD IGs include leveraging CDS Hooks functionality, which is not yet adopted in any Certification Program criterion.
        *   Converting between FHIR and X12 creates possibilities of failure, especially given payer variation.
        *   CDS Hooks functionality is another large standard that needs much wider development across the industry.
*   **I.f Additional Approaches to Support ePA Healthcare Attachments**
*   **II Requests for Comment**
    *   **Certified Health IT Functionality**
        *   Allscripts believes certification criteria for only part of the workflow (e.g., up to translation) is inappropriate; the entire workflow needs to be certified for all stakeholders.
            *   Addressing only the clinical EHR side leaves a massive risk that functionality will not be fully utilized.
            *   This could result in significant development work with little to no return, which software developers cannot afford.
        *   Challenges with translating between HIPAA-adopted and Certification Program standards are unknown without testing but seem problematic if translation is needed on both sides.
        *   HHS should address alignment by recommending X12 or FHIR on both sides of the transaction.
        *   Regarding the structure of new certification criteria:
            *   Allscripts acknowledges ONC's authority is limited to health IT vendors (providers and payers largely governed by CMS), creating a complicated regulatory process.
            *   However, the whole system needs coordination to be utilized as intended; none of this will work on a voluntary basis. All stakeholders must be compelled to participate.
            *   An overarching architecture governed by established standards is key.
            *   As an EHR vendor, Allscripts believes its certification obligation should end at creating and transmitting the FHIR resource (if adopted).
            *   Allscripts should not be responsible for FHIR to X12 mapping, as that is most effectively and efficiently implemented by clearinghouses and payers.
            *   Certification would be effective only if all actors are included; compliance would be easier with a well-designed infrastructure for stakeholders to plug into.
    *   **Implementation Specifications for Prior Authorization**
        *   There should not be a final rule until the Implementation Guides (IGs) are ready.
        *   All IGs must be ready prior to any proposed certification criteria being put into effect, as well-vetted standards are needed for such a broad and complex solution.
        *   Allscripts strongly encourages a whole solution rather than a piecemeal approach.
            *   It is too complex to move forward one piece at a time.
            *   The risk of a piecemeal approach is that development would be completed and then require rework or be unusable, which is unreasonable for software developers.
            *   It is critical that this be fully thought through and specified wholly and completely, rather than designed in parts hoping it comes together. This is especially true due to certification criteria requirements and new payer stakeholders.
        *   Allscripts believes a full three years (36 months) may be needed between the introduction of new certification criteria and the deployment of an impacted solution.
            *   Adopting criteria before standards are firmly established increases unnecessary burden for compliance.
            *   Content, format, and transmission standards must all be available simultaneously before certification criteria are created.
            *   Criteria should meet real-world scenarios of clients.
        *   Allscripts is not familiar enough with all Da Vinci IGs and state legal requirements to comment on whether they effectively support all requirements (e.g., signatures, record retention, security). However, health plans must be able to consume transmitted data.
        *   Simplified approaches: Translating between FHIR and X12 seems problematic, acting as "a solution on top of a solution."
            *   Moving ePA into the clinical workflow should be a priority; little willingness for providers to adopt if not placed there.
    *   **Healthcare Attachment Standards**
        *   CDA Attachments IG: A CDA carries too much information for a FHIR request; there should be a specific use case for each request type (e.g., a 275 transaction using a LOINC code). Specifications would need to limit data to only what is truly needed.
        *   FHIR Documents: In a broad sense, FHIR Documents can support more effective exchange, but the industry first needs to know what treatments require prior authorization and potential denial reasons. Workflows (proactive vs. on-demand document provision, storage) must be determined in a standardized way.
        *   **Feasible timeline for CDA Attachments IG or FHIR Documents in production:**
            *   This is a challenging problem requiring a very complex, interconnected set of solutions.
            *   Allscripts suggests the industry would need 36 months from the final rule (and the availability of all necessary details) to be available for client use due to:
                1.  No consensus across health plans on which procedures/equipment require PA.
                2.  Where agreement exists, questions and responses are not identical.
                3.  If CDA is an attachment, a new standard in addition to current ones will be needed.
                4.  The FHIR to X12 translation is a significant project requiring repeated stress-testing.
                5.  New contracts with health plans will need to be scoped and signed.
                6.  Unclear how testing between Allscripts' software and payers/health plans will occur, as well as real-world testing with clients.
                    *   Testing would be extensive beyond almost anything rolled out recently.
                    *   A standardized testing protocol is needed.
                    *   Need to test efficiently at scale, not with each payer separately.
                    *   Workflow testing is critical as it disrupts real-time clinical interaction.
            *   If these issues were worked through prior to certification release, work could be done faster, but Allscripts is skeptical given policymaker urgency.
            *   Advocates for enough time to think through, create, test thoroughly, and roll out successfully.
        *   Allscripts recommends just one approach (either FHIR documents or CDA) to accommodate improvements, and if FHIR is chosen, all efforts should be placed there.
        *   Certification criteria should not be established for healthcare attachments alone until IGs are completely ready; a full scope of standards is needed first, and ideally, real-world implementation should be seen at scale.
        *   Today, prior authorization is still done partly by phone and fax. FHIR R4 is too new to assess its effectiveness for healthcare attachments.
            *   **Burden/Benefit of using these standards:** Payer systems still use faxing/scanning, and clearinghouses use X12 275/279, which are well known. Payers and clearinghouses generally do not know FHIR and would need to learn and adjust. Significant mapping work is required to automate the process for clinicians.
            *   A large knowledge gap exists in the industry due to COVID-19 and the Great Resignation impacting resources.
    *   **Impact on Patients**
        *   **Positive impact:** If ePA is achieved at the time of a patient visit, it could speed time to care. This depends on payers processing requests fast enough or having the desire to do so.
            *   Requires real-time adjudication and structured, standardized data unified across the payer spectrum, which needs change from EHR developers, clearinghouses, and payers. Certification is a critical tool.
        *   Patients should be involved and apprised of prior authorization developments.
            *   Patients should be notified of communication between provider and payer via patient portals and personal health records.
            *   Denials with reasons should be required (electronic or paper) and shared with the patient through real-time notification, with the *payer* responsible for notification, not the provider.
            *   A complete solution involving all three stakeholders is preferable but requires clarification of standards and technical work by payers.
        *   **Reduce time to services, non-adherence, out-of-pocket costs:** Increased efficiency could *increase* the volume of required PA items by health plans.
            *   Unintended consequence: more denials, leading to patient suffering or delay in care, potentially resulting in non-adherence.
            *   Denials necessitate appeals, with unpredictable but frequently negative impacts on patient outcomes.
            *   However, if implemented efficiently, immediate denial could allow a provider to select an equivalent second choice, improving treatment.
            *   Real-time processes are essential, but clinical situations (e.g., waiting on lab results) might impede this goal.
            *   The entire process needs to be automated to improve care.
        *   **Additional functionality for patient participation:** Allscripts believes patients should be involved but does not see how they would have the ability to participate electronically at this point.
            *   The larger issue is getting providers and payers to work together, raising concern that the patient might be an afterthought.
            *   Patients might have to participate manually or in writing with the payer for denied treatment, especially with a non-participating provider.
            *   Ideally, payers should be required to share all ePA communications with the patient via their existing provider portal.
    *   **Impact on Providers**
        *   **Reduce burden:** Current PA activities are managed by support staff, not providers, so the premise of the question is incorrect. Providers want to treat patients, not be intermediaries.
            *   The best solution is ePA in the ordering workflow, but this could increase burden if not very smooth and non-disruptive.
            *   The degree of burden depends on how well it works; Allscripts fears it could just automate existing problems if not done correctly.
        *   **Provider use of capabilities; incentives needed:** Financial incentives may be required for providers to participate.
            *   While eager for PA burdens to be lifted, providers fear shifting burdens to a different workflow, causing more headaches for clinicians.
            *   Perhaps an extra MIPS incentive would be appropriate.
            *   Standardization across health plans is ideal, but if the payer side is not regulated and appropriately staffed, it will not work in the real world.
            *   The process needs to be as close to real-time as possible to reduce disruption and benefit the provider.
            *   Payers *must* be required to automate to reduce clinical impact.
    *   **Impact on Developers**
        *   **Cost and time estimates:** Difficult to estimate in RFI phase without standards and guidance.
            *   Needs to account for variations from each health plan and contract types.
            *   Standardization and regulation on both sides would help quantification.
            *   FHIR to X12 translation will impact costs, and third-party involvement is unknown.
        *   **Burden for Da Vinci IGs:** Allscripts believes there should be no requirements before standards are known; IGs must be complete and robust.
            *   Once the macro architecture is determined, significant time is needed for development, testing, and deployment to clients.
        *   **Burden if only base FHIR standard referenced, no IGs:** Health IT developers must have complete IGs at the outset; a piecemeal approach would be detrimental.
            *   This tactic would inevitably require rework, and resources for rework are unavailable.
            *   Concern that functionality would be developed but never utilized by clients (seen previously with regulation-driven but not market-demanded capabilities).
            *   Coordination is difficult even with IGs in place; that is the only way for success.
            *   The more the architecture is known upfront, the better.
    *   **Payer Implementation**
        *   **Certification Program support for payer technology needs; consider payer workflows:** Yes, payers must participate in partnership with health IT developers for this initiative to succeed.
            *   Payers will need to be responsive within clinical timelines on a 24/7/365 basis.
        *   **Availability of certified Health IT Modules reduce payer burden:** Allscripts does not suspect the burden will be felt more by payers than providers.
            *   All stakeholders must be held to the same standards and meet consistent, standards-based technical specifications.
            *   Clinical health IT vendors producing their half of the solution will not suffice without the same discipline and rigor from the payer side.
        *   **Payers likely to use certified Health IT Modules:** All healthcare payers must be required to participate for success.
            *   If rolled out as voluntary or optional, it is unlikely many payers will do the necessary development.
        *   **Developers seeking certification for payer workflows:** Allscripts would likely not seek certification unless it is required for all vendors on both the clinical and payer side.
            *   It is unreasonable to expect them to carry the weight of this only on their side when success requires commitment from both EHR software developers and payers.
            *   It is difficult to see how payers would participate in certification given no history in the process unless compelled, as it is faster and less resource-intensive for them to take a proprietary approach (different technologies and portals for providers to manage).
*   Allscripts appreciates the opportunity to provide feedback and welcomes further discussion.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Allscripts, a major health IT vendor, supports electronic prior authorization but advocates for a comprehensive, mandated, and standardized solution involving all stakeholders, stressing that real-time implementation is aspirational and requires a realistic, multi-year timeline with all specifications defined upfront.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Allscripts
- **Type:** Organization
- **Role/Expertise:** Vice President, Policy & Government Affairs. Allscripts provides clinical and business solutions for ambulatory, acute, and post-acute settings, relied upon by tens of thousands of physicians and over 2,000 hospitals, with three decades of experience deploying software.
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** As a prominent health IT vendor, Allscripts is directly responsible for developing and implementing the software necessary for electronic prior authorization, and its extensive client base of providers will be directly impacted by the regulation's requirements and timelines.
structuredSections.corePosition: Allscripts fundamentally supports the concept of electronic prior authorization (ePA) but strongly believes that its successful implementation requires a holistic, mandated approach that includes clear, comprehensive, and well-vetted standards across all stakeholders (health IT vendors, providers, and payers). We argue that the proposed real-time capabilities are overly ambitious under current conditions and that a piecemeal or voluntary rollout will lead to inefficiency, unintended consequences, and ultimately, substandard patient care.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Standards must be defined and codified comprehensively before vendors are required to create software to comply with ePA.
- The entire electronic prior authorization workflow needs to be certified for all stakeholders, not just parts of it.
- HHS should address alignment between standards by recommending the use of X12 or FHIR on both sides of the transaction (payer and provider).
- All stakeholders, including providers and payers, must be compelled to participate in the ePA process, as it will not work on a voluntary basis.
- The certification criteria should be established based on an overarching architecture governed by established standards.
- EHR vendor certification obligations should end at creating and transmitting the FHIR resource, with mapping between FHIR and X12 implemented by clearinghouses and payers.
- No final rule should be issued, and no certification criteria should be proposed or put into effect until all Implementation Guides (IGs) are completely ready and well-vetted.
- A whole, complete solution should be proposed, rather than a piecemeal approach, given the complexity.
- A full three years (36 months) should be allowed between the introduction of new certification criteria and the deployment of an impacted solution, ensuring all content, format, and transmission standards are available simultaneously.
- ONC should recommend and support only one standardized approach for healthcare attachments (e.g., focus all efforts on FHIR documents).
- Payers must be required to automate their processes and be responsive within clinical timelines (24/7/365) to reduce clinical impact and enable real-time ePA.
- Payers should be required to share all ePA communications (including denials with reasons) with the patient through their existing provider portal, with the payer responsible for this notification.
- Financial incentives, such as extra MIPS incentives, may be required for providers to gain their participation in ePA, especially if new burdens are shifted to them.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- The goal of real-time prior authorization occurring while a provider orders patient care is highly aspirational and likely impractical due to variability, complexity, and payer operational schedules (not 24/7/365).
- Implementing ePA as proposed would require an enormous and potentially disruptive shift in well-established clinical workflows, effectively asking providers to perform payment-related work during patient encounters.
- There is significant potential for unintended consequences, such as providers being motivated to select treatments known to be easily approved rather than those that might be more beneficial for the patient, or an increase in the overall volume of required prior authorizations and subsequent denials due to process efficiencies.
- There is currently no standardization across payer organizations regarding prior authorization requirements, making it difficult to create a workable, uniform system.
- The proposed use of CDS Hooks functionality is concerning as it has not yet been widely adopted in the Certification Program.
- Translating between FHIR and X12 standards seems problematic and "a solution on top of a solution" due to potential failures and payer variations.
- Adopting certification criteria before comprehensive standards and Implementation Guides (IGs) are firmly established would increase unnecessary burden on health IT developers and inevitably lead to rework, for which resources are unavailable.
- A piecemeal approach to ePA development risks creating functionality that is never utilized by clients or requires significant rework later.
- The industry faces a large knowledge gap and resource constraints due to the impacts of COVID-19 and the "Great Resignation," which could impede complex development efforts.
- The current premise that ePA capabilities will reduce provider burden is flawed, as prior authorization activities are primarily managed by support staff, not providers; ePA could shift burdens to clinicians.
- If payer participation in ePA is voluntary, it is unlikely that many will undertake the necessary development, leading to a fragmented system where health IT vendors carry an unreasonable burden without mutual commitment from payers.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- The historical challenge of electronic prescribing preauthorization, which only gained traction with the inclusion of price transparency, serves as a cautionary tale for the current ePA proposal that lacks similar incentives.
- The RFI's concepts are seen as essentially "asking providers to perform payment-related work as part of the clinical treatment workflows," highlighting a fundamental shift in responsibility.
- A significant unintended consequence could be providers being motivated to submit for treatments known to be easily approved by payers, potentially leading to patients receiving substandard care instead of more beneficial but harder-to-authorize options.
- The current disconnect between patient care occurring "24/7/365" and payers "not operating on the same schedule" underscores a critical obstacle to real-time ePA implementation.
- The observation that translating between FHIR and X12 seems "like a solution on top of a solution" reveals a concern about overly complex technical architectures.
- Allscripts details six specific reasons why a 36-month timeline from final rule to client use is necessary, including lack of consensus on PA requirements, the complexity of FHIR to X12 translation, and extensive testing needs, specifically highlighting the challenge of workflow testing disrupting real-time patient consultations.
- The identification of a "large knowledge gap to fill in the industry as COVID-19 and the Great Resignation have impacted resources" provides a unique perspective on current industry challenges affecting implementation capacity.
- The direct statement that the premise of the question regarding provider burden is incorrect, as "Current prior authorization activities are managed by support staff as opposed to the providers," offers a key insight into where the burden currently lies and where it might be misallocated.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "The goal to have all of this activity occur in real time while a provider is ordering patient care is admirable but highly aspirational due to the variability and complexity involved, as well as dependency on numerous changes to technologies and workflows on both the payer and provider sides."
- "The concepts embedded in this RFI are essentially asking providers to perform payment-related work as part of the clinical treatment workflows."
- "It is entirely unreasonable to expect us to carry the weight of this only on our side when success cannot happen without commitment from EHR software developers and the payers / health plans on the other end."

submitter: The American Association of Hip and Knee Surgeons

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The American Association of Hip and Knee Surgeons (AAHKS) appreciates the opportunity to submit comments to the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT (ONC) on its Request for Information (RFI) on “Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification.”
*   AAHKS is the foremost national specialty organization of more than 4,600 physicians with expertise in total joint arthroplasty procedures.
    *   Many members conduct research in this area and are experts in using evidence-based medicine to better define the risks and benefits of treatments for patients suffering from lower extremity joint conditions.
*   AAHKS is guided by three principles:
    *   Payment reform is most effective when physician-led.
    *   The burden of excessive physician reporting on metrics detracts from care.
    *   Patient access, especially for high-risk patients, and physician incentives must remain a focus.
*   General comments are summarized as follows:
    *   AAHKS supports ONC’s continued efforts to reduce barriers and burdens associated with prior authorization as it considers adoption of standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria to advance electronic prior authorization.
    *   Nevertheless, AAHKS urges the Secretary of HHS to prioritize addressing pressing issues of payor prior authorization policies as a central component of its strategy to tackle prior authorization burdens.
    *   AAHKS believes that improving health IT functionality without addressing the urgent, underlying issues associated with payor prior authorization policies would further build upon a broken system that creates barriers for patients and over-burdens providers.
    *   Shifting the center of care back to the patient-provider relationship and away from payer-imposed administrative burdens should be a paramount consideration in any policy ONC ultimately adopts.
    *   Therefore, if ONC proceeds to propose the adoption of standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria for electronic prior authorization, AAHKS urges HHS to offer providers incentives and implement policies to ensure providers have adequate financial support.
        *   Providers should not be asked to bear a disproportionate amount of the costs or burdens of system-wide standards enhancements.

*   **I. Certified Health IT Functionality**
    *   AAHKS urges HHS to improve alignment of the burdensome prior authorization policies payors impose on providers that vary in:
        *   When payors require prior authorization.
        *   The methods payors require providers to use to make prior authorization requests.
        *   The clinical criteria on which payors base their prior authorization standards.
        *   The qualifications of individuals reviewing prior authorization requests for payors.
    *   AAHKS has a positive view regarding the core set of functional capabilities ONC lists in the RFI and believes thoughtful, patient and provider-focused adoption of such capabilities to be critical to improving providers’ interactions with payor systems.
    *   However, AAHKS considers improvements to health IT functionality to be merely one component of greater policy reforms needed to reduce providers’ prior authorization burdens.
        *   Payors’ and health IT developers’ ad hoc attempts to address prior authorization have resulted in a diversity of payor standards reflecting individual payer’s technology considerations, lines of business, and customer-specific constraints.
        *   AAHKS believes this wide variation of payor prior authorization standards currently poses more immediate and significant burdens on providers and barriers for patients.
    *   Key challenges identified by ONC in its ONC HIT Strategy include:
        *   Difficulty in determining whether an item or service requires prior authorization.
        *   Difficulty in determining payer-specific prior authorization requirements.
        *   Inefficient use of provider and staff time to navigate communications channels (fax, telephone, web portals).
