        *   Since CMS is a payer that typically pays and reviews after payment, we strongly suggest this prior authorization approach address training of providers when a PA request is submitted but areas of the PA request are incorrect.
        *   In the commercial world of RAD-V, oftentimes, a payer will use the opportunity of denied claims to promote provider education.
        *   This PA should follow the same approach and use the opportunity to educate as well as review.
    *   This should be approached from an architectural planning perspective with a timeline of functionality add-on functional modules.
        *   The architecture is critical to insure that over time all programs interact.
    *   There is no need for a single approach that must be followed and applies to all cases.
        *   One can have multiple paths similar to the approach the QPP MIPS Quality Reporting systems work.
        *   There are flowcharts for quality measures and rationale.
        *   ONC can identify all logical scenario pathways (probably less than 5) that they know of today and build based on the needs of the different end users.
        *   ONC should be careful not to architect the approach so that future approaches are left out; there should always be a “reserved for future use” to address the unknown in the present.
        *   Perhaps lessons from Web3 and Blockchain distributed architecture planning approaches can be considered.
    *   Not addressable with my skill level. (Regarding readiness of FHIR-based Da Vinci IGs)
    *   Yes, ONC should pursue the approach of proposing certification criteria incorporating the FHIR Release 4 base standard and delaying adoption of implementation specifications.
        *   The risks of doing nothing far outweigh not taking this position.
        *   People are getting sicker, money is running out, and doctors are quitting.
        *   This entire approach, whatever is chosen, is urgent to pursue.
    *   Beyond my skillset to address. (Regarding handling integration of IGs over time)
    *   In general, this is beyond my skillset to address (regarding Da Vinci IGs supporting legal/compliance requirements).
        *   However, the concept of electronic signature that is compliant has many products existing.
        *   It is probably a key to review each State’s regulations on e-signature.
    *   Beyond my skillset to address. (Regarding alternative approaches to designing certification criteria)
    *   Beyond my skillset to address. (Regarding simplified approaches to workflows)
    *   Beyond my skillset to address. (Regarding new IGs needed for other workflows or HIPAA integration)
*   Healthcare Attachment Standards
    *   Yes, the specifications within the CDA Attachments IG should be included.
    *   Attachments are heavily used on the commercial side and with Dental and Vision, which will predictably become more intertwined with CMS approaches for in Medicare Advantage.
    *   Also, with Post Acute Care, attachments are used.
    *   It is good to mention that in hospice situations, admissions are emergent, and introducing time delays as with PA would be difficult.
    *   Start in 1Q 2023. (Regarding feasible timeline for CDA Attachments IG or FHIR Documents)
    *   Both (CDA Attachments IG and FHIR Documents) should be considered for supporting improvements over time.
*   At this point, I am noticing that the questions and requested comments are falling more into technological responses that I do not have the skill set to address.
    *   I will skip many of the remaining sections rather than stating “beyond my skillset” and only answer where I have expertise.
*   Impact on Patients
    *   By making it easier for providers to comply.
    *   Creates an interacting and standardized web of healthcare processes.
    *   Leads to better care and commoditized choice. (Regarding positive impact on healthcare consumers)
    *   Already addressed in previous comments. (Regarding reducing time for services, non-adherence, lowering costs)
*   Impact on Providers
    *   Certified health IT is a good thing.
    *   However, the testing and fees to achieve compliance by vendors, such as with 2015 EHR, are prohibitive except for the very large vendors.
        *   This shuts out innovation.
    *   Suggest oversight of fees charges by certification bodies and certification by module.
    *   Allow vendors to purchase sub modules from others, much like the CHPL ID can be created. (Regarding degree to which electronic PA capabilities reduce burden)
    *   Normally, I would say definitely yes, providers would use these capabilities.
    *   However, given CMS is known to pay first and review later, the carrot and the stick approach will not work as well as there is no need to change practice and the status quo is perceived to be fine for CMS.
    *   CMS needs to require use of certified Health IT for this to work.
    *   In addition, the incentive for private payers to quickly process PA and to shorten decisions will occur when a payer as large as CMS is part of the overall process. (Regarding provider likelihood of use and needed incentives)
    *   They are already overworked and have no time.
    *   Suggest ONC use Medpac, market basket, already defined work units or other collected cost and process related data. (Regarding estimates of cost and time for providers)
*   Impact on Developers
    *   This is a key question.
    *   The answer is that vendors can fill this requirement, or at least contribute to it, especially when the vendor is a services vendor that keeps track, much like HISPS keep track of secure messaging. (Regarding estimates of cost and time for developers)
*   Payer Implementation
    *   CAQH and AHIP, as well as HCP-LAN.org are good sources.
    *   However, always be aware of the incentives of some payers.
    *   Consider that with some of the smaller payers, there may be other priorities especially with the shortage of labor affecting us all, and it may not be a good thing for their financial business model to always fast track PA approvals. (Regarding Certification Program supporting payer technology needs)
    *   They would use them if CMS chooses a progressively aggressive approach over time (staged phases and short). (Regarding payer likelihood of using certified Health IT Modules)
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: Suncoast RHIO, a regional health information organization, strongly supports urgent, standardized electronic prior authorization but cautions against overly complex, costly certification processes that stifle innovation and burden providers, emphasizing the need for an interoperable, holistic approach that addresses the human factor and aligns payer incentives.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Suncoast RHIO, Inc.
- **Type:** Organization
- **Role/Expertise:** Regional Health Information Organization (RHIO) with pilot involvement in CMS demonstration (EMDI), possessing Prior Authorization and patient cost estimation software, X.12 capabilities, and FHIR modules, focused on supporting providers and patient care/financial aspects.
- **Geographic Scope:** Regional, located in Florida.
- **Stake in Issue:** As a RHIO, they are directly involved in health information exchange and supporting healthcare providers with technology and processes, making them a stakeholder in interoperability, standards, and certification criteria for prior authorization.
structuredSections.corePosition: We completely support the goal of adopting standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria for automated electronic prior authorization, as robust standards reduce costs and complexity. However, we caution against overly complex or expensive certification processes that burden providers, stifle innovation, and make compliance difficult. We advocate for a holistic, interoperable approach that extends beyond mere technology, incorporates the crucial "human factor," integrates with other programs, and provides clear, accessible certification pathways for both products and services.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- **Expand Certification Scope:**
    - Certify services in addition to products to allow organizations to offer centralized benefits to providers and mitigate financial barriers.
- **Simplify and Clarify Certification:**
    - Implement oversight of fees charged by certification bodies to prevent prohibitive costs for vendors, which currently inhibit innovation.
    - Introduce "certification by module" and allow vendors to purchase sub-modules, fostering a more modular and accessible certification ecosystem.
    - Provide clear guidance and accessible information on how Health IT vendors can find the website and specific details for product certification.
- **Harmonize and Integrate Prior Authorization with Broader Health IT Efforts:**
    - Link prior authorization efforts with other programs and ensure harmonization with interoperability, audit, and quality reporting over time.
    - Dovetail efforts with CMS esMD audit capabilities and synchronize with CAQH administrative simplification for commercial payers and Medicaid.
    - Adopt the three HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Implementation Guides (CRD, DTR, PAS) with clear guidance on their fit, interaction, and usage circumstances.
    - Include both HL7 C-CDA R2 Attachment Implementation Guide and HL7 FHIR Release 4, Section 3.3 for healthcare attachments.
- **Address Payer Dynamics and Provider Education:**
    - The prior authorization approach should incorporate training for providers when PA requests are submitted incorrectly, akin to how commercial payers use denied claims for education.
    - CMS should be required to use certified Health IT for prior authorization to ensure broad provider adoption, as their current "pay first, review later" model does not sufficiently incentivize change.
    - CMS should adopt a progressively aggressive, staged, and short-phased approach to encourage private payers to expedite PA processing.
- **Prioritize Urgent Action on Standards:**
    - ONC should propose certification criteria referencing the FHIR Release 4 base standard even if specific implementation specifications are not yet fully ready, as the risks of inaction (e.g., sicker patients, financial strain, doctor attrition) outweigh potential risks of this staged approach.
- **Design a Flexible and Future-Proof Architecture:**
    - Structure certification criteria from an architectural planning perspective, allowing for modular functional add-ons over time.
    - Identify multiple logical scenario pathways (e.g., less than 5) based on end-user needs, while ensuring flexibility for unforeseen future approaches by including a "reserved for future use" concept.
    - Consider insights from Web3 and Blockchain distributed architecture planning approaches.
- **Set Realistic Implementation Timelines:**
    - Aim to start the use of CDA Attachments IG or FHIR Documents in production for prior authorization transactions in 1Q 2023.
- **Leverage Existing Data for Cost Estimation:**
    - ONC should utilize existing data sources such as Medpac, market basket data, or defined work units to estimate provider costs and time, rather than asking already overworked providers for this information.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- **Prohibitive Costs and Complexity of Certification:**
    - A significant concern is the tendency of some vendors to collude and create high-priced, consulting-based "certifications on top of certifications," as observed in past experiences with HIPAA Security, certified EHR, and interoperability.
    - Current testing and compliance fees for vendors (e.g., 2015 HER certification) are prohibitive for all but the largest vendors, effectively stifling innovation from smaller entities.
    - Providers risk being held captive by excessive certification costs, potentially driving them away from compliance.
- **Lack of Transparency and Accessibility in Certification Processes:**
    - There is a lack of clarity for Health IT vendors on how to find the official website and specific information needed to certify products, even for existing programs like pharmacy-related product certification.
    - Without clear guidance on integrating existing solutions into a broader "Health Hub" structure, organizations like Suncoast RHIO risk contributing to the "stovepipe" issue by adding disconnected point solutions.
- **Neglect of the "Human Factor" in Interoperability:**
    - A major finding from their EMDI pilot was that the "human factor," a large part of interoperability, is often overlooked, leading to implementations that don't fully support healthcare organizations' focus on meeting requirements and patient support over technological mechanics.
- **Misalignment of Payer Incentives, Especially CMS:**
    - CMS's practice of "paying first and reviewing later" significantly reduces the incentive for providers to adopt new prior authorization processes, as the status quo is perceived as functionally adequate, unlike commercial payers who hold payment.
    - Smaller payers, particularly in the face of labor shortages, may prioritize other business needs over fast-tracking PA approvals, as it may not align with their financial models.
- **Risks of Inaction on Standardization:**
    - Delaying the adoption of certification criteria based on core standards (like FHIR Release 4) due to incomplete implementation guides poses severe consequences, contributing to patients getting sicker, healthcare system financial strain, and doctors quitting, making the need for urgent action paramount.
- **Challenges with Emergent Care and Attachments:**
    - In emergent care situations, such as hospice admissions, where healthcare attachments are heavily used, introducing time delays due to prior authorization processes would be problematic.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- **Insights from EMDI Pilot:** Their direct involvement in the CMS demonstration pilot EMDI highlighted two critical observations: first, the often-overlooked "human factor" is a significant component of interoperability; and second, many healthcare organizations are primarily driven by meeting requirements and supporting patients rather than technological intricacies.
- **Personal Experience with Certification Hurdles:** Despite having relevant software and capabilities (PA, cost estimation, X.12, FHIR modules), they personally experienced a lack of clear guidance on how to integrate and certify their solutions into a broader "Health Hub" structure, underscoring practical challenges for vendors.
- **The "Stovepipe" Problem Avoidance:** They explicitly recognize the risk of inadvertently creating more disconnected "point solutions" if new technologies aren't integrated into a cohesive "Health Hub" structure, regardless of certification status.
- **Urgency of Healthcare Crisis:** They convey a compelling sense of urgency regarding the need for electronic prior authorization, stating that "People are getting sicker, money is running out, and doctors are quitting," which strongly frames the imperative for immediate action.
- **Differing Payer Dynamics:** Their insight that CMS's "pay first and review later" model creates a different incentive structure for providers compared to commercial payers (who hold payment) is a nuanced understanding of real-world operational differences impacting adoption.
- **Transparency on Skillset Limitations:** The commenter's frank admission that certain highly technical questions fall "beyond my skillset to address" is a distinctive and practical aspect, reflecting a real-world perspective rather than attempting to answer every query.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Foremost was our finding that a large part of interoperability was the human factor and often left out, and secondly, that many organizations in health care are less focused on the technology of how things work than on meeting requirements and supporting their patients."
- "Beware of the tendency of some vendors, driven by profit as much as improved care and process, to band together creating certifications of their own at high priced consulting based fees... Providers should be able to pick and choose their own methods, products, or approaches without being held captive to certification on top of a certification with a price tag that drives them to not comply."
- "Yes, ONC should pursue this approach and the risks of doing nothing far outweigh not taking this position. People are getting sicker, money is running out, and doctors are quitting. This entire approach, whatever is chosen, is urgent to pursue."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   **Overview**
    *   AEGIS.net, Inc. (AEGIS) is an SBA-designated small business, founded in 1996 with primary offices in Rockville, Maryland.
    *   We provide Information Technology (IT) solutions to commercial industry, the Department of Defense, and federal civilian agencies of the U.S. Government.
    *   AEGIS’ core services consist of:
        *   Software Functional and Performance Testing
        *   Application Design and Development
        *   Organizational Performance / Process Improvement
        *   Project Management and Methodology
    *   We employ our process-driven approach to IT projects, called the AEGIS Shield Methodology.
    *   Based on these processes, AEGIS is certified as an ISO 9001:2015, ISO 20000-1:2018, and ISO 27001:2013.

*   **RFI Response**
    *   **B.1. Company’s experience in healthcare and related domains**
        *   **Experience**
            *   Our journey supporting Healthcare IT has spanned more than two decades, beginning when our principals supported a small-to-medium health plan vendor named AMISYS with group benefits, membership, and a claims processing platform.
            *   From that initial experience, AEGIS’ exposure has grown to support various government programs, including:
                *   HHS/ONC and the NHIN (Nationwide Health Information Network)
                *   HHS/PHS US Public Health Service (Commissioned Corps)
                *   Department of Veterans Affairs (VHA) and the MyHealtheVet Program
                *   The VA and DoD Virtual Lifetime Electronic Record (VLER) Health Program
                *   SSA and the MEGAHIT Program
            *   From government to commercial customers, AEGIS has continuously supported the US Behavioral Care industry for nearly twenty years, including Medco, Value Options Healthcare, and most recently Beacon Health Options (now owned by Anthem BCBS).
            *   Both in Healthcare and in many of AEGIS’ other IT Services, successful IT Service Delivery has seen quality and testing inextricably linked.
        *   Our approach and support of the US Healthcare and Standards community has spanned more than a decade.
            *   Initially with HHS/ONC and NHIN, AEGIS was engaged to build and deploy an Interoperability Test Lab (initially dubbed the ONC “InteropLab”), a Sandbox approach leveraging the NHIN Connect Open Source Initiative (NHIN Reference Implementation).
            *   This was followed by AEGIS’ Developers Integration Lab (DIL) technology, now a patented approach and technology which focused on supporting “Interoperability Testing seeking to validate that multiple systems implementing a particular standard or set of standards can all communicate with one another.”
            *   The DIL further defined an approach to mitigate standards-based implementation by introducing “Interoperability Test-Driven-Development (ITDD), which applies the same principles by incorporating automated testing of interoperability into the development lifecycle.”
            *   For more than five years, the DIL showcased how TDD could be leveraged to accelerate standards-based adoption, supporting Sequoia (formerly Healtheway) so that eHEX participants were able to “self-service” test their implementation and conformance on a daily basis for free.
            *   Other successful programs such as Walgreens and the VA National Immunization Program became a showcase to Healthcare Standardization and Interoperability, most importantly, validating for the VA and the ONC that they were on the correct path.
            *   The AEGIS DIL Technology initially made an appearance at the IHE Connectathon in 2012 and 2013 in support of the ONC VLER Support Program.
            *   It later supported HHS/OPA and Family Planning IHE Questionnaire and Questionnaire-Response implementation.
            *   HHS/OPA was further selected to appear in the HIMSS 2015 Interoperability Showcase, and the implementers showcased how they leveraged IHE and the AEGIS DIL (Test-Driven-Development) to accelerate their adoption and mitigate interoperability issues.
        *   Next, the AEGIS team took those lessons learned, as well as input from the user community, and reimagined the DIL 2.0 as Touchstone v1.0, with a focus toward supporting HL7 FHIR.
            *   AEGIS delivered this to the HL7 FHIR Connectathon community in October 2015, alongside the FHIR DSTU2 Standards definition and specifications for FHIR Testing and the FHIR level one resource FHIR TestScript that ensures “a structured set of tests against a FHIR server or client implementation to determine compliance against the FHIR specification.”
        *   With more than a decade as an HL7 Corporate Member and Contributor, eight-plus years supporting HL7 FHIR, and numerous HL7 Corporate sponsorships (including being an HL7 Benefactor sponsor), HL7 Workgroup Meeting sponsor, and HL7 FHIR Connectathon sponsor, AEGIS has significantly invested in the future of FHIR through the approach of supporting better “continuous” testing with Touchstone.
        *   Recognized as an integrated-Ecosystem, Touchstone now celebrates a community of more than 900 Organizations spanning 54 Countries worldwide.
        *   AEGIS has dedicated resources (FHIR Specification experts, FHIR Implementation experts, Healthcare Profile geeks, and Healthcare Testing specialists) to contribute to the global FHIR Community by advancing numerous FHIR Testing initiatives.
        *   Many made their initial appearances at the HL7 FHIR Connectathon events, including supporting the Da Vinci and CARIN Alliance (Blue Button) Accelerators which went on to have several HIMSS Interop Showcase appearances.
    *   **B.2. Electronic Prior Authorization Standards Request for Comments**
        *   ONC seeks public comments on whether to adopt additional standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria as part of the Certification Program to ensure that technology is available to providers for the automated, electronic completion of prior authorization tasks.
        *   Yes, we do recommend adopting additional certification criteria.
        *   AEGIS.net has been involved in and hosted certification testing programs both in the United States and internationally for many years.
        *   In our experience, participation in the process of designing new guidance, new workflows, and new technologies used in the exchange of healthcare information is highly dependent on whether or not the guidance is mandated.
        *   This mandate may come from an industry group or the government.
        *   While we would like to see collaboration and vigorous testing of planned guidance on a voluntary basis, our experience has shown that the real work in molding specifications comes after it has been directed for use.
        *   To that end, while we hold no opinion on the differing specifications that implementers may use to fulfill the Burden Reduction rules, we respectfully submit that in order for the specifications for Burden Reduction to mature at a meaningful pace, ONC must provide a mandate via a certification program.
        *   **CERTIFIED HEALTH IT FUNCTIONALITY**
            *   Should ONC adopt a certification criterion for prior authorization that accounts for the full, HIPAA compliant workflow for prior authorization transactions including translation from FHIR to the X12 standard? Or should ONC adopt certification criteria that include only the workflows up to the point of translation?
            *   Our recommendation would be to certify up to the point of translation.
                *   This would still meet the community where it is today by allowing implementers to use X12 functionality with translations.
                *   It would plan to align development of future requirements, e.g., Da Vinci PAS FHIR, in concert with supporting a certification criterion for prior authorization.
            *   If ONC were to propose to include these functional capabilities as part of the Certification Program, how should a new certification criterion (or multiple certification criteria) be structured, including technical requirements, attributed standards, and implementation specifications?
            *   Our recommendation would be for separate certification criteria.
                *   We find that once ONC adds a specification or implementation guide to their certification program criteria, implementers come to the table and help move the guides forward through increased participation and testing.
                *   As there is great expense in having to develop to a specification, implementers often wait to see which criteria they are required (by regulatory or industry mandate) to meet before embarking on any development projects.
                *   ONC has a multi-module model in Patient Access, where they point to existing implementation guides and specifications and have separate criteria for different business functions.
                *   In the case of burden reduction, the solution set tends to fall into three functional areas: coverage discovery, information gathering and data preparation, and the prior authorization process.
                *   These areas may each be covered by a separate Health IT module, so it could make logical sense for each to be covered under separate certification criteria.
                *   Implementers could then certify to each certification criterion (functional module) applicable to them.
        *   **IMPLEMENTATION SPECIFICATIONS FOR PRIOR AUTHORIZATION**
            *   What is the current readiness of the three FHIR-based Da Vinci IGs described above [the HL7® FHIR® Da Vinci Coverage Requirements Discovery (CRD), Documentation Templates and Coverage Rules (DTR), and Prior Authorization Support (PAS) IGs] for adoption as part of certification criteria for health IT?
            *   These implementation guides are still in the early stages of community engagement.
                *   This highlights the pattern we saw when building tests against specifications such as the Da Vinci Formulary implementation guide.
                *   In the absence of a mandate, very few implementers participated in the Formulary work groups or at FHIR Connectathons in the Da Vinci Formulary track.
                *   It was not until CMS pointed to the specification for Patient Access that implementers became involved and started addressing issues, testing, and maturing the guide(s).
                *   Based on our experiences with both Da Vinci Formulary and Da Vinci Plan-net (Provider Directory), we see the CRD and DTR in a similar situation.
                *   The Da Vinci Prior Authorization IG has seen wider community engagement, but the workflows defined are still in need of implementer collaboration, and we expect that if they become the basis of a certification program, they will mature at a rapid pace.
            *   Given limited testing of these specifications to date, what would be a feasible timeline for use of these IGs in production for prior authorization transactions?
            *   Based on our experience with the Patient Access guides, we would expect something in the area of 18-24 months would be needed by most implementers to design to these specifications, test thoroughly, and potentially re-implementing to a more mature version of the specifications.
            *   What, if any, additional changes are needed for these IGs prior to adoption as part of certification criteria for health IT?
            *   We recommend that the IGs add testing artifacts to remove ambiguity and ensure implementers fully understand and have implemented the functional requirements.
                *   These testing artifacts need to include suites of TestScript resources paired with test data to enable system-to-system interoperability acceptance testing.
                *   We expand on this recommendation in section B.3.
            *   If the existing IGs are not yet ready for adoption, should ONC still propose certification criteria? Should ONC consider proposing certification criteria incorporating the FHIR Release 4 base standard but delay adopting implementation specifications until a later date? What are the potential risks of this approach?
            *   The sooner ONC defines certification criteria for the existing IG’s, the faster they will be ready for adoption.
                *   The healthcare interoperability community has demonstrated a preference for waiting until a mandate for the adoption of new functionality before taking serious strides towards adoption.
                *   As a result, getting to "ready for adoption" is accelerated by such mandates, not a prerequisite for them.
            *   If possible, ONC should include in the certification criteria the need for explicit acceptance testing, using the testing artifacts described above.
    *   **B.3. The Role Testing serves in Standards, Implementation and Certification**
        *   AEGIS makes the following public comments on the role testing serves in the standards, implementation specifications, and certification to ensure continuous interoperability across the US Healthcare integrated ecosystem.
        *   We urge ONC and the implementer community to examine the evidence that adding testing to implementation guides and certification programs produces valuable results.
        *   This creates a cadence which aligns IG development with test cases being defined, along with sample messages and test data.
        *   This would enable the Reference Implementation (RI) development process to benefit from defined test methods.
        *   With test artifacts in place, test tooling and the ability to calibrate and validate the testing is made faster.
        *   A few data points the Healthcare Community may want to consider in reviewing the future proposed process of Testing:
            *   JMIR Research published by Mitre, AEGIS, and HHS/ONC – Conducted a two-year study into best-practices and best-in-breed approaches to Validation and Testing of Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources Standards Compliance: Data Analysis.
            *   HL7 FHIR Standards and Specifications currently references approaches to testing FHIR Implementations.
            *   HL7 FHIR Standard – Resources / Profiles FHIR TestScript.
            *   FHIR Testing Programs currently being supported (often by Touchstone):
                *   HL7 Da Vinci Accelerators
                *   Drummond CMS Certification and Testing Program
                *   Ontario Health (Pan Canadian) Patient Summary (IPS)
                *   Nictiz – Netherlands national FHIR Implementation (Medmij)
                *   MedCom – Denmark national FHIR Implementation programs
                *   ONC g.10 – Final Rule (21st Century Cures Act) – Inferno
                *   ONC US Core – IGs (Profiles) – Inferno, Touchstone
            *   Implementers will require testing both FHIR Clients and Servers roles:
                *   FHIR Server testing support – Inferno, Touchstone, Crucible
                *   FHIR Client testing support – Touchstone
                *   FHIR Negative testing support – Touchstone
                *   FHIR (Complex) Workflows (multi-actor, multi-organization) Patient Care Coordination exercises such as Prior Authorization – Touchstone
        *   **Qualified Testing Programs**
            *   Testimony about the value of certification processes built on strong testing programs:
                *   **Alexander Henket, Team Lead HL7, Nictiz**: "Nictiz is the Dutch competence centre for electronic exchange of health and care information. We help healthcare move forward with standards based semantic interoperability. The proof of the pudding is in the eating, so a key factor in our portfolio is qualification testing. This is a prerequisite before entering some of the Dutch main infrastructures. Qualification testing builds trust in the system as a whole. For FHIR we have leveraged the AEGIS Touchstone platform from the start. We have done so in favor of extending our home grown solution for HL7 V3/CDA, and after careful selection process. We test vendor implementations for use cases like medication, primary care, patient summary and a dozen others. Over the years AEGIS has treated Nictiz not only as a valued customer but also as a partner in making healthcare better.”
                *   **Timothy Bennett, Director of Strategic Healthcare Initiatives, Drummond Group**: “As the leading Health IT testing and certification organization, when Drummond Group prepared to develop and launch comprehensive FHIR testing and certification services, a survey of the FHIR testing platforms available revealed that Touchstone stood out as the most robust and comprehensive technology. Drummond has incorporated Touchstone as a cornerstone of the future of its FHIR testing and certification for our certification against both ONC and CMS Final Rules requiring FHIR API implementation and any future FHIR testing and certification initiative. It is a core component of our business, and should be considered for widespread adoption of FHIR testing across the board.”
        *   The Healthcare Community may need to specifically define the rubric to be used to “qualify” testing and conformance to a set of standards and specifications such as Prior Authorization.
            *   As an example, the Touchstone team works with the industry (Drummond, and FHIR Connectathon attendees) in order to calibrate the Testing artifacts (TestScript) to ensure the test results correlate with the type of condition of failure.
            *   Note: in more than 50% of the Test Executions completed with a status 200 OK [meaning the message was received], the FHIR message (information exchange) was found to be non-conformant to the FHIR IG or the FHIR data context for testing the patient information exchange.
            *   Complex data rules become guidelines to “assertions” (test rules).
            *   Following the FHIR message exchange, the message is evaluated by these rules to judge compliance with the FHIR specification beyond the FHIR Validation Service (note – FHIR Validation services are not workflow or data context aware).
        *   Touchstone as an example of a Global Community and Integrated-Ecosystem.
        *   **ADVISORY**
            *   Few implementers reference (FHIR) Version numbers at the standards level, IG, or profile level as a required part of their implementation.
            *   This risks introducing ecosystem level breaking changes in the future as standards evolve.
            *   To mitigate these types of risks, robust testing which addresses not only point-in-time Interoperability, but plans for “future proofing” solutions are a necessity for “Continuous Interoperability” which further demonstrates the value of rigorous testing.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: AEGIS.net, a long-standing IT solutions provider and interoperability testing expert, strongly urges ONC to mandate and certify electronic prior authorization standards, advocating for robust, continuous testing to accelerate adoption and ensure specification maturity.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** AEGIS.net, Inc.
- **Type:** Business
- **Role/Expertise:** Information Technology solutions provider with over two decades of experience in healthcare IT, specializing in software functional and performance testing, interoperability test labs (e.g., DIL, Touchstone), and conformance testing for standards like HL7 FHIR. They are an HL7 Corporate Member and Contributor, holding multiple ISO certifications.
- **Geographic Scope:** National (serves US Government, commercial industry), International (their Touchstone platform is used by 900+ organizations in 54 countries). Primary offices in Rockville, Maryland.
- **Stake in Issue:** As a company that develops and provides interoperability testing platforms and services for healthcare IT standards, AEGIS.net has a direct interest in the maturity, mandated adoption, and proper certification of electronic prior authorization standards, as these directly impact their core business and mission to improve healthcare interoperability.
structuredSections.corePosition: We recommend that the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) adopt additional certification criteria for electronic prior authorization standards. Our extensive experience in healthcare IT and testing demonstrates that the real work in molding specifications, ensuring their maturity, and driving widespread adoption comes after a mandate via a certification program has been provided. We believe this mandate, coupled with rigorous and continuous testing built directly into implementation guides, is essential for achieving true interoperability and reducing administrative burden.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Adopt additional certification criteria for electronic prior authorization standards.
  - Provide a mandate via a certification program to ensure meaningful maturation of burden reduction specifications.
- Certify prior authorization functionality up to the point of translation (e.g., from FHIR to X12).
  - This approach aligns with current community capabilities while allowing for future development and alignment with new requirements like Da Vinci PAS FHIR.
- Structure new certification criteria for prior authorization as separate criteria for different functional capabilities.
  - Recognize the three functional areas: coverage discovery, information gathering and data preparation, and the prior authorization process, potentially as separate Health IT modules.
  - This encourages implementer participation and allows them to certify applicable modules.
- Define certification criteria for existing FHIR-based Da Vinci Implementation Guides (CRD, DTR, PAS) as soon as possible.
  - A mandate accelerates readiness for adoption, as the healthcare community typically waits for mandates before seriously pursuing adoption.
- Require the addition of testing artifacts to implementation guides.
  - These artifacts should include suites of TestScript resources paired with test data to enable system-to-system interoperability acceptance testing, removing ambiguity and ensuring full understanding and implementation of functional requirements.
- Include explicit acceptance testing as part of the certification criteria, utilizing the recommended testing artifacts.
- Specifically define a rubric to "qualify" testing and conformance for prior authorization standards and specifications.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- **Lack of Mandate Stifles Adoption and Maturity**: Without a government or industry mandate (e.g., via certification), implementers are reluctant to invest in developing to new specifications, leading to slow maturation of critical implementation guides (e.g., Da Vinci Formulary, CRD, DTR).
  - Implementers often wait for required criteria before embarking on development projects due to high expense.
- **Ambiguity in Implementation Guides**: Current implementation guides lack sufficient testing artifacts, leading to ambiguity in understanding and implementing functional requirements.
- **Risks of Evolving Standards Without Versioning/Testing**: Few implementers reference FHIR version numbers at the standards, IG, or profile level as required, risking ecosystem-level breaking changes as standards evolve.
  - This lack of foresight underscores the need for "future proofing" solutions through rigorous, continuous testing, not just point-in-time interoperability.
- **Non-conformant Data Exchange**: Even when messages are technically "received" (HTTP 200 OK), a significant percentage (over 50%) of FHIR messages exchanged were found to be non-conformant to the FHIR Implementation Guide or the specific FHIR data context, highlighting the inadequacy of basic validation services alone.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- **Testing as a Catalyst for Mandated Adoption**: AEGIS.net's consistent experience shows that the "real work" in molding and maturing specifications for healthcare information exchange only begins after a mandate (regulatory or industry) is issued, demonstrating that mandates accelerate adoption and maturation, rather than requiring pre-existing readiness.
- **Proprietary Testing Technology (DIL/Touchstone)**: Development of patented "Developers Integration Lab (DIL)" technology, later reimagined as "Touchstone," specifically for accelerating standards-based adoption and interoperability testing, showcasing a unique approach to mitigate implementation issues through "Interoperability Test-Driven-Development (ITDD)."
- **Global Reach of Testing Platform**: Touchstone is used by over 900 organizations across 54 countries, supporting various national FHIR implementation programs (e.g., Nictiz in Netherlands, MedCom in Denmark) and major US initiatives (Da Vinci Accelerators, Drummond CMS Certification).
- **Evidence of Non-Conformance in Successful Exchanges**: A critical insight from their testing experience is that even when FHIR messages are technically "received" (HTTP 200 OK), over 50% were found to be non-conformant to the FHIR Implementation Guide or the specific FHIR data context, emphasizing the need for robust testing beyond basic validation services.
- **Testimonial Validation**: Strong endorsements from key industry players like Nictiz (Dutch competence center) and Drummond Group (leading Health IT testing/certification) highlight Touchstone's effectiveness and the value of structured testing programs in building trust and ensuring compliance.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "While we would like to see collaboration and vigorous testing of planned guidance on a voluntary basis, our experience has shown that the real work in molding specifications comes after it has been directed for use."
- "As there is great expense in having to develop to a specification, implementers often wait to see which criteria they are required (by regulatory or industry mandate) to meet before embarking on any development projects."
- "In the absence of a mandate, very few implementers participated in the Formulary work groups or at FHIR Connectathons in the Da Vinci Formulary track. It was not until CMS pointed to the specification for Patient Access that implementers became involved and started addressing issues, testing, and maturing the guide(s)."
- "In more than 50% of the Test Executions completed with a status 200 OK [meaning the message was received], the FHIR message (information exchange) was found to be non‐conformant to the FHIR IG or the FHIR data context for testing the patient information exchange."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   Formed on February 1, 2022, from two leading health systems in Michigan (Beaumont Health and Spectrum Health), BHSH System is a not-for-profit health system.
    *   Provides care and coverage with an exceptional team of 64,000+ dedicated people.
        *   Includes more than 11,500 physicians and advanced practice providers.
        *   Includes more than 15,000 nurses.
    *   Offers services in 22 hospitals, 300+ outpatient locations, and several post-acute facilities.
    *   Includes Priority Health, a provider-sponsored health plan serving over 1.2 million members across the state of Michigan.
    *   Our mission is to boldly create a future where health is simple, affordable, equitable, and exceptional.
*   BHSH System supports the Administration’s efforts to improve healthcare in our country, specifically relating to improving interoperability in prior authorization.
    *   As an integrated health system, we understand the role prior authorization has in:
        *   Ensuring patient safety.
        *   Increasing quality.
        *   Ensuring optimal patient outcomes.
        *   Reducing risk, waste, and variation.
*   Our health system believes that true interoperability in prior authorization will only be achieved if all stakeholders (providers/payers/health IT) work together.
    *   Through transparency.
    *   Through full engagement in standardized processes and expectations.
    *   With the support of health IT infrastructure to create automated systems that meet the unique needs of both providers and payers.
*   To achieve this, our health system is dedicated to exploring solutions to make the prior authorization process more efficient, less burdensome, more cohesive, and standardized for providers and payers by offering the following comments.
*   Because BHSH System is early in its integration journey, the following specific comments are at this time offered only on behalf of the legacy Spectrum Health divisions of BHSH System.
    *   These include 14 hospitals serving primarily West Michigan.
    *   As well as Priority Health, the health plan described above.
*   Reducing Provider and Payer Burden in Prior Authorization
    *   Transparency in Prior Authorization Requirements, Determinations, Member Eligibility, and Payer Benefits
        *   Providers and payers would both benefit greatly from health IT systems that allow providers to access information in a less cumbersome/more easily navigated manner.
            *   To identify prior authorization requirement information.
            *   To identify member benefit level, including authorization determination and eligibility.
        *   As this information can vary by purchaser coverage selection and from payer to payer, providers need a more efficient method to access this information that doesn’t require toggling through different applications, websites, and payer web portals.
        *   It would benefit payers to have providers understand what the requirements are to have services covered, which could reduce authorization denials, authorization appeals, and questions to payer call centers.
        *   Our health plan supports payer autonomy in establishing medical necessity standards and authorization requirements; we desire to have these standards transparent and easily accessible to providers.
        *   We support purchasers’ rights to select benefit and coverage levels.
        *   We encourage other payers to be transparent with their prior authorization requirements to foster a more cohesive relationship with providers in this process.
        *   Health IT partners can support providers and payers in achieving this by creating an interface that allows this information to be gathered in one place.
    *   Promoting Transparency While Upholding Privacy Standards
        *   Providers need to access multiple sources of information from payers to ensure an authorization is submitted correctly and to track the status of an authorization; this requires access to payer and payer third-party systems.
        *   While our health plan promotes transparency in our prior authorization standards and requirements, we also have concerns about jeopardizing our privacy standards and exposing proprietary information by providing too much access.
        *   To bridge this gap, we recommend establishing standards and expectations about what level of payer information should be accessible to the right people at the right time (including providers contracted and not contracted with the health plan).
        *   Additionally, we encourage health IT partners to create APIs and systems that protect patient and payer privacy, while also providing access to the appropriate level of information needed by providers.
    *   Standard Authorization Turnaround Times
        *   It is important that providers know when they can anticipate receiving an authorization determination in order to facilitate patient care appropriately.
        *   Priority Health strives to turn around authorization determinations quickly, with a significant portion of our authorizations being auto-approved as opposed to going through a manual review process.
        *   We understand that this may not be the case for all payers.
        *   We recommend standardizing turnaround times for authorizations across all plan types (Commercial, Medicare, Medicaid).
        *   We encourage payers to automate their authorization determination processes as much as possible.
        *   The standard turnaround time needs to take into account:
            *   The review process needed for non-automated requests or requests that need further review.
            *   The request is submitted in a timely manner based on when the service is needed.
            *   That all information required to review the authorization has been submitted by the provider.
    *   Patient Interactions with Prior Authorizations
        *   A significant consideration in creating interoperability in prior authorization is the impact on patients.
        *   Any changes made to prior authorization should be done with the best interest of the patient in mind.
        *   As a health system, we desire to see benefits managed and authorizations processed in a timely manner to ensure patients have access to the care they need at the right time.
        *   This will be achieved through standardized processes and expectations that both the provider and payer fully embrace and partner in to deliver with the support of health IT.
        *   We believe that healthcare should be transparent for the patients we serve and that patients should have a voice in the care they receive.
        *   As such, we support the sharing of clinical information with patients and encourage them to discuss any questions they have with their provider.
        *   Providers should be the source of information sharing with a member as a relationship already exists between the patient and their provider.
        *   Payers should not have the responsibility of sharing patient information with patients.
            *   While patient information is shared with payers to manage patient benefits and make authorization determinations, the patient’s information should come from the provider as they are best suited to have discussions with patients about any questions they may have.
        *   Priority Health supports price transparency for our services but transparency in patient clinical information should be owned by the provider.
        *   Additionally, patient information should be shared with patients at a level that does not create confusion for the patient.
            *   Sharing too much information with a patient can create burden to the provider in handling questions and concerns received from patients (this will particularly cause burden to the payer if they are required to share clinical information to members).
        *   We encourage health IT partners to create solutions for patient information sharing that upholds all HIPAA standards.
        *   Additionally, patients should have a choice in who their clinical information is shared with.
            *   As such, health IT solutions should be created in a manner that allows a patient to opt in/out of where their information is being shared and stored (e.g., third-party applications).
*   Technical Recommendations
    *   Priority Health has been involved in prior initiatives regarding interoperability in information sharing.
    *   In these implementations, we used HL7 FHIR standards.
    *   Due to the alignment between former initiatives and the goal of prior authorization interoperability, we encourage HL7 FHIR standards to be used consistently with future implementations to ensure consistency.
    *   Additionally, we support the use of EDI 278 / 275 standards versus custom implementations and using SMART on FHIR.
    *   We encourage standardization in implementation requirements as much as possible.
*   We appreciate the consideration of our comments relating to achieving interoperability in prior authorization.
*   As an integrated health system, our goal is to reach interoperability in a way that supports both the provider and payer unique concerns with the prior authorization process.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: BHSH System, an integrated Michigan health system including Priority Health, advocates for standardized, transparent, and automated electronic prior authorization processes leveraging health IT to reduce burden on providers and payers, ensure patient access, and protect privacy.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Priority Health (submitted by BHSH System)
- **Type:** Organization
- **Role/Expertise:** BHSH System (formed from Beaumont Health and Spectrum Health) is a not-for-profit health system with over 64,000 staff, including 11,500+ physicians and 15,000+ nurses, operating 22 hospitals and 300+ outpatient locations. It includes Priority Health, a provider-sponsored health plan serving over 1.2 million members. The comments are specifically on behalf of the legacy Spectrum Health divisions and Priority Health. David M. Leonard is Chief Legal Officer and Senior Vice President of Strategic Partnerships for Spectrum Health.
- **Geographic Scope:** State (Michigan), with legacy Spectrum Health divisions serving primarily West Michigan.
- **Stake in Issue:** As an integrated health system that is both a large provider network and a health plan, BHSH System has a direct stake in improving prior authorization processes to reduce administrative burden for both providers and payers, ensure efficient patient care, protect patient and proprietary data, and achieve true interoperability in healthcare.
structuredSections.corePosition: Our health system believes that true interoperability in prior authorization will only be achieved if all stakeholders (providers/payers/health IT) work together in transparency through full engagement in standardized processes and expectations with the support of health IT infrastructure to create automated systems that meet the unique needs of both providers and payers. We are dedicated to exploring solutions to make the prior authorization process more efficient, less burdensome, more cohesive, and standardized for providers and payers.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Establish standards and expectations about what level of payer information should be accessible to the right people at the right time, including providers contracted and not contracted with the health plan.
- Encourage health IT partners to create APIs and systems that protect patient and payer privacy while providing appropriate information access to providers.
- Standardize turnaround times for authorizations across all plan types (Commercial, Medicare, Medicaid).
  - This standard needs to account for the review process for non-automated requests or those needing further review.
  - It should consider timely submission based on when the service is needed.
  - It should also consider that all required information for review has been submitted by the provider.
- Encourage payers to automate their authorization determination processes as much as possible.
- Encourage health IT partners to create solutions for patient information sharing that uphold all HIPAA standards.
- Create health IT solutions that allow patients to opt in/out of where their information is being shared and stored (e.g., third-party applications), as patients should have a choice in who their clinical information is shared with.
- Encourage HL7 FHIR standards to be used consistently with future implementations to ensure consistency, given their alignment with prior interoperability initiatives.
- Support the use of EDI 278 / 275 standards versus custom implementations and using SMART on FHIR.
- Encourage standardization in implementation requirements as much as possible.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Providers face significant burden having to toggle through different applications, websites, and payer web portals to access varying prior authorization requirement information, member benefit levels, authorization determinations, and eligibility.
- There is a risk of jeopardizing privacy standards and exposing proprietary information if excessive access is provided to payer systems and payer third-party systems.
- The lack of standardized turnaround times for authorization determinations across various plan types makes it difficult for providers to plan and facilitate timely patient care.
- Sharing too much clinical information directly with patients by payers could lead to patient confusion and create an undue burden on providers (and payers) in handling subsequent questions and concerns.
- The responsibility for sharing patient clinical information should primarily remain with the provider, as they have the existing relationship and are best suited for these sensitive discussions, rather than being shifted to payers.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- As an integrated health system encompassing both a large provider network (legacy Spectrum Health) and a major health plan (Priority Health), BHSH System offers a unique, dual perspective on the operational challenges and opportunities within the prior authorization process.
- Priority Health demonstrates a practical commitment to efficiency by auto-approving a significant portion of its authorizations, indicating successful internal process automation.
- The organization has prior experience and success using HL7 FHIR standards in other interoperability initiatives, offering a proven pathway for future standardization in prior authorization.
- They provide a nuanced view on information transparency, advocating for price transparency for services but asserting that clinical information transparency for patients should be "owned by the provider" to prevent confusion and maintain the integrity of the patient-provider relationship.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "Our health system believes that true interoperability in prior authorization will only be achieved if all stakeholders (providers/payers/health IT) work together in transparency through full engagement in standardized processes and expectations with the support of health IT infrastructure to create automated systems that meets the unique needs of both providers and payers."
- "While our health plan promotes transparency in our prior authorization standards and requirements, we also have concerns about jeopardizing our privacy standards and exposing proprietary information by providing too much access."
- "Priority Health supports price transparency for our services but transparency in patient clinical information should be owned by the provider. Additionally, patient information should be shared with patients at a level that does not create confusion for the patient."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The American Medical Informatics Association (AMIA) appreciates the opportunity to respond to the Request for Information (RFI) on electronic prior authorization (ePA) standards, implementation specifications, and certification criteria.
    *   AMIA is the professional home for over 5,500 informatics professionals, including clinicians, researchers, and public health experts, who work to make data meaningful, manage information, and generate new knowledge in healthcare.
    *   AMIA plays a leading role in advancing health and wellness by moving basic research findings to clinical practice and evaluating interventions, innovations, and public policy.
    *   A work group of seven AMIA member experts prepared this response, reviewed by the AMIA President and Board Chair.
    *   AMIA commends ONC for soliciting feedback and recognizing the multiple stakeholders involved.
*   Consistent with prior comments to CMS, AMIA supports efforts to address prior authorization workflow through application program interface (API) standards.
    *   We support CMS’s intent to provide more specific guidance to implementers.
    *   More consistent exchanges between payers and providers could reduce current burdens if implemented thoughtfully.
    *   While the goals are laudable, proposals must also address the underlying causes of prior authorization burden.
    *   New Electronic Health Record (EHR) certification criteria are needed, and AMIA has previously recommended that CMS establish industry adoption timelines for health IT.
    *   Streamlining the prior authorization process through FHIR-enabled APIs will only succeed if these requirements are built into certification.
        *   AMIA has urged CMS to determine such adoption requirements.
*   **1) Functional capabilities for electronic prior authorization in certified health IT**
    *   Development of consistent standards for data and/or supporting documentation required from providers should be mandatory for payers to participate in future automated Health IT systems for prior authorization.
        *   While some variation is necessary for certain specialties and unique clinical scenarios, more consistency in required data could allow for more automation and decrease administrative burden.
    *   Consider incentivizing payers to participate in future standardization and automation solutions.
*   **2) Additional approaches to support electronic prior authorization: healthcare attachments**
    *   ONC notes existing capabilities and standards in certified health IT products may facilitate elements of prior authorization workflows, including supporting clinical documents for attachments.
    *   ONC should provide guidelines and test examples to verify the integrity of healthcare attachment implementation and demonstrate its effectiveness per vendor.
    *   The timeline for implementation should have a two-track goal:
        *   Soft implementation of the exchange.
        *   Hard implementation deadline to follow.
    *   There is a need to promulgate timelines that responsibly mitigate prior authorization burdens on patients and providers, while giving adequate time for public and private payers to comply.
    *   FHIR Documents would facilitate better exchange and provide support for other applications built on the FHIR protocol.
    *   While having both Clinical Document Architecture (CDA) Attachments and FHIR Documents could benefit implementation, it would inevitably lead to confusion and potential interoperability issues.
        *   Therefore, FHIR Documents is the most appropriate protocol to adopt.
*   **3) Impact on providers**
    *   Prior authorization leads to disruptions in clinical practice and workflow management.
    *   Achieving uniform electronic prior authorization processes will ease clinician burden in an environment where clinicians manage increasing numbers of patients with complex conditions.
    *   Integrated electronic prior authorization could reduce fragmentation between payer and prior authorization systems by:
        *   Ensuring payer information is up-to-date.
        *   Reducing misfirings or misclassifications of medications that may not currently require prior authorization.
        *   Decreasing cognitive load and burden for providers.
    *   Computerization of prior authorization alone will not ensure better efficiency or higher adherence to treatment plans.
    *   A problematic situation would arise if not all payers participate in an integrated system or network.
        *   This would require providers to track participating and non-participating payers.
        *   Lack of participation creates further gaps in information processing and generalizability of workflow.
    *   Providers are burdened by two care delivery systems:
        *   One supporting traditional prior authorization methods (phone, fax, etc.).
        *   Another using electronic prior authorization.
        *   An estimated 75% of providers who use electronic prior authorization also use other prior authorization methods.
        *   These two different systems require different workflows, which create additional time constraints and inefficiencies when used concurrently.
    *   Creating a system that aligns with the most efficient and safe workflows to support providers and patients is the most important goal.
        *   These systems must be evaluated on their own merit.
        *   For example, if electronic prior authorization creates efficiencies but then causes time constraints (e.g., requiring providers to address many in-basket messages about denials/approvals), efficiency may be lost, and patient care could suffer.
        *   Therefore, research using user-centered design approaches to evaluate the impact of different prior authorization systems and workflows on providers and patients is critically needed at each step of the system's life cycle.
    *   A multitude of problems with prior authorization could be addressed by good human-centered design principles and standardization.
        *   For instance, electronic portals do not always provide correct forms.
        *   Providers must interface with multiple pharmacy benefits managers (PBMs), each with unique formulary, prior authorization forms, and formats.
*   **4) Impact on patients**
    *   AMIA is concerned about barriers to patient care resulting from prior authorization processes.
    *   Access to essential care is often delayed due to burdensome prior authorization requirements.
    *   Electronic prior authorization could enhance capabilities to suggest medication alternatives rather than outright rejecting medications.
        *   This could help patients understand available alternatives as they make healthcare decisions.
    *   The impact of out-of-pocket costs to patients for the development and implementation of different prior authorization systems must be understood, especially if increased costs do not lead to more efficient and enhanced care.
    *   Electronic prior authorization systems may not alleviate differences across insurance plan formularies, which can create fragmentation and difficult communication.
        *   As we invest in these technologies, underlying differences need to be addressed.
    *   There could be a lack of transparency for patients with electronic delivery regarding the reasoning behind payer choices in offering treatments.
        *   This could reduce trust in care and potentially increase healthcare disparities.
    *   The 2022 Medication Access Report found that 21% of patients experienced a delay in receiving medication over the last 12 months due to prior authorization.
    *   AMIA believes that including prior authorization information as part of a Patient Access API is ultimately essential.
        *   However, considerable development by information technology providers would be required.
        *   Until patient-facing third-party apps are better able to consume data from prior authorization APIs, the gain will be minimal.
        *   Solving this problem will require coordination among patient-facing app developers, payers, and providers.
*   **5) Payer implementation**
    *   There is a need for payers to partner and collaborate with the informatics communities to best configure processes, innovation, and resources to improve and streamline prior authorization practices.
        *   The goal is to minimize inefficiencies for patients and providers.
    *   AMIA would be pleased to serve as a resource to the payer community as the regulatory framework takes shape.
*   Thank you for your consideration of these comments.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The American Medical Informatics Association (AMIA) supports electronic prior authorization (ePA) through FHIR-enabled APIs and health IT certification but emphasizes the critical need for mandatory payer participation, consistent standards, user-centered design, and addressing underlying workflow burdens to ensure efficient and effective care for both providers and patients.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** American Medical Informatics Association (AMIA)
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Professional home for over 5,500 informatics professionals (clinicians, researchers, public health experts) who advance health and wellness by applying biomedical and health informatics.
- **Geographic Scope:** National
- **Stake in Issue:** Represents health informatics professionals whose work is directly impacted by health IT regulations, particularly those concerning electronic prior authorization standards and their implementation in certified health IT. They aim to reduce administrative burden and improve care through effective health IT.
structuredSections.corePosition: We support efforts to address prior authorization workflow through application program interface (API) standards, as more consistent exchanges between payers and providers could reduce current burdens if implemented thoughtfully. While the goals are laudable, proposals must also address the underlying causes of prior authorization burden, and the goal of streamlining the process through FHIR-enabled APIs will only ultimately be successful if these requirements are built into certification.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Make the development of consistent data and documentation standards mandatory for payers to participate in future automated prior authorization systems.
  - Incentivize payers to participate in future standardization and automation solutions.
- ONC should provide guidelines and test examples to verify the integrity and demonstrate the effectiveness of healthcare attachment implementation per vendor.
- Implement a two-track timeline for healthcare attachment exchange: a soft implementation followed by a hard implementation deadline.
- Adopt FHIR Documents as the most appropriate protocol for healthcare attachments, rather than supporting both CDA Attachments and FHIR Documents.
- CMS should establish industry adoption timelines for health IT related to prior authorization.
- Conduct research using user-centered design approaches to evaluate the impact of different prior authorization systems and their workflows on providers and patients at each step of the systems life cycle.
- Include prior authorization information as part of a Patient Access API.
  - Acknowledge that this will require considerable development by IT providers.
  - Recognize that minimal gains will occur until patient-facing third-party apps can better consume this data.
  - Emphasize the need for coordination among patient-facing app developers, payers, and providers to solve this.
- Payers should partner and collaborate with informatics communities to configure processes, innovation, and resources to improve and streamline prior authorization practices, aiming to minimize inefficiencies for patients and providers.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Proposals for electronic prior authorization may not address the underlying causes of prior authorization burden.
- Lack of mandatory consistent data and documentation standards from payers could continue administrative burden.
- Potential for confusion and interoperability issues if both Clinical Document Architecture (CDA) Attachments and FHIR Documents are supported for healthcare attachments.
- Prior authorization processes lead to disruptions in clinical practice and workflow management for providers.
- The risk that computerization of prior authorization alone will not guarantee better efficiency or higher adherence to treatment plans.
- If not all payers participate in an integrated electronic prior authorization system, it will create further information processing gaps and dual workflows, requiring providers to track participating vs. non-participating payers.
- Electronic prior authorization, if poorly implemented, could create new time constraints for providers (e.g., managing excessive in-basket messages for denials/approvals), negating efficiency gains and potentially harming patient care.
- Electronic portals often do not provide correct forms, and providers must interface with multiple pharmacy benefits managers (PBMs), each with unique formulary, forms, and formats.
- Prior authorization processes create barriers to patient care and cause delays in accessing essential care.
- Lack of understanding regarding the impact of out-of-pocket costs to patients for ePA system development and implementation, especially if it doesn't lead to more efficient or enhanced care.
- Electronic prior authorization systems may not alleviate differences across insurance plan formularies, leading to continued fragmentation and communication difficulties.
- Potential for lack of transparency for patients with electronic delivery regarding the reasoning behind payer choices in offering treatments, which could reduce trust and increase healthcare disparities.
- Significant development is required for IT providers and patient-facing apps to consume prior authorization API data effectively, otherwise, the initial gain from Patient Access APIs will be minimal.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- An estimated 75% of providers who use electronic prior authorization also rely on other traditional prior authorization methods (e.g., phone, fax), indicating a persistent dual-system burden and inefficiency.
- Computerization alone is not a panacea for prior authorization burdens; careful, user-centered design is critically needed to ensure true efficiency and avoid creating new time constraints or workflow problems for providers.
- Specific data highlights patient impact: The 2022 Medication Access Report found that 21% of patients experienced a delay in receiving medication over the last 12 months due to prior authorization.
- The insight that supporting both CDA Attachments and FHIR Documents for prior authorization attachments would lead to confusion and interoperability issues, strongly advocating for the exclusive adoption of FHIR Documents.
- While including prior authorization information in a Patient Access API is essential, it's recognized as a complex undertaking requiring significant development and coordination among app developers, payers, and providers before meaningful patient benefit can be realized.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "While the goals are laudable, proposals must also address the underlying causes of prior authorization burden."
- "Computerizations of prior authorization will not alone be the answer to better efficiency or higher adherence to treatment plans."
- "The 2022 Medication Access Report found that 21% of patients experienced a delay in receiving medication over the last 12 months due to prior authorization."

submitter: CMS Data Element Library - Health IT Workgroup

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid (CMS) Data Element Library (DEL), Health Information Technology Workgroup (HITWG) evaluated the RFI on Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria.
*   Our comments are based on knowledge and work with post-acute care (PAC) settings, the standardized data collected via federally required PAC assessment instruments, and how this data supports interoperability and exchange.
*   PAC assessment instruments and settings include:
    *   Long-term Care Hospitals: LTCH Continuity Assessment Record & Evaluation (CARE) Data Set (LCDS)
    *   Skilled Nursing Facilities/Nursing Facilities: Resident Assessment Instrument (RAI) Minimum Data Set (MDS)
    *   Home Health Care Agencies: Outcome and Assessment Information Set (OASIS)
    *   Inpatient Rehab Facilities: IRF Patient Assessment Instrument (IRFPAI)
    *   Hospice: Hospice Item Set (HIS)
    *   Home and Community-based Service Providers: HCBS Functional Assessment Standardized Items (FASI)
*   The PACIO Project is an HL7 FHIR Initiative aimed at improving interoperable health information exchange between long-term and post-acute care providers and their partners.
    *   PACIO’s work includes the exchange of CMS PAC assessment instruments (in full or in part) and the use of the CMS Data Element Library (DEL).
    *   This work supports the patient in their healthcare journey and reduces administrative burden for providers.
    *   Leaders of the PACIO Project are also engaged in DaVinci initiatives related to payers, payment, and prior authorization.

*   § Section A and B – ONC Health IT Certification Program and Requirements Under HIPAA for Electronic Prior Authorization Transaction Standards
    *   Functions and Standards
        *   Q1. Should ONC adopt certification criteria that includes compliance with HIPAA transactions (e.g. FHIR and X12)? What are ongoing challenges providers will face? If new certification criteria were to be in place how should they be structured?
            *   PAC settings are required to exchange documentation using prior authorization.
            *   However, PAC is not a part of the ONC Health IT certification program.
            *   We recommend that the ability to exchange documents not be limited to certification status, because it would exclude providers and services within PAC.
            *   An alternative phased-in approach or mechanism for those who are not part of the certification program is requested.
            *   We fully support the specification of standards being utilized; however, we do not support limiting the standard based on certification status.
            *   The goal should be to make it easier to submit and have industry standards to create efficiency and reduce burden by facilitating the use of electronic prior authorization without penalizing a setting for not having certified program status.

*   § Section C – Recent Efforts To Advance Electronic Prior Authorization Processes
    *   Implementation Specifications for Prior Authorization
        *   Q2. If not ready, should ONC still propose requirements? Integration of future Implementation Guides (IGs)? Effectiveness of DaVinci IGs in supporting federal, state and health plan requirements? Alternative approaches to explore for certification criteria if not DaVinci; Alternative approaches to the workflow than what was proposed in DaVinci? New IGs that need to be developed?
            *   PAC assessments provide structured data available for exchange via an API and can support pre-claim and prior authorization decisions as appropriate.
            *   The current DEL can support IG development, such as with the PACIO IGs, to exchange PAC assessment instruments and content.
            *   For example, the Home Health PAC assessment instrument is completed on admission and at subsequent intervals, collecting administrative and clinical content that could support information needed for prior authorizations and pre/post claim (e.g., information on administrative/identifying data, functional status and mobility, mental function, and nutrition).
