- OCHIN highlights the critical need for new standards and implementation guides to be "fully tested to ensure functionality in real world settings, including, at a minimum, in limited resourced provider settings," emphasizing the unique challenges faced by safety-net providers.
- A crucial design principle for API solutions is that they "must ensure that providers and patients who are not subject to prior authorization do not need to navigate any of the processes," preventing unnecessary burden.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "While utilization management can be an important tool, the proliferation of these requirements for well-established, evidence-based clinical practice and therapeutics creates a form of structural inequality that drives provider burn-out and worse outcomes for patients, particularly those in underserved communities who are among the most clinically and socially complex."
- "Modernized technology and national standards are key ingredients to optimized delivery and interoperability, but not sufficient without the workforce able to deploy the systems as intended."
- "Even routine software upgrades can slightly alter the look and feel of the OCHIN Epic electronic health records (EHR) system in a way that has the potential to impact end-user workflows."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   Medical Information Technology, Inc. (MEDITECH) offers comments on the Electronic Prior Authorization Standards, Implementation Specifications, and Certification Criteria RFI.
*   ePrior Authorization (ePA) is necessary for continued healthcare interoperability.
*   Applauds the Da Vinci Project for outlining the ePA workflow and advancing FHIR.
*   MEDITECH's preference is to code to FHIR, and ONC is encouraged to incorporate FHIR standards into certification.
*   Overall recommendations for the program, echoing the EHRA's comment:
    *   Appreciates the opportunity to provide input on automating and reducing prior authorization burdens.
    *   ePA is a complex process requiring collaboration across many domains within healthcare organizations and with health plans.
    *   Streamlining ePA requires significant coordination and standardization among stakeholders and health IT tools.
    *   The three Da Vinci Project Implementation Guides (IGs)—Coverage Requirements Determination (CRD), Documentation Templates and Rules (DTR), and Prior Authorization Support (PAS)—provide a good starting point.
        *   These IGs outline the general flow and interactions across modules and systems for providers and health plans for a complete end-to-end workflow.
        *   Da Vinci Project's efforts have made significant progress toward automated ePA workflows.
        *   However, these IGs are not yet mature enough for Health IT certification adoption.
    *   The boundaries of Health IT support for specific interactions are intentionally undefined within the Da Vinci guides.
        *   Requiring certification to an entire prior authorization workflow for provider-focused Health IT is premature without clearly defined, granular interaction sets.
        *   ONC should recognize the need for flexibility and not allocate all provider-side interactions to one Health IT solution.
        *   To provide flexibility and ensure provider software support, a staged approach is suggested:
            *   Stage 1:
                *   Work with CMS to focus adoption and certification of CRD, DTR, and PAS IGs by payers to establish clear implementation standards for payer interactions supporting prior authorization.
                *   Do not set certification criteria for provider-focused Health IT until there is clarity on how specific functional needs, supported by the IGs, map to various systems (certified EHRs, non-certified Health IT, SMART on FHIR Apps).
                    *   Standardized payer APIs for prior authorizations will encourage Health IT developers to adopt the same standards for interactions their systems perform.
                    *   ONC should make test labs available for individual IG interactions.
            *   Stage 2:
                *   Establish Health IT criteria based on matured and evolved interaction distributions across Health IT.
                *   Typical interaction sets should be clearly documented within each of the CRD, DTR, and PAS IGs.
                *   Extend the provider functional requirement for prior authorization engagement using certified Health IT to support the workflow.
                *   Providers should be fully supported over time by a suite of certified Health IT for the full prior authorization workflow to avoid incomplete workflows and lack of support assurance.
        *   Initial focus of Stage 1 should be on CRD and PAS interactions due to the complexity of DTR capabilities on the provider side.
            *   Requires full transparency of data requirements for each service/item/procedure needing authorization to enable automated data gathering.
            *   Complexities include adopting data collection automation using CQL and/or Questionnaires.
            *   A timely transition from manual to automated data collection with workflow management and follow-up request management is imperative, considering diverse payer plans and data requirements.
            *   Once Stage 1 matures, a Stage 1B or blending with Stage 2 could occur.
*   **Certified Health IT Functionality**
    *   The described functional capabilities generally cover the overall prior authorization workflow, but some areas are missing (e.g., consumer-focused query ability for prior authorization status updates).
    *   Capabilities can be supported by multiple forms of Health IT (provider and payer side), requiring a modular, finer-grained certification approach.
    *   Translating in/out of X12 should be addressed as a separate capability, allowing isolation for specific Health IT modules.
        *   Provider-focused Health IT does not operate in the X12 transaction space and does not need X12 capabilities.
        *   Intermediaries may already have X12 translation and transmission capabilities.
    *   ONC should not adopt a singular ePA criterion that encompasses all capabilities for the full ePA workflow.
        *   SMART Apps and intermediaries enable various stages (e.g., interaction with payers for coverage determination, documentation, submission).
        *   An EHR may only need to initiate the workflow and receive status change notifications.
        *   ONC should allow Health IT developers discretion to certify to some or all adopted ePA criteria.
    *   Current Da Vinci IGs, individually or together, would yield too coarse a certification criterion for a single Health IT solution (provider or payer) to support fully.
        *   The community, in collaboration with ONC, should establish more granular interaction building blocks.
        *   Individual Health IT solutions should select applicable criteria they can fulfill and include other software for other interactions.
        *   The staged approach, where Health IT certification criteria are introduced in Stage 2 after sufficient experience and maturity, is crucial.
        *   Payers can use the current ballot cycle of the three Da Vinci IGs to establish an initial interaction set for certification criteria, maturing through actual implementations.
*   **Implementation Specification For Prior Authorization**
    *   Deployed solutions are minimal, but some Health IT suppliers (including payers) have started initial rollouts using SMART Apps.
    *   The phased approach outlined (payer implementation first) allows calibration with Da Vinci IGs and payers, providing Health IT with further analysis.
        *   This initial phase should not require Health IT certification.
        *   Similar to EHRs being certified to FHIR US Core APIs while connecting applications are not.
        *   Focus on CRD and PAS IGs initially, as DTR IG involves more complexity and learning.
        *   DTR should not stop progress, as Health IT can connect to payers for DTR when relevant payer Health IT is certified.
    *   Supports certification criteria requiring FHIR R4 or the latest FHIR core standards as the "way of the future."
    *   The staged approach provides an anchor around payer-supported interactions defined in Da Vinci IGs for provider Health IT to target.
        *   Payer Health IT based APIs can be couched with existing Da Vinci IGs as certification criteria.
        *   Provider Health IT criteria should be phased in later.
    *   Da Vinci IGs, combined with the SVAP process, provide a path forward if there is no requirement for a single Health IT solution to cover all interactions.
        *   Certification for Health IT must be phased in once appropriate interaction building blocks are established and validated.
    *   ONC's original 2015 Certification Edition approach (not specifying a standard) would not be viable, as it would lead to more variations that cannot interoperate.
    *   Interoperability IGs specify functional requirements (e.g., data retention, signatures, security, integrity) that should be addressed separately as general system requirements, not unique to prior authorization.
    *   Requiring review and signature for automated data collection introduces an extra human step.
        *   Concern with e-signature requirement when the industry wants automated data collection and already has user access controls, audit logs, and security for certified EHRs.
        *   Relevant certification criteria should focus on non-EHR Health IT to ensure fundamental capabilities are in place.
    *   An alternative to certification-based approach: voluntary industry rallying around the Da Vinci process, driven by CMS policy incentives rather than certification.
        *   Recognizes competing priorities and resource needs for other customer-requested capabilities not subject to certification.
    *   While opportunities exist to reduce prior authorizations based on determination level (item, condition, population), the workflow is essentially as described once PA is required.
        *   Optimization must focus on automatically collecting relevant data.
        *   Simplification of rules and electronic expression based on discrete data (akin to eCQMs and eDQMs) should be considered.
        *   Another way to simplify is fewer requirements for granular PAs, emphasizing general (chronic) conditions.
    *   Targeted guidance for the following capabilities would be helpful:
        *   Consumer apps to obtain authorization status (including awareness of submission).
        *   Translation in/out of X12.
        *   Notification of coverage requirement determination outcome to the initiating system.
        *   Notification of authorization request to the initiating system and other interested systems.
        *   Payer/health plan endpoint directory for respective interactions.
*   **Healthcare Attachment Standards**
    *   Introducing an extra transformation step to populate the PAS prior authorization request is unnecessary.
    *   The concept of a "document" for packaging purposes has no real value.
    *   Review and signature should not be required in automated data collection, even if it could make "document" use appealing, as it adds burden.
    *   The approach already in the PAS IG, enabling attachment of individual data points and documents of various kinds (e.g., C-CDA, FHIR, .pdf), is appropriate, eliminating the need for a dedicated C-CDA based document for packaging.
    *   Does not see value in introducing the notion of a document into the prior authorization flow and suggests substantial effort could be focused elsewhere.
    *   Submission of data sets (individual data, C-CDA or FHIR documents, other non-structured documents) should be permitted as supported by the current PAS IG.
    *   Does not support required translation into or creation of a document where one did not previously exist.
    *   Does not support a focus on introducing a document-based approach for supporting information.
    *   The Da Vinci approach of using a data set approach is appropriate in automated ePA workflows where relevant data is automatically retrieved by clear documentation requirements.
    *   Not aware of widespread use of C-CDA and certainly not FHIR documents for prior authorization.
    *   Creating new documents solely for packaging necessary data for prior authorization is unnecessary.
    *   Moving toward FHIR-based, automated workflows that can include original source data points and/or documents as available.
*   **Impact on Patients**
    *   Reducing time for the prior authorization process would be substantial.
    *   Patients would receive services faster, and the system could facilitate document exchange.
    *   Patients would understand payer responsibility for services and make informed decisions.
    *   Automation reduces touch points and failure points.
    *   The patient portal is an excellent way to communicate PA status.
    *   Most patients want a hands-off approach to ePA requests; no requirement for patient action should be in certification.
*   **Impact on Providers**
    *   Manual process creates lag time; ePA in EHR would streamline user workflow.
    *   Automated documentation and requests would significantly reduce provider burnout.
    *   Reduces back-and-forth between organization and payers.
    *   Clearing Houses could assist in a touchless process.
    *   Less back-and-forth could allow staff reallocation due to fewer FTE needs.
    *   Expects providers to use this functionality if it is automated.
    *   Responses from payers are of interest to initiating users, back-office revenue cycle staff, and patients.
    *   Believes this is a needed functionality and most will adopt, but incentives for use of new functionality are important.
    *   Encourages ONC to implement certification requirements with clear specifications and IGs before imposing regulation.
    *   If specifications and/or payers are not ready, the process will continue to burden providers.
    *   Needs widespread, simultaneous implementation; partial coverage (certain types of care/payers) will likely cause more frustration.
*   **Impact on Developers**
    *   Estimates 1.5 years from design to deployment for ePA functionality.
    *   Coarse certification approach and early certification for Health IT (before granular, mature operational experience) would substantially burden EHRs.
        *   EHRs would have to implement all capabilities in one phase and coordinate with other Health IT.
        *   DTR capabilities would be particularly challenging due to managing data collection requirements across 1500+ health plans and coordinating automated/manual follow-up.
        *   These are administrative processes typically outside EHR scope, though EHRs are prime sources for supporting information via FHIR US Core based APIs.
    *   The staged approach outlined provides a path to evolve necessary support in manageable steps, allowing each relevant Health IT to advance and focus contributions.
    *   Always encourages working towards FHIR in the industry.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: MEDITECH, a health IT developer, strongly supports electronic prior authorization and FHIR standards but urges a phased, modular certification approach that prioritizes payer adoption of Da Vinci IGs before imposing broad requirements on provider-focused Health IT.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
-   **Name/Organization:** Medical Information Technology, Inc. (MEDITECH)
-   **Type:** Organization
-   **Role/Expertise:** Health IT developer, specifically for electronic health records (EHRs).
-   **Geographic Scope:** National
-   **Stake in Issue:** As a developer of certified Health IT, new certification criteria directly impact their product development, costs, and timeline, influencing their ability to support healthcare providers with effective ePA solutions.
structuredSections.corePosition: We believe ePrior Authorization (ePA) is necessary to continue healthcare interoperability and we applaud the Da Vinci Project for outlining the ePA workflow and pushing FHIR forward. Our preference continues to be to code to FHIR, and we encourage ONC to strive to incorporate FHIR standards into certification. However, the Da Vinci IGs are not yet mature enough for Health IT certification adoption on the provider side, and ONC should adopt a phased, modular approach that prioritizes payer adoption and allows for granular, flexible certification criteria.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
-   Adopt a staged approach for ePA certification:
    -   **Stage 1:**
        -   Work with CMS to focus payer adoption and certification of CRD, DTR, and PAS IGs to establish clear payer implementation standards.
        -   Do not set certification criteria for provider-focused Health IT until functional needs mapped to various systems (EHRs, non-certified HIT, SMART on FHIR Apps) are clear.
            -   Make test labs available for individual IG interactions.
        -   Initial focus of Stage 1 should be on CRD and PAS interactions due to DTR's complexity.
    -   **Stage 2:**
        -   Establish Health IT criteria based on matured and evolved interaction distributions across Health IT, clearly documented within each IG.
        -   Extend provider functional requirements for prior authorization engagement using certified Health IT to support the full workflow, ensuring providers are fully supported by a suite of certified Health IT over time.
-   Address X12 translation as a separate, isolatable capability, not required for provider-focused Health IT.
-   Do not adopt a singular ePA criterion that encompasses all capabilities; allow Health IT developers discretion to certify to some or all adopted ePA criteria.
-   Establish more granular interaction building blocks for certification criteria, allowing individual Health IT solutions to select applicable criteria.
-   Support certification criteria requiring FHIR R4 or the latest FHIR core standards.
-   Address data retention durations, signatures, security, and integrity as general system requirements, not unique to prior authorization or interoperability.
    -   Focus relevant certification criteria on non-EHR Health IT to ensure fundamental capabilities are in place.
-   Consider voluntary industry adoption of the Da Vinci process, driven by CMS policy incentives, as an alternative to certification.
-   Simplify rules and express them based on discrete, electronically available data, akin to eCQMs and eDQMs.
-   Develop targeted guidance for specific capabilities:
    -   Consumer apps for authorization status updates.
    -   Translation in/out of X12.
    -   Notification of coverage requirement determination outcome to the initiating system.
    -   Notification of authorization request to the initiating system and other interested systems.
    -   Payer/health plan endpoint directory for respective interactions.
-   Do not introduce an extra transformation step or the concept of a "document" solely for packaging healthcare attachment data.
    -   Permit submission of data sets (individual data, C-CDA or FHIR documents, non-structured documents) as supported by the current PAS IG.
-   Implement certification requirements with clear specifications and IGs before imposing regulation.
-   Ensure widespread, simultaneous implementation across all types of care and payers to prevent frustration.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
-   The three Da Vinci Project IGs are "not yet in a state of maturity that is sufficient for Health IT certification adoption."
-   Requiring certification to an entire prior authorization workflow for provider-focused Health IT is premature due to the absence of clearly defined, granular interaction sets within the Da Vinci guides.
-   Allocating all provider-side interactions to one Health IT solution lacks necessary flexibility.
-   The complexity of managing DTR capabilities on the provider side is substantial, especially across 1500+ health plans and coordinating automated/manual follow-up.
-   A singular ePA criterion embracing all capabilities would be overly broad, not accounting for specialized SMART Apps or intermediaries.
-   The current Da Vinci IGs would yield "too coarse of a certification criterion" for a single Health IT solution to support fully.
-   Requiring review and signature when automating data collection introduces an extra, unnecessary human step and conflicts with the industry's desire for automation.
-   Introducing an "extra transformation step" or the "concept of a 'document'" for packaging healthcare attachments is unnecessary, adds substantial effort, and lacks real value.
-   Adopting a coarse certification approach and starting certification for Health IT before appropriate granularity and maturity are established would impose a "substantial burden" on EHRs.
-   ONC's 2015 Certification Edition approach (not specifying a standard) is not viable as it would lead to non-interoperable variations.
-   Competing priorities may challenge resources needed to advance other customer-requested capabilities not subject to certification.
-   Partial implementation of ePA (only certain types of care/payers covered) would likely cause "more frustration" for providers.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
-   Draws an analogy between the proposed ePA phased approach and how EHRs are currently certified to FHIR US Core APIs while applications connecting to those APIs are not, suggesting a similar progression for ePA.
-   Highlights the similarity of simplifying rules for prior authorization to the progression of eCQMs and eDQMs (electronic clinical quality measures and electronic data quality measures).
-   Estimates that developing electronic prior authorization functionality within certified health IT products would take **1.5 years from design to deployment**.
-   Offers the insight that "most patients want a hands-off approach to the ePA requests," suggesting that patient action requirements within certification are undesirable.
-   Points out that reduced back-and-forth communication with payers due to ePA would allow "reallocating staff due to fewer FTE needs," indicating a potential for administrative cost savings.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
-   "Although these IGs show promise, they are not yet in a state of maturity that is sufficient for Health IT certification adoption."
-   "Therefore, to provide a path to enable the necessary flexibility on the Health IT side and ensure providers have the relevant software support available, we suggest the following staged approach..."
-   "Currently, the manual process creates lag time, so having ePA readily available within the EHR would streamline user workflow. Receiving documentation and requests automatically would be huge for provider burnout."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
*   The Alliance of Community Health Plans (ACHP) appreciates the opportunity to provide feedback on the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) Request for Information on e-Prior Authorization.
    *   ACHP supports the Administration’s efforts to create a more interoperable health care system and reduce burden for patients, providers, and plans.
    *   Prior Authorization is an important component to the delivery of appropriate care.
    *   ACHP’s member companies look forward to working with the Administration to streamline this process.
*   ACHP represents the nation’s top-performing non-profit health plans.
    *   They improve affordability and outcomes in the health care system.
    *   ACHP member companies are provider-aligned health organizations that provide high-quality coverage and care to nearly 24 million Americans across 36 states and D.C.
    *   They are leading the industry in practical, proven reforms around primary care delivery, value-based payment, and data-driven systems improvement.
*   **1. How can the ONC IT Certification Program support the technology needs of healthcare payers in implementing electronic prior authorization?**
    *   ACHP recommends that ONC establish a phased-in certification.
        *   In a landscape where most payers have only recently built and implemented Patient Access APIs, it is unreasonable to assume that payers have full FHIR capabilities.
            *   Many are still actively working internally or with vendor partners to refine the infrastructure and troubleshoot issues.
            *   Patient uptake for the Patient Access API has been low, which limits plans’ ability to identify any potential real-time issues with the API.
        *   ACHP members also note that Clinical Decision Support (CDS) Hooks, Clinical Quality Language (CQL), and SMART on FHIR applications are not widely used within health plan IT infrastructure.
        *   Implementing electronic prior authorization, unlike patient access and provider directory APIs, requires bidirectional exchange with providers.
            *   This introduces challenges, especially for providers with limited access and resources for IT infrastructure investments.
        *   Not all payers have a clearinghouse as an intermediary.
            *   If the module requires data translation from FHIR to X12 and vice versa, the burden of providing the software capable of that translation should not be placed on the payer.
        *   Allowing plans to phase-in adoption of these IT Certifications, starting with the Da Vinci Implementation Guides and then moving to CQL and CDS Hooks, will be necessary for effective implementation.
*   **2. Would the availability of certified Health IT Modules supporting these workflows reduce the burden for healthcare payers of engaging with healthcare providers in prior authorization processes? To what extent would healthcare payers be likely to use these certified Health IT Modules if they were available?**
    *   ACHP requests ONC provide health plan and vendor incentives for the adoption of Health IT Certification Modules.
        *   ACHP recognizes that Certified Health IT Modules are intended to help establish a nationwide, connected, and interoperable health information infrastructure.
        *   ACHP is supportive of the Certified Health IT Modules once they have been thoroughly vetted and proven efficient through pilots.
        *   The incentive for using these Modules currently resides in reimbursement for providers, notably by providing Medicare incentive payments for eligible physicians when meaningful use of an EHR is demonstrated.
        *   ACHP notes that there is not an equivalent incentive for payers.
        *   ACHP recommends that there should be an incentive for health plans to make the significant investment of aligning with the certification internally or selecting a vendor using the Modules.
    *   Payers generally obtain administrative platforms from vendors, so the payer is restricted to what the vendor provides.
        *   For a health plan to adopt the Certified Health IT Modules, its vendor would either need to modify its infrastructure in line with the certification or the health plan would need to recontract with a new vendor, both of which are expensive and burdensome endeavors.
    *   ACHP reiterates that incentives for plans and vendors would provide the resources needed to effectively approach this issue and drive more efficient industry adoption of these Modules.
*   **3. To what extent are health IT developers likely to seek certification for Health IT Modules if they were available?**
    *   ACHP recommends ONC adopt appropriate standards that support prior authorization and are incorporated into the certification criteria of Health IT Modules developed by Health IT developers.
        *   Vendors of payer administrative platforms will be unlikely to make changes to their products without a mechanism to incentivize payers to choose that vendor.
        *   ACHP urges the Administration to consider standards for vendors so Certified Health IT Modules are inherited by the payer administrative platform.
        *   Certification would be a valuable criterion in health plans’ solution evaluation process and enable developers to demonstrate the value of these new standards and systems.
        *   Increased acceptance of this functionality would encourage current vendors to enhance their products to support that functionality and reduce concerns of patients and providers not using these new tools.
*   **4. Do the functional capabilities described above include all necessary functionality for certified Health IT Modules to successfully facilitate electronic prior authorization processes? Should any of these functional capabilities not be included in certified Health IT Modules (please cite the reason they should be excluded) or should ONC focus on a more limited set of functional capabilities for certified Health IT Modules than those described above?**
    *   ACHP agrees with ONC’s outlined functional capabilities for Certified Health IT Modules to successfully facilitate e-prior authorizations.
    *   ACHP reiterates our recommendation for a phased-in approach that would allow for ONC to define an initial reasonable set of use cases for which health plans should expect to achieve with these functional capabilities.
        *   The scope of information associated with prior authorization requirements can vary substantially, even to the extent of using additional third-party support for specialty service determinations.
        *   To account for such cases, ACHP recommends that electronic prior authorization should not be used for infrequent, complex services.
        *   ONC should identify an industry-wide “80/20 rule” enabling the industry to achieve appropriate value without solving complex, low-occurrence use cases.
*   **5. If ONC were to propose to include these functional capabilities as part of the Certification Program, how should a new certification criterion (or multiple certification criteria) be structured, including technical requirements, attributed standards, and implementation specifications?**
    *   ACHP supports ONC including the functional capabilities as part of the Certification Programs and recommends Coverage Requirement Discovery (CRD)/Documentation Templates and Rules (DTR)/Prior Authorization Support (PAS) processes be separated into three workflows.
        *   While ACHP member organizations made tremendous steps to implement the CMS Interoperability and Patient Access API rule, assumptions that in doing so the groundwork has been laid for additional FHIR functionality is incorrect.
        *   Health plans will need time and resources to work towards this new capability.
        *   Approaching the CRD/DTR/PAS processes in phases will build in the necessary time plans need while allowing for incremental value in each phase.
        *   As each phase builds on previous workflows, this approach allows for a hardening of the solution before extending too far into the next phase.
        *   This approach will make inter-system testing easier and increase the odds of success and adoption.
    *   Currently, PAS is still useful without CRD and DTR.
        *   However, CRD and DTR requires infrastructure and FHIR capabilities that are not widely adopted by payers.
            *   For CRD, CDS hooks are currently too complex to implement for payers immediately.
            *   For DTR, CQL is not easily understood by any payers or providers.
        *   ACHP recommends ONC begin with a PAS phase and move CRD and DTR to a later phase of certification to allow payers to implement the necessary infrastructure.
*   **6. Should ONC adopt a certification criterion for prior authorization that accounts for the full, HIPAA compliant workflow for prior authorization transactions including translation from FHIR to the X12 standard? Or should ONC adopt certification criteria that include only the workflows up to the point of translation?**
    *   ACHP supports fully transitioning to FHIR-to-FHIR transactions for its ability to facilitate real-time determination.
        *   Successful electronic Prior Authorization demands real-time feedback, which is a continued struggle for other, non-FHIR approaches.
    *   ACHP is supportive of a FHIR-X12 “translator” as an appropriate temporary bridge by issuing discretionary enforcement action that would allow both standards to coexist.
        *   However, ACHP notes that this bridge risks becoming a long-term barrier to success and ultimately does little to reduce health plan processing burden.
        *   The FHIR-FHIR transactions rely on providers transitioning to FHIR when they are accustomed to HIPAA X12 transactions.
        *   Without an incentive to drive this transition for providers, health plans will be burdened with maintaining the X12 to FHIR translators.
    *   Ultimately, ACHP agrees with HITAC and ONC that the best solution is to transition to FHIR-FHIR transactions once payers and providers have the resources and infrastructure to do so.
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: The Alliance of Community Health Plans supports e-prior authorization and interoperability but urges ONC to implement a phased IT certification approach with incentives for health plans and vendors, recognizing current infrastructure limitations and the need for a practical transition to FHIR-to-FHIR transactions.
structuredSections.commenterProfile:
- **Name/Organization:** Alliance of Community Health Plans (ACHP)
- **Type:** Trade Association
- **Role/Expertise:** Represents the nation’s top-performing non-profit health plans; member companies are provider-aligned health organizations that lead in primary care delivery, value-based payment, and data-driven systems improvement.
- **Geographic Scope:** National, with member companies serving nearly 24 million Americans across 36 states and D.C.
- **Stake in Issue:** Their member health plans will be directly responsible for implementing and utilizing electronic prior authorization technologies and will be significantly impacted by ONC's IT Certification Program requirements, standards, and incentives.
structuredSections.corePosition: We support the Administration’s efforts to create a more interoperable health care system and reduce burden for patients, providers, and plans through electronic prior authorization. However, successful implementation requires a practical, phased approach to IT certification, strong incentives for health plans and vendors, and a clear path for transitioning to FHIR-to-FHIR transactions that accounts for current infrastructure realities.
structuredSections.keyRecommendations:
- Establish a phased-in IT Certification Program for e-prior authorization, starting with Da Vinci Implementation Guides before moving to CQL and CDS Hooks.
- Provide explicit financial or other incentives for health plans and vendors to adopt Certified Health IT Modules.
- Adopt appropriate standards for vendors to ensure Certified Health IT Modules are inherited by payer administrative platforms.
- Define an initial reasonable set of use cases for e-prior authorization and adopt an industry-wide "80/20 rule," focusing on high-occurrence cases and excluding infrequent, complex services.
- Structure certification criteria for Coverage Requirement Discovery (CRD), Documentation Templates and Rules (DTR), and Prior Authorization Support (PAS) processes into three separate, phased workflows, prioritizing PAS first.
- Support a temporary FHIR-X12 “translator” bridge with discretionary enforcement action to allow coexistence of standards, while ultimately aiming for a full transition to FHIR-to-FHIR transactions.
- Implement incentives for providers to transition to FHIR to avoid burdening health plans with maintaining X12 to FHIR translators long-term.
structuredSections.mainConcerns:
- Most payers have only recently built and implemented Patient Access APIs, making the assumption of full FHIR capabilities unreasonable at present.
- Low patient uptake for Patient Access APIs limits health plans' ability to identify real-time issues with their APIs.
- Clinical Decision Support (CDS) Hooks, Clinical Quality Language (CQL), and SMART on FHIR applications are not widely used within health plan IT infrastructure.
- Electronic prior authorization requires bidirectional exchange with providers, which poses challenges for providers with limited IT access and resources.
- Placing the burden of FHIR to X12 translation software on payers without a clearinghouse intermediary is problematic.
- There is no equivalent incentive for health plans to adopt Certified Health IT Modules, unlike the Medicare incentive payments for providers demonstrating meaningful use of EHRs.
- Health plans are restricted by their administrative platform vendors; adopting new Certified Health IT Modules would require expensive and burdensome vendor modifications or recontracting.
- Vendors are unlikely to modify their products without a mechanism to incentivize payers to choose vendors with certified modules.
- The varying scope and complexity of prior authorization requirements, including reliance on third-party support for specialty services, makes a one-size-fits-all e-prior authorization solution difficult.
- CRD and DTR require infrastructure and FHIR capabilities (like CDS hooks and CQL) that are not currently widely adopted or easily understood by payers or providers, making immediate implementation difficult.
- A FHIR-X12 "translator" bridge, while temporary, risks becoming a long-term barrier and does not significantly reduce health plan processing burden without provider incentives to transition to FHIR.
structuredSections.notableExperiences:
- Payers have only recently implemented Patient Access APIs, and current FHIR capabilities are still being refined, indicating a need for a realistic timeline for additional FHIR functionality.
- Low patient engagement with existing Patient Access APIs highlights a potential challenge in vetting and troubleshooting new API functionalities in real-time.
- The observation that key technologies like CDS Hooks, CQL, and SMART on FHIR are not yet widely adopted by health plans suggests significant infrastructure gaps for immediate advanced e-prior authorization implementation.
- The recommendation for an "80/20 rule" to focus e-prior authorization efforts on common, high-volume use cases reflects a practical, value-driven approach to implementation, avoiding complex, low-occurrence services.
- The insight that Prior Authorization Support (PAS) can provide value independently of Coverage Requirement Discovery (CRD) and Documentation Templates and Rules (DTR), which are more technologically complex for payers to implement immediately, offers a strategic phasing suggestion.
- The concern that a FHIR-X12 "translator" bridge could become a permanent fixture and increase health plan burden if providers lack incentives to transition to FHIR-based transactions reveals a nuanced understanding of interoperability challenges.
structuredSections.keyQuotations:
- "In a landscape where most payers have only recently built and implemented Patient Access APIs, it is unreasonable to assume that payers have full FHIR capabilities."
- "ACHP recommends that electronic prior authorization should not be used for infrequent, complex services and ONC should identify an industry-wide '80/20 rule' enabling the industry to achieve appropriate value without solving complex, low-occurrence use cases."
- "ACHP notes that this bridge risks becoming a long-term barrier to success and ultimately does little to reduce health plan processing burden."

structuredSections.detailedContent:
- As an oncology registered nurse practicing in Southern California, I support establishing standards-based APIs to streamline the process of submitting prior authorization requests and reduce burden on both providers and payers.
- While I am not qualified to comment on specific functional capabilities or implementation specifications for electronic prior authorizations, I want to comment on the impact this effort would have on oncology patients.
- Prior authorization (PA) requirements in oncology practices have long been a deterrent to efficient and high-quality delivery of health care.
- Your request for information asks for input about electronic prior authorization processes for items and services other than medications.
  - While it is understandable that PAs may be required for novel oncology medications, Lin et al. (2018) found that payers also are requiring approval for supportive care or antineoplastic medicines that have been on the market for a long time and/or the use of which falls squarely within ASCO and National Comprehensive Cancer Network treatment guidelines.
  - Supportive treatments such as imaging (CT, MRI and PET scans) and out-patient procedures (paracenteses and thoracenteses) are often delayed due to the requirement of PAs.
  - In my experience, I have often seen that delays of these supportive treatments leads to health complications, such as febrile neutropenia, that require hospital admission to manage.
  - This leads to greater costs for patients, payors, and hospitals, not to mention the significant emotional burden on patients and their families.
- Another area in oncology practice that often gets delayed due to PAs is radiation treatment.
  - Bingham et al. (2022) reported that annual radiation treatment related PA departmental costs to an academic practice were estimated to be $491,989 USD.
  - In addition to the increased cost to the practice, current PA processes for radiation delay patient treatment significantly.
  - In my experience, some of my patients have had to wait an average of 2 months for radiation treatment to be approved.
  - This increases the cost and time associated with symptom management for the patient due to their cancer.
- Establishing a standardized process to complete PAs for not only medications and chemotherapy, but also for supportive care and other treatments is a step in the right direction to improve oncology care for patients.
  - It would also help reduce the administrative burden on providers and support staff by potentially automating the process and leaving providers with more time to dedicate to actual patient care.
  - There have been many times in my practice that the oncologist I work with has had to take time during a busy clinic to complete a peer-to-peer review on the phone.
  - This is inefficient and not beneficial to either providers or patients.
  - In terms of efficiency, I’d also like to suggest that if peer-to-peers are needed, there be an option to have an Advanced Practice Provider such as a Nurse Practitioner or Physician’s Assistant able to complete the peer-to-peer on the physician’s behalf, which is currently not allowed.
  - Furthermore, I believe that adding a component to the process in which the patient can also track their PA progress via a website would enhance the patient experience and promote adherence to the treatment.
- I am excited about the prospect of improving patient care via standardizing the process of prior authorizations.
- While I know this endeavor will likely be complicated and may not be seamless, it is an important step to making healthcare more efficient and useful for our patients.
- References:
  - Lin, N. U., Bichkoff, H., & Hassett, M. J. (2018). Increasing Burden of Prior Authorizations in the Delivery of Oncology Care in the United States. Journal of Oncology Practice, 14(9), 525–528. https://doi.org/10.1200/jop.18.00428
  - Bingham, B., Chennupati, S., & Osmundson, E. C. (2022). Estimating the Practice-Level and National Cost Burden of Treatment-Related Prior Authorization for Academic Radiation Oncology Practices. JCO Oncology Practice. https://doi.org/10.1200/op.21.00644
structuredSections.oneLineSummary: An oncology registered nurse from Southern California supports standards-based APIs for electronic prior authorizations to reduce patient delays and provider burden, citing firsthand experiences of health complications, increased costs, and treatment delays caused by current manual processes in cancer care.

This leads to greater costs for patients, payors, and hospitals, not to mention the significant emotional burden on patients and their families."
